name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads featured waxing services, packages, and promotions, and exposes quick-book options and navigation shortcuts to key waxing-related pages.

core_features: Load and display featured waxing services, packages, and promotions using getHomeHighlights.
Load and display quick-book waxing options with next available time slots using getQuickBookOptions.
Provide navigation tiles to Waxing Services, Men’s Waxing, Build Your Own Session, Packages, Shop (Aftercare pre-filter), Gift Cards, and Offers.
Provide service-level quick-book entry points that navigate directly to the relevant service detail page for booking.
Expose a link to view all offers, leveraging the promotions summary from getHomeHighlights.
user_workflows:
Browse featured waxing options and navigate to details:
1) User lands on Home.
2) Page initializes data loader component, which calls getHomeHighlights.
3) When data is returned, featured services, packages, and promotions sections render.
4) User clicks a featured service card.
5) Browser navigates to service_detail.html with the URL parameter serviceId set to the clicked Service.id.
6) Alternatively, user clicks a featured package navigation tile, which navigates to packages.html without parameters.

Use quick-book to schedule the next available waxing slot:
1) User lands on Home.
2) Page calls getQuickBookOptions.
3) Quick-book list renders each option with its Service and next_available_start_datetime.
4) User clicks a quick-book service card or its primary action (e.g., "Book next available").
5) Browser navigates to service_detail.html with the URL parameter serviceId set to the selected Service.id.
6) The target page can then use serviceId (and optionally the datetime and technician context if it supports them) to pre-configure booking.

Navigate quickly to Men’s Waxing and other key sections:
1) User lands on Home.
2) Navigation tiles render statically (no API needed) but are positioned alongside dynamic highlight content.
3) User clicks the Men’s Waxing tile.
4) Browser navigates to waxing_services.html with URL parameter categoryId=men.
5) User clicks the Shop Aftercare tile.
6) Browser navigates to shop.html with URL parameter categoryId=aftercare.
7) User clicks the Gift Cards tile or Offers tile.
8) Browser navigates to gift_cards.html or offers.html respectively (no parameters).

Discover current offers from homepage promotions:
1) User lands on Home.
2) Page calls getHomeHighlights and receives featured_promotions.
3) Promotions summary component renders a concise list of current promotions (derived entirely from the Promotion objects returned).
4) User clicks the "View All Offers" link in the promotions section.
5) Browser navigates to offers.html (no parameters), where full offer details can be explored.

Return to Home from other pages:
1) User is on any other page.
2) User clicks the salon logo or the Home item in the global navigation header.
3) Browser navigates to index.html (Home) without additional parameters.
4) Home page re-calls getHomeHighlights and getQuickBookOptions to refresh all dynamic data.
interactions: On page load: A home data loader component automatically invokes getHomeHighlights and getQuickBookOptions. While either request is in-flight, loading states are shown in the dependent components. If either request fails, an error state is displayed for that section only, without affecting other sections.
Click featured service card: When a user clicks any card in the featured services list, the click handler reads the bound Service.id and navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={Service.id}.

Click quick-book service: When a user clicks a quick-book card or button, the click handler reads the bound Service.id from the quick-book option and navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={Service.id}. Any additional contextual data from getQuickBookOptions (like next_available_start_datetime or technician.id) remains local state and is not encoded in the Home URL.

Click Men’s Waxing tile: The Men’s Waxing navigation tile has a fixed click handler that navigates to waxing_services.html?categoryId=men, using a URL parameter to indicate the men’s category filter on the destination page.
Click Waxing Services tile: The general Waxing Services navigation tile navigates to waxing_services.html without any URL parameters, allowing the destination page to show its default view.
Click Build Your Own Session tile: The tile’s click handler navigates to build_session.html with no URL parameters, where the custom session builder flow begins.
Click Packages tile: The tile’s click handler navigates to packages.html with no URL parameters, letting the packages page handle its own filtering or sorting.
Click Shop Aftercare tile: The tile’s click handler navigates to shop.html?categoryId=aftercare, passing the category filter through the URL.
Click Gift Cards tile: The tile’s click handler navigates to gift_cards.html without additional parameters.
Click Offers tile or View All Offers link: Both interactions navigate to offers.html without parameters; any filtering or highlighting of specific promotions is handled by that page.
Header navigation Home click: Clicking the Home link in the header navigation or the site logo navigates to index.html, re-triggering the Home data loading cycle.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Home page does not consume any URL parameters to determine its own state. Its runtime state is derived solely from real-time responses of getHomeHighlights and getQuickBookOptions. On initial load (or any full reload of index.html), the page clears prior in-memory state and re-requests data from both interfaces.

Dynamic sections each maintain independent loading and error states: featured services, featured packages, promotions, and quick-book options. A failure in one interface (e.g., getQuickBookOptions) does not block or alter the rendering of data from the other interface (e.g., getHomeHighlights). The page uses internal state flags to determine whether to render loading placeholders, error messages, or the data-driven content for each section.

URL parameters are used only for outgoing navigation to other pages:
- Service detail navigation: service_detail.html?serviceId={Service.id} is used when users click a featured service or a quick-book service.
- Men’s Waxing navigation: waxing_services.html?categoryId=men is used when users click the Men’s Waxing tile.
- Shop Aftercare navigation: shop.html?categoryId=aftercare is used when users click the Shop Aftercare tile.

Because Home does not define or read its own query parameters, bookmarking or sharing index.html always yields the default Home state, with fresh data loaded from the APIs. Any filtering, selection, or booking configuration is handled by the destination pages via their own URL parameters.

components.functionality: Central controller that triggers getHomeHighlights and getQuickBookOptions on page load, manages their loading/error states, and provides the resulting data to child components via a shared state or props mechanism.

Displays a selectable list of featured waxing services returned from getHomeHighlights.featured_services. Each service card exposes basic information and a click action to view and book that specific service on its detail page.

Displays a list of featured waxing packages returned from getHomeHighlights.featured_packages. Each package item shows its name and key summary information and supports a click action that navigates to the Packages page for full details and booking options.

Shows a concise list of current featured promotions using getHomeHighlights.featured_promotions. It renders each Promotion with its promotional details and exposes a separate control to navigate to the Offers page to see all offers.

Renders a list of quick-book options from getQuickBookOptions. Each list item binds to a Service, its next_available_start_datetime, and optional Technician, and provides a primary action to start booking the next available slot for that service via navigation to the service detail page.

Provides clickable tiles for core navigation paths: general Waxing Services, Men’s Waxing (sending a categoryId parameter), Build Your Own Session, Packages, Shop pre-filtered for aftercare products (sending a categoryId parameter), Gift Cards, and Offers.

Provides site-wide header navigation with a Home link and clickable salon logo that both navigate to the Home page, plus links to other major sections (implementation of those sections is external to this page).

Controls the conditional rendering of loading indicators and error messages for each dynamic section (featured services, packages, promotions, quick-book), based on the success or failure of getHomeHighlights and getQuickBookOptions.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top‑to‑bottom flow with a full‑width, visually light header at the very top, followed by a tall hero band of whitespace and the home page title (e.g., Waxing / Home) centered in a fixed‑width content column. Below the hero, all functional components are stacked in a single central column: featured services first, then featured packages, then promotions, followed by quick‑book options, and finally primary navigation tiles. Within each list or navigation grid, content is laid out in a three‑column pattern where each row holds up to three items spaced evenly across the central column, echoing the service menu grid from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top‑to‑bottom flow with a full‑width, visually light header at the very top, followed by a tall hero band of whitespace and the home page title (e.g., Waxing / Home) centered in a fixed‑width content column. Below the hero, all functional components are stacked in a single central column: featured services first, then featured packages, then promotions, followed by quick‑book options, and finally primary navigation tiles. Within each list or navigation grid, content is laid out in a three‑column pattern where each row holds up to three items spaced evenly across the central column, echoing the service menu grid from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The layout is governed by a centered, fixed‑width content column (approximately 60–70% of the viewport width, or a max width around 1040–1120px) with generous lateral margins on larger screens. The header navigation spans the full viewport width but its content is horizontally aligned with the central column. A large vertical gap (roughly 80–96px) separates the header from the hero title, and similarly generous gaps (64–80px) separate each main section below. Within each section, subsection headings are given breathing room above and below, and individual item rows have spacious vertical spacing (24–32px) between them. The home‑data‑loader appears subtly aligned to the same central column, and section loading/error states occupy the same spatial footprint as their content, preserving vertical rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains intentionally low. Each section displays only a handful of items per view, arranged in a three‑column grid that uses large horizontal gutters so no column feels crowded. Line height and row spacing are generous, allowing service names, prices, and labels to breathe. Sections do not visually bleed into one another; instead, clear whitespace bands separate them. There are no dense tables, no compressed carousels, and no multiple stacked sidebars; everything lives in one calm, vertical narrative within the central column.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first registers the header navigation at the top due to its position and crisp typography, then quickly drops into the open whitespace that leads to the centered home title and its subtle divider. From there, the gaze flows down into the featured services section, scanning each service row from left to right across up to three service‑price pairs. This zig‑zag scanning pattern continues down the page through featured packages and promotions. The quick‑book options section interrupts the reading pattern slightly by presenting more obviously interactive elements within the same three‑column structure, before the eye finally settles on the primary navigation tiles near the bottom, which act as jump‑off points to deeper pages. If the data is loading or an error arises, the loader and state indicators appear in line with these sections, but remain visually understated so the primary flow remains clean.

layout.overall_description: The home page presents as a calm, editorial single column centered within a wide field of white space. A slim, full‑width header navigation bar sits pinned to the very top of the viewport, its logo and links aligned to an invisible central column. Beneath it, a tall expanse of white space leads the eye downward to a large, softly colored page title for the waxing home, centered both horizontally and within the content column, with a fine horizontal rule beneath that subtly marks the transition from hero to content. Below this, the main content unfolds as a vertical sequence of clearly separated sections, each introduced by a small, refined heading and then a three‑column grid of items that mimics a luxury service menu: featured waxing services first, then curated packages, current promotions, an accessible band of quick‑book options, and finally a grid of primary navigation tiles that link into deeper waxing categories. Each section respects generous top and bottom margins, and within each grid, items are grouped in rows of up to three, with large gutters and ample vertical spacing, so each service or option sits in its own pocket of air. Loading indicators and error states appear lightly and centrally within this same column when needed, without collapsing the white space. The overall impression is of a high‑end, boutique waxing menu translated into a website: airy, centered, and effortlessly scannable from top to bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header navigation forms a slim bar anchored to the very top of the page, spanning 100% of the viewport width. Its interior content (logo/brand on the left, minimal navigation links on the right or center) is constrained to the same fixed‑width central column that governs the rest of the page (roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, centered). There is minimal vertical height to the bar itself, just enough for elegant typography and comfortable vertical padding (~16–24px). Beneath the header, a large block of empty vertical space (about 80–96px) separates it from the main hero area, reinforcing the feeling that the nav floats above the content instead of crowding it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document; full‑width bar with content centered in line with the main content column; does not scroll with sticky behavior but remains visible at the top until the user scrolls past it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home data loader appears as an understated, central indicator that overlays or occupies space just below the header and above the hero title when the page first loads. Visually, it can take the form of a very thin blush‑colored progress bar that stretches horizontally across the fixed‑width content column (spanning roughly 60–70% of the viewport, centered) or a small, refined inline loader centered within that same column. It sits comfortably within the tall whitespace band at the top of the page, aligned with the page title position, without touching the edges of the viewport. Once data is loaded, the loader disappears entirely, returning that space to pure whitespace so the title and sections can dominate.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally; positioned relative to the main content column just below the header, centered horizontally, and does not remain sticky or fixed once content is available.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured services list is the first substantial content block after the hero title. It sits within the central content column, centered on the page, starting roughly 64–80px beneath the hero title and divider. A small, elegant section heading such as Featured Services is center‑aligned at the top of this block, occupying the full width of the column with generous top and bottom padding (about 24px above and 24px below). Beneath this heading, the services are arranged in a multi‑row, three‑column grid reminiscent of the original waxing menu: each row spans the entire width of the content column and holds up to three service‑price pairs, evenly distributed from left to right. Each pair sits in its own informal column, with the service name aligned left within the pair and the price aligned close to it on the right, separated by a small horizontal gap. The three pairs in a row are separated by wide horizontal gutters (approximately 48–64px) so the grid feels airy. Vertically, rows are spaced with about 24–32px of empty space between them, maintaining the relaxed rhythm of the mockup. The entire section occupies roughly the middle third of the initial scroll area, depending on the number of services.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the main vertical flow; adapts responsively so that on narrower screens the three columns stack to two or one while preserving generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured packages list sits directly beneath the featured services section, separated by a clear band of white space of about 64–80px to distinguish it as a new content block. It is centered within the same fixed‑width content column and follows a nearly identical structure: a concise, refined section heading (e.g., Packages) centered at the top, then a three‑column grid of package items below. Each row spans the content column width and holds up to three package entries. Within each column, the package name is given prominence, with any brief descriptor beneath in smaller text, and the price aligned close to the right edge of that column group, echoing the service‑price visual pattern. Generous horizontal gutters (~48–64px) separate the three columns, and vertical gaps (~24–32px) separate each row of packages, maintaining a low density, boutique feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section stacked in the main column; responsive behavior mirrors the featured services list, collapsing columns gracefully on smaller screens while maintaining white space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotions summary list is positioned below the featured packages, again with a generous vertical gap of approximately 64–80px separating the sections. It remains within the same centered content column. A subtle section heading such as Current Promotions appears at the top, center‑aligned with ample breathing room above and below. Under the heading, promotions are arranged in a lighter three‑column grid: each promotion tile occupies one informal column per row, with up to three promotions per row. Instead of dense text, each item might feature a short title and a one‑line description or date range, laid out with plenty of padding within each column. The horizontal gutters remain wide (~48–64px) to keep promotions feeling sparse and special, and vertical spacing between rows is on the order of 24–32px. Overall, the promotions block is slightly shorter and visually lighter than the services and packages sections, supporting but not overpowering them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content section in the vertical flow; responds to screen size by reducing columns while preserving generous spacing and central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick book options list is placed immediately after the promotions section, separated by another wide band of whitespace (around 64–80px), and is still aligned within the central content column. This section is designed to feel more actionable while preserving the editorial calm: a concise heading, such as Quick Book, appears centered at the top. Beneath it, a three‑column layout presents quick‑book options as elevated but minimal interactive elements, such as wide pill‑style buttons or lightly outlined tiles. Each row holds up to three quick‑book options spaced evenly across the width, with large horizontal gutters (~48–64px) between them. Each option tile spans the width of its column and includes a short label (e.g., Brows, Full Leg, Popular Package) centered within, with generous internal padding (12–16px vertically, 24–32px horizontally) to maintain the airy feel. Vertical spacing between rows of quick‑book options is slightly larger (around 32px) to clearly separate tappable areas. This section occupies a noticeable band across approximately the lower middle of the page, inviting action without breaking the overall minimalism.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, grid-based section; interactive tiles remain within the central column and maintain their three‑column arrangement as long as screen width allows, collapsing to fewer columns on smaller devices while keeping vertical spacing generous.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation tiles form the last major content block near the bottom of the page, appearing about 64–96px below the quick book section. They are centered within the same fixed‑width column and serve as a visual footer‑like navigation hub for key waxing pages (e.g., All Services, Membership, About Waxing). A small section label or could be omitted for maximal minimalism; if present, it sits centered above the tiles with modest spacing. The navigation tiles themselves follow the familiar three‑column grid: each row holds up to three tiles, evenly distributed from left to right with wide gutters (~48–64px). Tiles are simple, flat rectangles with subtle borders or background tints, each containing a short, uppercase or title‑case destination label centered vertically and horizontally, and possibly a small arrow or subtle icon aligned to the right. Internal padding is generous (16–24px vertically) so each tile feels like a refined editorial card rather than a dense button. Vertical spacing between tile rows matches other sections (~24–32px). This grid extends toward the lower third of the scroll, leading gently toward the page’s end while still feeling cohesive with the central column layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation grid at the end of the main content; grid is responsive, reducing columns on narrow screens while maintaining central alignment and generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Section loading and error states appear inline within the same central content column, occupying the vertical space where each list or grid would normally render. For example, if featured services are loading, a simple centered loading message or skeleton row set appears in the middle of that section’s allotted space, maintaining the same top and bottom margins as the fully loaded section. Error states follow the same pattern: a short, elegantly styled message (e.g., Could not load promotions) is center‑aligned horizontally, with one or two lines of text and an optional understated retry link or button directly beneath, all contained within the width of the central column. Surrounding whitespace remains generous, so even in error or loading modes the page feels calm and uncluttered. No full‑screen overlays are used; instead, these states sit naturally within the vertical flow, preserving the overall rhythm of sections and the editorial layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally displayed inline elements that replace or sit inside the space reserved for each section’s content; they do not change the global layout or column width, and they scroll with the rest of the page.

name: Waxing Services
title: Waxing Services
description: Browse all waxing services, apply filters and sorting, view service details, and quickly compose a simple multi-area waxing session before proceeding to booking or the full session configurator.

core_features: Load dynamic filter and sort options for waxing services using getServiceFilterOptions and render them as interactive controls (main area groups, specific areas, duration ranges, price ranges, sort options).

Read URL parameters (categoryId, mainAreaGroup, areaKey, genderSegment, minDuration, maxDuration, priceRange, sort, page) to initialize the filter and sort state, then fetch the matching services list via listWaxingServices.
Display a paginated list of waxing services returned by listWaxingServices, including key attributes (name, main_area_group, area_key, gender_segment, duration_minutes, base_price).
Allow users to change filters and sorting, update the URL parameters accordingly, and reload the services list by calling listWaxingServices with the new criteria.
Provide per-service interactions: open service_detail.html with the selected serviceId, or add the service to a pending custom session using addServiceToCurrentSession.
Retrieve the current custom session state via getCurrentSessionState and show a concise summary of selected services, total duration, and total price.

Allow users to start from this page to build a simple multi-area session (e.g., combine multiple body areas) and then navigate to booking.html with appointmentType=custom_session&origin=waxing_services once a session exists.

Offer a clear, functional entry point to the build_session.html page (Build Your Own Waxing Session), optionally forwarding relevant filter context (e.g., mainAreaGroup, areaKey, genderSegment) via URL parameters.
Support pagination of services by reading and setting the page URL parameter in combination with listWaxingServices pagination parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Browse and filter waxing services
1. User navigates to waxing_services.html (optionally with URL parameters from another page).
2. On load, the page calls getServiceFilterOptions to retrieve filter and sort options.
3. The page reads current URL parameters (categoryId, mainAreaGroup, areaKey, genderSegment, minDuration, maxDuration, priceRange, sort, page) and derives an initial filter state.
4. Using this state, the page calls listWaxingServices with the appropriate filters, sort key, and pagination object.
5. The services list component renders the returned services and pagination controls.
6. The user adjusts filters (e.g., selects a main area group, duration range, or price range).
7. Each change updates the corresponding URL parameters and triggers a new listWaxingServices call.
8. The user optionally changes the sort order; the sort URL parameter is updated and listWaxingServices is called again.
9. The user navigates between pages using pagination controls, which update the page parameter in the URL and reload the list via listWaxingServices.

Workflow 2: View service details and book a single service
1. After locating a service in the list, the user clicks on the service name or a specific details/booking action.
2. The click handler reads the service.id for the selected item.
3. The browser navigates to service_detail.html with serviceId set to the selected service.id in the URL (e.g., service_detail.html?serviceId={id}).
4. Further booking steps occur on the service_detail.html page and are outside the scope of waxing_services.html.

Workflow 3: Build a simple multi-area session directly from the services list
1. On page load, the page calls getCurrentSessionState to determine if there is an existing session and to populate the session summary panel if present.
2. The user browses filtered services and, for each desired service, clicks an "Add to Session" type control.
3. The click handler calls addServiceToCurrentSession with the selected service's id.
4. When addServiceToCurrentSession returns, the page updates the session summary panel using the returned session and session_services data.
5. The user can continue adding services; each add updates the current session state in the UI via the same flow.
6. Once satisfied, the user clicks a "Continue to Booking" action in the session summary panel.
7. The browser navigates to booking.html with the URL parameters appointmentType=custom_session and origin=waxing_services (e.g., booking.html?appointmentType=custom_session&origin=waxing_services).
8. The booking page uses the existing session (linked on the backend) to proceed with scheduling; this behavior is handled outside this page.

Workflow 4: Switch to the full Build Your Own Waxing Session configurator
1. The user either has not started a session yet or wants more advanced configuration than the simple add-from-list flow.
2. The user clicks a functional link or button labeled for building a custom waxing session.
3. The click handler optionally reads current context (e.g., mainAreaGroup, areaKey, genderSegment) from the URL.
4. The browser navigates to build_session.html, optionally including those context parameters (e.g., build_session.html?mainAreaGroup={currentMainAreaGroup}&areaKey={currentAreaKey}).
5. The detailed session building process occurs on build_session.html and is outside the scope of this page.

Workflow 5: Access page from Men’s Waxing or Offers with pre-applied filters
1. The user clicks a navigation link (e.g., "Men's Waxing" in the header or a specific offer) that points to waxing_services.html with certain URL parameters such as categoryId, genderSegment, mainAreaGroup, areaKey, minDuration, maxDuration, priceRange, or sort.
2. On load, the URL-state initializer component reads these parameters and sets the initial filter and sort state accordingly.
3. If categoryId corresponds to a recognized segment (for example, men) and no explicit mainAreaGroup or genderSegment is set, the initializer derives reasonable defaults (e.g., main_area_group or gender_segment filters) without hardcoding display labels.
4. listWaxingServices is called with filters consistent with those URL parameters.
5. The user sees a service list already focused on the relevant category (e.g., men’s waxing or services matching an offer) and can further refine or broaden the filters as desired.
interactions: On page load, the controller component calls getServiceFilterOptions and getCurrentSessionState, then reads URL parameters to build an initial filters/sort/pagination state and calls listWaxingServices with that state.

When the user selects or changes a main area group filter, the filter-bar component updates the mainAreaGroup URL parameter, resets the page parameter to the first page, and triggers a new listWaxingServices call using the updated filters.

When the user selects or changes a specific area (areaKey), the filter-bar updates the areaKey URL parameter, optionally constrains mainAreaGroup to the associated group, resets pagination, and triggers listWaxingServices.
When the user selects or changes a gender segment filter (if displayed), the filter-bar updates the genderSegment URL parameter, resets pagination, and triggers listWaxingServices.

When the user selects a duration range option, the filter-bar maps the selected option to minDuration and/or maxDuration URL parameters, updates them, resets pagination, and triggers listWaxingServices.

When the user selects a price range option, the filter-bar sets or updates the priceRange URL parameter according to the application’s mapping to getServiceFilterOptions.price_ranges, resets pagination, and triggers listWaxingServices.
When the user changes the sort option, the sort-control component updates the sort URL parameter and calls listWaxingServices with the same filters but updated sort.
When the user clicks a pagination control (next, previous, specific page), the pagination component updates the page URL parameter and calls listWaxingServices with the updated page value.
When the user clicks a service name or a details/booking action in the service list, the services-list component reads the associated service.id and navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={id}.

When the user clicks an "Add to Session" action for a service, the services-list component calls addServiceToCurrentSession with the selected service.id, then updates the session summary panel with the returned session and session_services data.

When the user opens or refreshes the page with an existing building session, the session-summary-panel component uses data from getCurrentSessionState to show the current collection of services, total duration, and price.
When the user clicks "Continue to Booking" within the session summary panel, the browser navigates to booking.html with appointmentType=custom_session and origin=waxing_services as URL parameters.

When the user clicks the "Build Your Own Waxing Session" entry point, the build-session-entry component navigates to build_session.html, optionally including mainAreaGroup, areaKey, and genderSegment URL parameters derived from the current state.

When the user clicks a "Clear all filters" action (if provided), the filter-bar removes related filter URL parameters (mainAreaGroup, areaKey, genderSegment, minDuration, maxDuration, priceRange), resets page to the first page, and calls listWaxingServices with only default filters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for filters, sorting, and pagination. On initial load and whenever the browser’s location changes, a controller component reads categoryId, mainAreaGroup, areaKey, genderSegment, minDuration, maxDuration, priceRange, sort, and page from the URL. These values are mapped to a filters object for listWaxingServices. Typically, mainAreaGroup maps to Service.main_area_group, areaKey to Service.area_key, genderSegment to Service.gender_segment, minDuration/maxDuration to numeric constraints on Service.duration_minutes, and priceRange to a chosen range mapped to getServiceFilterOptions.price_ranges. The sort parameter is mapped to one of the sort_options returned by getServiceFilterOptions, and page is mapped to the pagination.index or similar parameter for listWaxingServices. If any URL parameters are missing, the page derives defaults strictly from the data returned by getServiceFilterOptions (for example, using the first sort option as a default) without hardcoding specific labels or values. If categoryId is present and corresponds to a known segment or grouping, it is used to set or adjust initial filter values (such as defaulting to a certain main_area_group or gender_segment) when more explicit parameters like mainAreaGroup or genderSegment are not already provided, while still keeping the actual filter values consistent with the Service entity fields. User interactions always update the URL parameters first (using navigation or history APIs), then re-derive the filter/sort/pagination state from the URL, and then call listWaxingServices again, ensuring that the page state is fully reproducible by reloading or sharing the URL. Session-related state (the set of services in the current custom session, plus total duration and price) is not stored in the URL; instead it is always retrieved via getCurrentSessionState and updated via addServiceToCurrentSession, with any downstream navigation to booking.html using only simple URL flags (appointmentType and origin) to indicate that an existing session should be used.

components.functionality: Initializes and synchronizes page state with URL parameters, orchestrates data loading by calling getServiceFilterOptions, getCurrentSessionState, and listWaxingServices based on the current URL, and reacts to URL changes triggered by user interactions.

Displays dynamic filter controls for main area group, specific area, gender segment (if applicable), duration range, and price range using data from getServiceFilterOptions; reads initial values from URL parameters, and when filters are changed, updates URL parameters and triggers a reload of services via the controller.

Renders sort options obtained from getServiceFilterOptions.sort_options, selects the active option based on the sort URL parameter, and when the user selects a different sort order, updates the sort URL parameter and requests a fresh services list.

Shows a concise summary of currently active filters derived from URL parameters and filter options (e.g., selected main area group, area, gender, duration, and price range) and offers quick actions like clearing all filters; relies on the controller for actual URL updates and list reloading.

Displays the list of Service entities returned from listWaxingServices, using the current filter, sort, and pagination state; for each service, exposes actions to view service details and to add the service to the current session.

Uses the total, page, and pageSize values returned by listWaxingServices to render pagination controls; when the user changes the page, updates the page URL parameter and triggers a new listWaxingServices call via the controller.

Shows the current custom session state retrieved via getCurrentSessionState or from the latest addServiceToCurrentSession response, including a list of associated services (from session_services), total_duration_minutes, and total_price; for non-empty sessions, exposes actions to proceed to booking or open the full session builder.

Provides a functional link or button that navigates to build_session.html as the dedicated Build Your Own Waxing Session configurator, optionally appending relevant context parameters (mainAreaGroup, areaKey, genderSegment) from the current URL state.

Listens for successful addServiceToCurrentSession responses and uses getCurrentSessionState when necessary to refresh the session summary panel, ensuring that the session information displayed is always consistent with the backend state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strictly top‑to‑bottom, centered composition: global navigation (outside this spec) at the very top, followed by generous empty space, then a large centered page title with a thin divider. Directly beneath the title area, place a light filter/sort band, then the active filters summary. The services list occupies the main central column as a three‑column text grid. Pagination sits centered below the list. A lightweight, floating session summary panel lives to the right of the main column, and a subtle 'build session' link is placed near the top of the content area for an alternate path.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strictly top‑to‑bottom, centered composition: global navigation (outside this spec) at the very top, followed by generous empty space, then a large centered page title with a thin divider. Directly beneath the title area, place a light filter/sort band, then the active filters summary. The services list occupies the main central column as a three‑column text grid. Pagination sits centered below the list. A lightweight, floating session summary panel lives to the right of the main column, and a subtle 'build session' link is placed near the top of the content area for an alternate path.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a relatively narrow central content column (~70–80% of viewport width on desktop) with wide left and right margins. Reserve the upper 40–50% of the first viewport for navigation whitespace and the title block. Allocate a compact but airy strip beneath the title for filters and sorting. The services list receives the bulk of the vertical space, with very generous vertical spacing between rows. The session summary panel occupies a slim vertical slice near the right margin and floats independently so it does not crowd the central column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low. Each functional band (title, filters, services, pagination) is clearly separated by 48–96px vertical gaps. Within the services list, use generous line height and 24–32px gaps between rows. Controls in the filters bar and summary panel use minimal borders, subtle typography, and ample padding, so the page reads as calm and editorial rather than tool‑like.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from top to bottom: first the nav, then the prominent centered page title in blush, then the thin divider. From there, attention moves into the filters/sort band, then down into the services list, where the gaze scans left‑to‑right across each three‑column row. The pagination forms a natural stopping point at the bottom of the content column. In peripheral vision, the floating session summary panel on the right and the site’s existing chat bubble at the bottom right act as secondary anchors for action without disrupting the main vertical reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a refined, editorial menu: a wide white canvas with a narrow, centered content column framed by generous left and right margins. At the top, the site’s navigation sits lightly, followed by a large cushion of whitespace. The page title 'Waxing Services' appears in a soft blush tone, centered with fine serif typography, and a thin horizontal rule beneath it marks the transition into interactive content. Just below this, a single horizontal band holds subtle filters on the left and a compact sort control on the right, both understated and aligned within the central column. A low‑key active filter summary line, if present, sits directly underneath. The services list then occupies the heart of the page: a text‑only, three‑column grid of service name and price pairs, with each row breathing in ample white space so nothing feels crowded. Pagination controls are centered below the list, keeping the composition balanced. Along the right side of the content area, a slim, softly‑outlined session summary panel floats or sticks within the viewport, echoing the feel of the chat bubble but with more structure, allowing guests to see and adjust their chosen waxing areas as they browse. Overall the layout feels calm, luxurious, and highly legible, with every functional element nestled into the same restrained, airy rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non‑visual, infrastructural component. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the page hierarchy, just beneath the document root, managing URL parameters for filters, sorting, pagination, and session state. It does not create any box, occupy any width or height, or alter the spatial composition; all visible elements retain the clean, editorial layout unaffected by its presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non‑visual, global controller with no rendered footprint; it behaves like an invisible layer attached to the page, reacting to scroll and interactions without affecting layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters bar is the first visible interactive band beneath the title area. It is positioned within the centered content column, approximately 48–64px below the thin divider under the page title. Horizontally, it spans nearly the full width of the content column (about 70–80% of the viewport, with large outer margins on both sides). Within this band, the filter controls are left‑aligned, occupying roughly the left two‑thirds of the content column, leaving generous empty space between individual filter inputs and between the filters and the right edge. The overall bar height is modest—around 56–72px—driven by comfortable 8–12px vertical padding inside the form controls, so the bar reads as a light, airy row of text and outlines rather than a dense toolbar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as a normal block element. On larger screens it sits as a single, horizontal row; on narrower screens it can naturally wrap to multiple lines while remaining centered within the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control sits on the same horizontal line as the filters bar, visually sharing that band but anchored to the right side of the content column. It occupies roughly the right one‑third of that row, but its actual control width is compact—around 160–220px—leaving generous whitespace to its left. The label and selector are right‑aligned so the control forms a crisp endpoint to the filters band, mirroring the editorial precision of the mockup. Vertically, it is aligned with the middle of the filter inputs so the entire band reads as a single, balanced strip across the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an inline or flex item within the filters bar row, remaining right‑aligned in the central column. It scrolls with the content and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active filters summary appears directly beneath the filters/sort band, separated from it by a subtle vertical gap of about 16–24px. It is left‑aligned with the filters, starting at the same left edge as the filters within the central column. The summary is a low, horizontal strip of small, pill‑like tags or light text—typically no more than one or two lines tall—occupying only as much width as needed, often less than half the column width, allowing the right side of this row to remain mostly empty white space. This preserves the airy, editorial feel while still surfacing applied filters. When no filters are active, this row either collapses entirely or reduces to a very subtle, single‑line hint to preserve whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in the flow. Its height expands or collapses based on content, pushing the services list down as needed, but otherwise it does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services list is the primary content block and is positioned approximately 48–64px below the active filters summary (or directly below the filters band if no summary is shown). It occupies the full width of the central content column, which is significantly narrower than the viewport and centered, leaving wide lateral gutters on both sides. Inside this column, services are presented as a three‑column grid of text pairs: each service‑price pair is a compact horizontal group, with the service name left‑aligned and its price immediately to the right, separated by a small gap of around 8–12px. Up to three such pairs are laid out across a single row, evenly distributed so the three informal columns feel balanced with substantial gutters (~48–64px) between them. Rows are spaced vertically with generous gaps (about 24–32px), and line height for each text line is comfortable, creating the impression of a luxe, minimal menu rather than a dense table. The overall block can extend over several viewport heights, but thanks to the vertical rhythm and central alignment, it retains a calm, scrollable reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A vertically scrollable content block within the main page flow. It does not stick or overlay; its internal grid is responsive, collapsing from three informal columns to fewer columns on narrower screens while maintaining central alignment and generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Pagination controls are placed directly beneath the services list, separated from the last row of services by a distinctive vertical gap of about 48px, giving a clear sense of closure to the list. They are horizontally centered within the content column, forming a compact row of page numbers and navigation arrows that spans no more than 30–40% of the column’s width. The controls maintain the minimalist aesthetic—fine text, subtle hover states, and ample space between page numbers—so they read as a light editorial footer rather than a heavy control block. Additional whitespace (around 64px) below the pagination ensures that even the bottom of the list feels unhurried and refined.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element, centered within the main column and scrolling naturally with the content. It does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session summary panel is a slim, vertically‑oriented card that sits visually to the right of the central content column, echoing the bottom‑right chat bubble’s peripheral presence but higher in the hierarchy. On desktop, its left edge aligns roughly with the right edge of the main content column, intruding slightly into the outer right gutter but still within the overall page frame. It occupies about 20–25% of the viewport width (e.g., 260–320px), with ample internal padding (16–24px) and plenty of whitespace between items listed inside. Vertically, the panel begins around the middle of the viewport height, ensuring it does not compete with the page title; as the user scrolls, it maintains a consistent offset from the top (approximately 80–96px), so it remains visible while browsing the services list. Its bottom edge is kept at least 96px above the viewport bottom to leave space for the persistent chat bubble and to avoid a crowded corner. Inside, the panel lists selected services with sparse typography and thin dividers, plus a calm call‑to‑action to proceed to booking or the full configurator. The overall impression is of a lightweight, floating note at the side of the page rather than a chunky sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/floating behavior on larger screens: the panel is positioned relative to the main content area so it stays visible as the user scrolls through the services, with a fixed offset from the top of the viewport after it enters view. On smaller screens it can drop into the normal flow beneath the services list, spanning the full content column width while preserving generous padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The build‑session entry is a subtle, text‑based navigation link offering a more advanced configuration path. It is positioned within the central content column just below the page title block and its thin divider, but above the filters bar. Horizontally, it is right‑aligned with the column—occupying only as much width as the text requires, typically a single line—so it reads as a refined, secondary option off to the side rather than a loud hero button. The vertical spacing between the divider and this link is modest (about 16–24px), and another similar gap sits between the link and the top of the filters bar, allowing the title area, link, and filters to feel like a coherent editorial stack. Typographically, it can be styled as small caps or a fine serif with a minimalist arrow, maintaining the premium aesthetic while remaining visually lighter than the main 'session summary' and primary booking actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline/block element in the main column flow. It scrolls naturally with content and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is another non‑visual controller that ensures the session data shown in the session summary panel and the services list remains synchronized. Conceptually, it lives alongside the URL state controller near the top of the page’s component hierarchy and responds to navigation or state changes in the background. It has no visual footprint, so it does not alter or claim any on‑screen space; all visible spacing and alignment remain dedicated to the editorial layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non‑visual, global behavior. It does not occupy layout space, does not scroll, and does not affect spatial arrangement; it acts purely as a background listener and updater.

name: Service Detail & Booking
title: Waxing Service Detail & Booking
description: Displays details for a single waxing service and lets users book a one-time appointment, adjust date/time/technician, see suggested add-ons, and optionally switch to a multi-appointment scheduling flow.

core_features: Load and display full details for the selected waxing service (name, category, description, duration, price, preparation notes, aftercare notes) using getServiceDetails(serviceId).
Show a list of technicians who can perform this service and allow optional technician selection using getTechniciansForService(serviceId).

Provide a date selector and availability controls to query and display available time slots for the chosen service and technician using getServiceAvailability(serviceId, date, searchMode, technicianId).
Allow users to select a specific time slot and enter optional appointment notes to configure a single-service appointment.

Create a single-service appointment for the chosen date, time, and technician, and add it to the current booking summary/cart using bookServiceAppointment(serviceId, startDateTime, technicianId, notes).
Provide navigation to view the current booking summary (cart.html) after an appointment has been added.
Offer a clear path to switch into the multi-appointment booking flow for this service on booking.html with appropriate URL parameters.
Display suggested add-on services returned from getServiceDetails and link to each add-on’s own service detail page using URL parameters.
Provide navigation back to the general Waxing Services list (waxing_services.html).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View service details and book a single appointment
1. User lands on service_detail.html with a serviceId URL parameter.
2. Page reads serviceId from the URL and calls getServiceDetails(serviceId) to load service information and suggested add-ons.
3. Page calls getTechniciansForService(serviceId) to load available technicians.
4. Page initializes a default date and search mode (or uses date/searchMode from URL if present) and calls getServiceAvailability(serviceId, date, searchMode, technicianId or null).
5. User reviews service description, duration, price, preparation and aftercare notes.
6. User selects a preferred technician (optional) from the technician selector.
7. Page refreshes availability by calling getServiceAvailability with the updated technicianId.
8. User selects a date (if different from default) via the date selector.
9. Page updates the date in the URL and calls getServiceAvailability with the new date and current technician/searchMode.
10. User chooses a specific time slot from the list of available time slots.
11. User optionally enters notes for the appointment.
12. User clicks the primary action to add the appointment to the booking summary.
13. Page calls bookServiceAppointment(serviceId, selectedStartDateTime, selectedTechnicianId or null, notes).
14. If the response indicates success, the page shows a confirmation state and reveals navigation control(s) to view the booking summary (cart.html).

Workflow 2: Choose technician and refine availability
1. User lands on the page and waits for technicians to load via getTechniciansForService.
2. User selects a technician in the technician selector.
3. Page updates technicianId in the internal state and optionally in the URL.
4. Page calls getServiceAvailability(serviceId, currentDate, currentSearchMode, selectedTechnicianId).
5. Time slot list updates to reflect technician-specific availability.
6. User can switch technicians again; each change repeats steps 3–5.

Workflow 3: Switch to multi-appointment scheduling
1. User is viewing service details.
2. User clicks the control labeled for scheduling multiple visits or recurring appointments.
3. Page navigates to booking.html with URL parameters: appointmentType=single_service, serviceId=current serviceId, origin=service_detail.
4. The new page (not part of this specification) handles multi-appointment configuration while knowing which service initiated the flow.

Workflow 4: Explore suggested add-on services
1. After getServiceDetails loads, the page shows a list of suggested_add_ons services.
2. User reviews the list and clicks on an add-on service.
3. Page navigates to service_detail.html with serviceId set to the chosen add-on’s id.
4. The page reloads with the new serviceId and repeats the core loading and booking flow for that service.

Workflow 5: Navigate between service details, services list, and booking summary
1. From the breadcrumb or a dedicated link, user clicks to go back to the Waxing Services list.
2. Page navigates to waxing_services.html.
3. After successfully booking an appointment, user clicks a control to view the booking summary.
4. Page navigates to cart.html, where the previously booked appointment is reflected in the cart/booking summary.
interactions: On page load, the application reads serviceId from the URL. If present, it triggers getServiceDetails(serviceId) and getTechniciansForService(serviceId). If serviceId is missing or invalid, the page should show an error state or redirect (handled by routing logic external to these interfaces).

After service details are loaded, the page initializes date and search mode from URL parameters if available (e.g., date and searchMode). If they are not present, the page derives defaults (e.g., current date and an appropriate search mode) without hardcoding service-specific values.

When the date selector changes, the page updates the date parameter in the URL (using navigation state methods) and calls getServiceAvailability(serviceId, selectedDate, currentSearchMode, selectedTechnicianId or null).

When the search mode control changes (for example, from a standard date view to an earliest-available search), the page updates the searchMode URL parameter and calls getServiceAvailability(serviceId, selectedDate or null depending on the mode, newSearchMode, selectedTechnicianId or null).

When the technician selector changes, the page updates its internal selectedTechnicianId state (and optionally a technicianId URL parameter) and calls getServiceAvailability(serviceId, currentDate, currentSearchMode, selectedTechnicianId).
When an availability request completes, the page updates the time-slot list with the returned time_slots and clears any previously selected time slot if it is no longer available.
When the user clicks a time slot, the page sets that slot as selected (storing start_datetime and end_datetime in state) and highlights it as the active choice.
When the user types into the notes field, the page updates an internal notes state bound to the bookServiceAppointment notes parameter.

When the user clicks the primary booking action (e.g., 'Add to Booking Summary'), the page validates that a time slot is selected and then calls bookServiceAppointment(serviceId, selectedStartDateTime, selectedTechnicianId or null, notes).

On a successful response from bookServiceAppointment, the page uses the returned Appointment data to show confirmation (for example, date/time and technician name from the selected state and appointment.duration_minutes / appointment.price when available from the Appointment) and enables the 'View Booking Summary' control that navigates to cart.html.

If the bookServiceAppointment response indicates failure (success=false), the page displays the message property from the response and leaves the user’s selections intact so they can adjust date, time, or technician and retry.

When the user clicks 'Schedule multiple visits' or 'Book recurring appointments', the page navigates to booking.html with appointmentType=single_service, the current serviceId value, and origin=service_detail in the URL.

When the user clicks a suggested add-on service, the page navigates to service_detail.html with the clicked service’s id as the serviceId URL parameter so that this same functional flow repeats for the add-on service.
When the user clicks 'Back to Waxing Services' (or equivalent breadcrumb element), the page navigates to waxing_services.html without passing additional parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the URL parameter serviceId, which determines which Service entity and related data are loaded. On initial load, the page reads serviceId from the URL and passes it into getServiceDetails(serviceId) and getTechniciansForService(serviceId). Optional URL parameters such as date, searchMode, and technicianId can also be used to preconfigure the booking context: date defines the date passed into getServiceAvailability; searchMode determines how availability is resolved (e.g., by fixed date, earliest available, or another valid mode supported by the interface); technicianId specifies an initially selected technician to filter availability. When the user changes date, search mode, or technician via UI controls, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters so that the current state can be restored on reload or shared via link. The currently selected time slot (start_datetime and end_datetime) and notes are maintained in in-memory page state only and are not required to persist across reloads, so they are not mirrored to the URL. The page never uses localStorage for state; all shareable or restorable configuration is represented via URL parameters, and all business data is loaded dynamically from the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading URL parameters, loading service details and technicians, and triggering the first availability lookup. Centralizes references to getServiceDetails, getTechniciansForService, and initial getServiceAvailability calls.

Provides navigation back to the Waxing Services list and contextual breadcrumbs for the current service. Uses the loaded Service data for display labels but only relies on URL-based navigation actions.

Displays the core service information: name, main_area_group, gender_segment (if present), description, duration, base_price, preparation notes, and aftercare notes. It also can display rating if provided.

Displays a list of technicians who can perform the service and allows the user to select one or leave the selection unset to indicate no technician preference. Changing the selection triggers an availability refresh.
data_binding: selectedTechnicianId (local state, may be initialized from URL parameter technicianId)

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose the date for the appointment and optionally adjust search mode for availability resolution. Any change triggers a fresh availability lookup and URL parameter update.
data_binding: currentDate (local state, initialized from URL date or defaulted)
currentSearchMode (local state, initialized from URL searchMode or defaulted)

components.functionality: Lists available time slots returned from getServiceAvailability and allows the user to select one slot to use when booking the appointment.
data_binding: selectedTimeSlot (local state containing start_datetime and end_datetime)

components.functionality: Captures optional notes from the user to be attached to the appointment when booking via bookServiceAppointment.
data_binding: notes (local state to be passed to bookServiceAppointment.notes)

components.functionality: Validates that a time slot is selected, then calls bookServiceAppointment to create a single-service appointment and add it to the booking summary/cart. Displays success or error feedback based on the response and reveals navigation to the booking summary.
Provides a control that sends the user to the multi-appointment booking flow for the currently viewed service.
Displays the suggested_add_ons services returned from getServiceDetails and allows navigation to each add-on’s own detail and booking page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: All content sits in a single, centered content column with generous side margins (roughly 60–70% of viewport width, max ~1040px). The page flows top-to-bottom: global nav (outside this spec), then the breadcrumb, then a large, airy service summary/hero section, followed by the booking flow (technician → date/availability controls → time slots → multi-appointment switch), then suggested add-ons, notes, and finally the booking action panel. Within this central column, lists like time slots and add-ons use a 3-column grid pattern that echoes the original 3-column service menu.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All content sits in a single, centered content column with generous side margins (roughly 60–70% of viewport width, max ~1040px). The page flows top-to-bottom: global nav (outside this spec), then the breadcrumb, then a large, airy service summary/hero section, followed by the booking flow (technician → date/availability controls → time slots → multi-appointment switch), then suggested add-ons, notes, and finally the booking action panel. Within this central column, lists like time slots and add-ons use a 3-column grid pattern that echoes the original 3-column service menu.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper half of the first viewport is dominated by whitespace around the service summary, mirroring the mockup’s emphasis on the title. Vertical spacing between major sections (summary, booking controls, add-ons, booking action) is generous (64–96px). Inside each section, controls and rows are spaced with 24–32px vertical gaps. Horizontal space is airy: the central column is narrow compared to the viewport, and inner multi-column grids (time slots, add-ons) have wide gutters (48–64px) between columns.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low. Each logical grouping (e.g., technician selector, date controls, time slot grid, add-ons) feels like its own editorial block with ample breathing room around labels and controls. Time slots and add-ons are presented as lightweight tiles in a 3-column grid rather than dense tables. Form elements avoid stacking too tightly; labels, fields, and helper text each have clear vertical separation.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the global nav to the subtle breadcrumb at the top of the column, then is pulled into the large, centered service title in the summary panel. A thin divider and short description guide the gaze downward into the booking controls, which are left-aligned for easy scanning. Within the booking section, the user moves top-to-bottom through technician and date controls, then left-to-right across the 3-column time slot grid, row by row. Below this, the add-ons grid invites a secondary left-to-right scan. The flow ends at the prominently styled booking action panel at the bottom, while a peripheral chat bubble (global UI) remains in the lower right of the viewport.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a calm, editorial service detail screen centered within a wide field of white space. A slim breadcrumb, aligned to the left within a narrow central column, sits just beneath the global navigation. Below a generous vertical gap, the service summary panel dominates the upper portion of the viewport, with a large, refined serif title set at the visual center, a subtle horizontal rule beneath it, and a short, airy description. The main booking flow unfolds in a single vertical stack below this: technician selector, date and availability controls, and then a spacious 3-column grid of time slots that visually echoes the original 3-column waxing menu layout. A small, understated switch to multi-appointment mode aligns toward the right edge of the column near the time slots, functioning as a secondary pathway without interrupting the main flow. Beneath, suggested add-ons appear as a light, editorial section with their own heading and a 3-column add-on layout. A simple notes field follows, and the composition resolves in a strong but minimal booking action panel near the bottom of the column. Throughout, generous margins, fine typography, and a muted blush accent create a high-end, boutique feel that is airy yet clearly structured.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a translucent overlay centered over the entire content column (and, on smaller screens, effectively over the full viewport). When active, it lightly dims everything from the breadcrumb downwards, while a minimal, circular spinner or subtle progress indicator is centered both horizontally and vertically within the viewport. The overlay respects the editorial feel: edges are clean, with no heavy borders, and ample white space surrounds the loader indicator, allowing the underlying centered-column layout to remain perceptible in the background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay positioned over the main content area; it covers the scrollable region and remains centered as the user scrolls, then disappears entirely once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the very top of the main content column, directly beneath the global navigation bar, aligned to the left edge of the central column. It spans roughly 80% of the column width, leaving comfortable side space on both left and right. The breadcrumb text is small, muted, and in a fine-weight font, with ample padding above and below; there is approximately 32–40px of white space above it (to separate it from the nav) and a larger 64–80px gap below it before the service summary panel begins, reinforcing its subtle, secondary role.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the page as part of the main content column; no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service summary panel occupies the upper central portion of the page and is the first visually dominant block. It spans about 60–70% of the viewport width (matching the central column), centered horizontally. At the top of this panel, an optional small, uppercase category label (e.g., “WAXING”) is centered and lightly set in the muted blush accent. Below it, the service name (e.g., “Full Leg Wax”) appears as a large serif heading, center-aligned, visually occupying roughly the top third of the panel’s height. Immediately beneath the title, a thin, elegant horizontal rule extends about 25–30% of the column width, centered. Under the rule, a short 1–3 line description is center-aligned with generous line height. If key service metadata (e.g., duration, base price) are included, they are presented as a single horizontal row below the description, using up to three equally spaced items across the width, echoing the 3-column service menu pattern with wide gutters. A large vertical margin of 64–96px separates the bottom of this panel from the first booking control below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the central column; scrolls with the page and anchors the top of the booking flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The technician selector sits directly beneath the service summary panel, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 64px. It is left-aligned within the central column, occupying roughly 60% of the column width on larger screens, with the right portion left as open white space to maintain the airy layout. A concise label (e.g., “Select Technician”) appears above the control with about 8–12px of space between label and field. The selector itself (dropdown, chips, or list) uses comfortable padding and is not overly tall; there is approximately 24–32px of space below the selector before the next section (availability-date-controls) begins. On narrow screens, the selector expands to nearly the full column width while preserving vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block in the vertical flow; it reflows to full-width in smaller viewports but remains within the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability and date controls appear as a cohesive section directly below the technician selector, separated by about 32–40px of white space. This block is also left-aligned and spans slightly more width than the technician selector—around 70% of the column—to comfortably accommodate a date picker and any mode toggle (e.g., day/week view, or single vs multi-appointment view labels). Controls inside this section are arranged horizontally where space allows: for example, a date picker on the left and a compact ‘search mode’ or view toggle to its right, both aligned on a common baseline. The entire section is visually shallow, with a clean single-row feel, and has about 32–40px of white space beneath it before the time slot list starts, signaling a transition into the slot grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in the main content flow; elements inside can wrap to multiple rows on smaller screens while maintaining left alignment and vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time slot list forms a visually distinct grid section occupying the full width of the central column (about 60–70% of the viewport), centered and aligned with the sections above. At the top left of this block, a modest heading like “Available Times” may appear, with 16–24px of space below it before the grid. The slots themselves are arranged in a 3-column grid that echoes the original service menu: each slot is a small, pill-like tile or minimal card with the time centered inside, and the three columns are evenly distributed across the column width with wide gutters (48–64px) between them. Rows are generously spaced vertically (about 24–32px between rows), keeping the grid light and breathable. The grid extends downward as needed, but never feels dense; ample white space surrounds the entire grid, with a 40–56px margin below before the next elements (multi-appointment-switch and/or add-ons) appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable grid within the central column; the number of columns gracefully reduces (e.g., to 2 then 1) on smaller screens while preserving generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The multi-appointment switch is positioned in close proximity to the time slot list, but treated as a subtle, secondary control. On larger screens, it is placed just above the time slot grid’s top-right edge or aligned to the right within the same horizontal band as the “Available Times” heading, creating a right-anchored micro-control. It occupies only a small fraction of the column width, leaving ample white space around it and ensuring the slots remain visually dominant. There is about 16–24px of vertical space separating the switch from the grid below. On smaller screens, the switch drops to a line above the grid, right-aligned or centered, still with comfortable top and bottom padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the time-slot section; its position is relative to that block and it scrolls naturally as part of the content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The suggested add-ons panel appears as a distinct, editorial-style section below the time slots and multi-appointment control, separated by a generous 64–80px vertical margin to signal a new phase in the journey. It spans the full width of the central column. A simple, left-aligned heading such as “Suggested Add-ons” appears at the top of the section, with a short one-line description in lighter text beneath it, together occupying the top 20–25% of the section height. Below this, add-on services are displayed in a 3-column layout mirroring the menu style: each add-on is represented by a service name and price pair, aligned left within each column, with wide horizontal gutters (48–64px) separating columns and comfortable vertical spacing (24–32px) between rows. The section has ample white space at both top and bottom (about 32–48px), allowing it to feel optional and non-intrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the main vertical flow; on smaller screens the add-on grid collapses to 2 then 1 column while preserving generous margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The appointment notes form sits below the suggested add-ons panel with about 48–64px of vertical spacing, acting as a quiet, utilitarian block before the primary booking action. It is left-aligned within the central column and typically spans about 70% of the column width so the text area does not feel excessively wide. A concise label like “Notes for your esthetician” is placed above the text area with 8–12px of space. The text area itself has comfortable internal padding (around 12px vertical and 16–20px horizontal) and is set to a moderate height with ample room above and below (24–32px) to preserve the light, editorial feel. The bottom of this block is separated from the booking-action-panel by a 40–56px gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows inline with the rest of the content; expands vertically as needed without breaking the centered-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking action panel forms the visual and functional anchor at the bottom of the page’s main content column. It is centered horizontally and slightly narrower than the column—about 50–60% of the viewport width—to feel focused and intentional. The panel may appear as a lightly framed or subtly elevated block with a slim outline or soft shadow, consistent with the minimalist aesthetic. At the top of the panel, a concise summary line (e.g., selected service, date, time, technician) is left-aligned, with the primary call-to-action button (e.g., “Confirm Booking”) aligned either directly beneath or to the right in the same row on larger screens. The button uses the muted blush accent and generous padding (around 12px vertical, 24px horizontal) and is surrounded by generous white space: about 24–32px inside the panel around all elements, and 64–96px of whitespace beneath the panel before the page footer area. The overall block height is modest, allowing it to feel like a clear, decisive endpoint to the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in the vertical flow; on mobile it can stretch closer to full column width but remains centered with comfortable margins. It is not sticky by default, instead appearing at the natural end of the booking journey.

name: Build Your Own Session
title: Build Session
description: Configure a custom waxing session by combining multiple services, viewing live total time and price, and ensuring the session fits any provided duration and budget targets before proceeding to booking.

core_features: Load available waxing services with filtering and sorting, using dynamic options returned from getServiceFilterOptions and the listWaxingServices interface.
Highlight or pre-filter the service list based on an optional preselectAreaKey URL parameter so users can start from a specific body area.
Display and update a live summary of the current custom session using getCurrentSessionState, including selected services, total duration, and total price.
Allow users to add services into the current session from the services list via addServiceToCurrentSession, refreshing totals in real time.
Allow users to remove services from the current session via removeServiceFromCurrentSession, updating totals and constraint status.
Interpret optional targetDurationMin, targetDurationMax, and targetBudget URL parameters as session targets, and display whether the current session is within, below, or above these targets.
Enable or disable the 'Continue to Booking' action based on whether all provided duration and budget targets are satisfied by the current session.
Navigate to booking.html with the correct URL parameters when the user clicks 'Continue to Booking', passing the session along as a custom_session appointment.
