3. The package summary is displayed (name, description, and any available package metadata from the response).
4. The user opens technician selection, which uses getTechnicianProfiles() so they can optionally choose a specific technician (if applicable) or keep any technician.
5. The user picks a date or quick search mode, and the page calls getPackageAvailability(packageId, date, searchMode, technicianIdOrNull) to refresh available time slots.
6. The user clicks a desired time slot, adding a pending package appointment to the in-page appointment list.
7. The user may add notes or edit date/time and technician, triggering new getPackageAvailability calls as needed.
8. The user clicks "Continue"; the page calls bookPackageAppointment(packageId, startDateTime, technicianIdOrNull, notes) for the pending appointment.
9. Upon success, the page navigates to cart.html.

Workflow 4 – Recurring single-service series (e.g., monthly eyebrow waxing):
1. User arrives on booking.html with appointmentType=single_service, serviceId set, and recurrenceMode=series in the URL.
2. The page loads initial context with getServiceAvailability(serviceId, initialDateOrNull, initialSearchMode, null) and populates service summary.
3. The user chooses a technician using getTechniciansForService(serviceId); the selected technician is stored in page state as the default technician for the series.
4. The user opens the series/recurrence builder, specifying the number of appointments, recurrence pattern, and preferred time window; this configuration remains in page state.
5. For each target occurrence date calculated client-side from the recurrence configuration, the page queries getServiceAvailability(serviceId, occurrenceDate, appropriateSearchModeOrNull, selectedTechnicianIdOrNull) to load available time slots.
6. For each occurrence, the user selects a concrete time slot, creating a pending appointment entry for that occurrence (all default to the same technician unless the user overrides per occurrence).
7. The in-page appointment list now shows multiple pending appointments in the series. The user can edit or delete individual occurrences (editing date/time or technician calls getServiceAvailability again for that specific occurrence).
8. When satisfied, the user clicks "Continue"; the page iterates over all pending series appointments and for each calls bookServiceAppointment(serviceId, occurrenceStartDateTime, occurrenceTechnicianIdOrNull, notesForOccurrence).
9. If all booking calls succeed, the page navigates to cart.html. If any booking fails, the page indicates which occurrence failed and keeps that occurrence editable in the in-page appointment list.

Workflow 5 – Managing pending appointments before finalizing:
1. At any point after at least one time slot is selected, pending appointments are listed in the in-page appointment list component.
2. The user can click an edit action on any pending appointment to change date, time, technician, or notes.
3. When date or technician changes, the page refreshes availability for that appointment using the appropriate availability interface and updates time slot options.
4. The user can remove individual appointments from the list, which deletes them from page state without calling any booking interface.
5. The user may add more appointments (e.g., additional single-service or series occurrences) before finalizing.
6. When ready, the user clicks "Continue"; only the remaining pending appointments are converted into real appointments via the booking interfaces.
interactions: On page load, the booking context loader reads appointmentType, serviceId, packageId, origin, and recurrenceMode from the URL and triggers the appropriate initial API calls (getServiceAvailability, getCurrentSessionState/getCurrentSessionAvailability, or getPackageAvailability).

Clicking the technician dropdown for a single service triggers getTechniciansForService(serviceId) (if not already loaded) and updates the selectedTechnicianId in page state when the user chooses a technician.

Clicking the technician dropdown for a custom session or package triggers getTechnicianProfiles() (if not already loaded); selecting a technician updates the shared technician selection in page state.

Changing the date in the calendar or selecting a quick search mode triggers a call to the relevant availability interface (getServiceAvailability, getPackageAvailability, or getCurrentSessionAvailability) using the current technician selection and the chosen date/searchMode.

Clicking a time slot in the time-slot list creates or updates a pending appointment object in page state, capturing appointment_type, reference IDs (serviceId/sessionId/packageId), start_datetime, end_datetime, duration_minutes, price, technicianId, and notes placeholder.
Typing into a notes field (per appointment or shared) updates the notes property of the corresponding pending appointment object in page state.

In the recurrence builder, changing recurrence settings updates the series configuration in page state; clicking "Generate series" (or equivalent) calculates occurrence dates and triggers repeated getServiceAvailability calls for each occurrence date, then prompts the user to choose a slot for each.

Clicking an edit control on a pending appointment opens an edit modal or inline form; changing its date or technician triggers a fresh availability call for that specific appointment and updates its start_datetime when a new slot is chosen.
Clicking a remove/delete control on a pending appointment removes that appointment from the in-page list and from page state without any API calls.
Clicking "View service details" navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={currentServiceId}.
Clicking "Edit services in custom session" navigates to build_session.html when appointmentType=custom_session.
Clicking a back link uses the origin parameter (if present) to determine the target page (e.g., waxing_services.html, service_detail.html, build_session.html, packages.html, package_detail.html).

Clicking "Continue" / "Add to Booking Summary" iterates over all pending appointments in page state and calls the appropriate booking interface (bookServiceAppointment, bookPackageAppointment, or bookCustomSessionAppointment) for each; after successful responses for all, the page navigates to cart.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is initialized and controlled primarily through URL parameters and API responses. On load, appointmentType determines which booking flow to use: 'single_service' requires a valid serviceId and uses getServiceAvailability and bookServiceAppointment; 'custom_session' ignores serviceId/packageId and uses getCurrentSessionState, getCurrentSessionAvailability, and bookCustomSessionAppointment; 'package' requires packageId and uses getPackageAvailability and bookPackageAppointment. The optional recurrenceMode parameter controls whether the UI shows a series builder (e.g., when recurrenceMode='series') or a single-appointment flow. The origin parameter is stored in page state to configure the back navigation target; it does not affect which APIs are called. Date, searchMode, and technicianId selections are stored in in-memory page state and passed into the availability interfaces whenever the user changes date, search mode, or technician. For custom sessions, the session data (Session and SessionService + Service) is loaded once via getCurrentSessionState and reused across subsequent availability calls. Pending appointments exist only in page state until the user clicks 'Continue'; at that point, each pending appointment is transformed into one booking API call using the appropriate booking interface and the selected startDateTime, technicianId, and notes. The page does not rely on localStorage or persistent client storage; if the page is reloaded, URL parameters are re-read and context is re-fetched from the APIs. Navigation to other pages passes necessary identifiers via URL parameters (e.g., service_detail.html?serviceId={serviceId}).

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters (appointmentType, serviceId, packageId, origin, recurrenceMode) on page load, validates them, and dispatches initial API calls to load either the relevant service, custom session, or package availability and metadata.

Displays the high-level context of what the user is booking: for single_service, shows details from the Service object returned by getServiceAvailability; for custom_session, shows Session details and included services from getCurrentSessionState; for package, shows Package details from getPackageAvailability. Also surfaces the total duration and price as available.

Allows the user to select a technician for the current booking context. For appointmentType=single_service, it calls getTechniciansForService(serviceId) to populate choices. For appointmentType=custom_session or package, it calls getTechnicianProfiles() and may optionally filter by specialties. The selected technicianId is stored in shared page state and applied as default for new or series appointments.

Provides controls for selecting a specific date (calendar interface) and/or a search mode such as earliest_available, same_day, or next_week. Changes here trigger the appropriate availability API call for the current booking context.

Watches for changes in selected date, searchMode, technicianId, appointmentType, and the relevant identifiers (serviceId/packageId or current Session) and calls the appropriate availability interface: getServiceAvailability, getPackageAvailability, or getCurrentSessionAvailability. Stores the returned time_slots and associated metadata in page state.

Displays the set of available time_slots returned by the active availability API. Each time slot can be selected to create a new pending appointment or to update an existing one. The component does not hardcode time labels; it directly uses start_datetime and end_datetime from the API response.

Activated when recurrenceMode indicates a series flow for appointmentType=single_service. Collects recurrence parameters such as count of appointments, interval, and preferred time ranges, stores them in page state, and orchestrates per-occurrence calls to getServiceAvailability to propose available time slots for each occurrence. Uses the currently selected technicianId by default for all occurrences.
Allows users to enter notes for each pending appointment (or optionally shared notes) which are stored alongside the pending appointment and passed as the notes parameter to the booking interfaces.

Shows all pending appointments in the current booking session before they are submitted to the backend. Each list item includes date/time, technician, and notes, and provides controls to edit or remove the appointment. Editing triggers availability reload for that item as needed.

Provides a focused interface to edit a single pending appointment from the list, including changing date, time, technician, and notes. When date or technician changes, it calls the appropriate availability interface for that appointment’s context to refresh slots, then updates the pending appointment’s start_datetime and technicianId.

Manages navigation links based on the current context: back to the origin page derived from the origin URL parameter, link to service_detail.html with the current serviceId for single-service flows, and link to build_session.html when scheduling a custom session.

Aggregates actions for finalizing the booking. On click of the main button, iterates through all pending appointments and calls the correct booking interface for each (bookServiceAppointment, bookPackageAppointment, or bookCustomSessionAppointment) using the appointment’s startDateTime, technicianId, and notes. If all calls succeed, it navigates to cart.html; if any fail, it reports errors and keeps the corresponding appointments in the pending list.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: Single centered content column, visually separated from the global site nav above by generous white space. At the top of this column, a bold, centered booking page title with a thin divider echo the 'WAXING' heading structure from the mockup. Below the divider, the booking context summary establishes the session overview. Under that, a horizontal control row holds technician selection and date/search mode, aligned to an implicit 3-column grid. Availability and time slots occupy the mid-lower portion of the column as the main interactive area, with recurrence and notes forming a secondary configuration band beneath. Pending appointments appear in a multi-row, 3-column grid near the bottom of the content column, mirroring the mockup’s service menu pattern. A sticky submit bar anchors to the bottom of the viewport, aligned with the central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Single centered content column, visually separated from the global site nav above by generous white space. At the top of this column, a bold, centered booking page title with a thin divider echo the 'WAXING' heading structure from the mockup. Below the divider, the booking context summary establishes the session overview. Under that, a horizontal control row holds technician selection and date/search mode, aligned to an implicit 3-column grid. Availability and time slots occupy the mid-lower portion of the column as the main interactive area, with recurrence and notes forming a secondary configuration band beneath. Pending appointments appear in a multi-row, 3-column grid near the bottom of the content column, mirroring the mockup’s service menu pattern. A sticky submit bar anchors to the bottom of the viewport, aligned with the central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper half of the viewport is dominated by whitespace, the page title, and the context summary, with 64–96px gaps between the global nav, title, summary, and first control row. The main interactive area (date picker + time slots) takes most of the remaining vertical space but still uses generous 24–32px vertical gaps between sections and rows. The central column itself spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with wide side margins. Within rows (technician/date, time slots, pending appointments), content is arranged into up to three implicit columns separated by large gutters (~48–64px).
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally sparse and breathable. Each logical group (summary, selectors, slots, recurrence, notes, pending list) is visually separated by generous vertical margins. Lists and grids avoid dense packing: time slots are displayed in a lightly populated 3-column button grid; the pending appointments list shows only a few appointment summaries per row with comfortable spacing; forms use single or double column fields with ample line height and padding rather than tightly stacked inputs.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the global nav down to the centered 'Booking & Scheduling' title, then to the thin divider beneath it. From there, the user’s attention flows into the booking context summary, then down to the technician and date controls as a left-to-right sweep across the first row. The gaze then drops to the availability status and time-slot grid, scanning left-to-right and top-to-bottom in a zig-zag pattern similar to the service menu. Below, attention flows through recurrence options and notes, and finally to the pending appointments grid. Throughout, the sticky submit bar at the bottom right acts as a persistent call-to-action anchor in peripheral vision, similar in emphasis to the chat bubble in the original mockup.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a calm, editorial booking experience centered within a narrow, fixed-width column floating in generous white space. Above, the site’s global navigation sits lightly at the very top, with a large vertical gap before a centered, serif 'Booking & Scheduling' title in muted blush, followed by a thin horizontal divider that visually caps the hero space. Beneath this, a clean context summary panel introduces the current booking session. A first functional row aligns technician selection on the left with a broader date and search-mode picker to its right, echoing a 3-column grid with large gutters. Dropping further down, the availability status and time-slot list form the main body of the page: time slots appear as evenly spaced, pill-like buttons arranged in up to three columns across the central column. Below, recurrence configuration and appointment notes are laid out with generous padding, followed by a pending appointments list that adopts the mockup’s menu-like 3-column pattern—each row showing up to three concise appointment summaries with ample vertical breathing room. Over everything, a sticky booking submit bar is docked at the bottom of the viewport, aligned to the central column, lending a sense of constant, unobtrusive control. The overall effect is airy, luxurious, and restrained, guiding the eye down the column in a gentle, predictable rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-context-loader occupies the same central column region as the booking-context-summary and the initial control row while data is loading. It appears directly below the title divider, centered horizontally within the content column (about 60–70% of the viewport width), and consists of one or two horizontal skeleton bars or shimmer blocks stacked with 16–24px vertical gaps. There is a generous 48–64px white space margin above it (from the divider) and below it (before where the technician and date controls will appear), maintaining the airy feel even during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the central column; replaces or overlays the booking-context-summary area while loading, then collapses out of the flow once data is ready.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-context-summary sits at the top of the main content column, directly beneath the centered page title and thin divider. It is horizontally centered and spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, matching the implicit central column. Vertically, it has a generous top margin of 48–64px from the divider and a bottom margin of 48px before the control row below. The panel itself is low-profile: a subtle, full-width card within the column with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides, fine serif headings, and minimalist lines or blush accents. It should feel like a calm summary band that anchors the rest of the booking controls that cascade down from it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow; scrolls naturally with the column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The technician-selector is part of the first control row below the booking-context-summary. This row is centered within the content column and aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. The technician-selector occupies the left third of this row (about 3–4 grid columns), starting flush with the left edge of the content column. It appears as a single, tall, minimalist field with a label above and a dropdown or pill-like selector below. There is about 24px vertical space between the summary panel and this control row, and the technician-selector has 16–24px internal vertical padding, maintaining generous click targets and an uncluttered look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; aligned to the left side of the central control row, responsive so that on narrower screens it can stack above the date-and-searchmode-picker with a 24px vertical gap.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date-and-searchmode-picker sits in the same horizontal row as the technician-selector, immediately to its right, within the content column. It spans the remaining two-thirds of the row (about 8–9 grid columns) and is visually balanced by a large horizontal gutter (~48–64px) between it and the technician-selector. The component presents the primary date picker and any search mode toggles in a single, elegant band: label text aligned with the technician field, then a wide input/interactive region that stretches nearly to the right edge of the column. Vertical spacing mirrors the technician-selector, with 24–32px top and bottom padding inside the component and about 24px margin below the entire control row before the availability section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the first control row; on narrow screens it collapses below the technician-selector, maintaining generous 24–32px spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-loader appears directly below the first control row and above the time-slot-list, occupying the full width of the central content column. It is a slim horizontal skeleton bar or a set of two bars stacked with 8–16px spacing, suggesting loading of daily availability. There is a 32px margin above (from the controls) and 32px below (before the time-slot-list), preserving the airy rhythm even in loading states. The loader aligns left and right with the technician and date-picker row to maintain consistent column boundaries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main column, temporarily occupying the vertical space reserved for availability status until real data is shown.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-slot-list forms the main interactive body of the page and sits directly below the availability loader (or availability status when loaded), centered within the content column. It spans the same width as the column and is organized into an airy, 3-column grid. Each time slot appears as a small, pill-shaped button or minimalist card with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal internal padding. Slots are arranged in rows of up to three, evenly distributed across the width with large gutters (~48–64px) between the three columns, echoing the service menu pattern from the mockup. Vertically, rows are separated by 24–32px. The overall block has a top margin of about 32px from the availability status and a bottom margin of 48–64px before the recurrence and notes section, giving this area visual weight as the key decision zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; scrolls within the main column. If many slots are present, the list can extend downward with consistent row spacing, but it should avoid feeling dense or table-like.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recurrence-series-builder sits below the time-slot-list as part of a secondary configuration band. It occupies roughly the left two-thirds of the content column, starting at the column’s left edge, with a large horizontal gutter (~48px) to its right. It is presented as a clean, low-density form with one or two fields per row. Labels sit above inputs, and any internal grouping (e.g., frequency, end date) uses 16–24px vertical spacing between controls to maintain a calm, editorial pace. There is a 48px margin above it from the time-slot-list and a 32px margin below before the appointment-notes-editor if laid out below, or they share the same row if there is adequate width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; in wider layouts, it shares a horizontal row with the appointment-notes-editor, while in narrower layouts it stacks above notes with 32px vertical separation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The appointment-notes-editor is positioned in relation to the recurrence-series-builder. On desktop, it occupies roughly the right one-third of the same row, aligned to the right edge of the content column with a wide horizontal gutter (~48px) separating it from the recurrence builder. It is a simple, single text-area field with a label above, internal padding of 16–24px, and ample height to feel generous but not heavy. This creates a visually balanced two-block row below the time-slot-list without feeling crowded. On smaller widths, the notes editor stacks directly beneath the recurrence builder with about 32px vertical space between them. Below the notes editor, there is a 48px margin before the pending-appointments-list begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; responsive behavior toggles between being a right-aligned column partner to the recurrence builder and stacking beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pending-appointments-list anchors the lower part of the content column. It spans the full width of the central column and adopts the mockup’s menu-like, 3-column row pattern. Each row can display up to three compact appointment summary tiles, evenly spaced across the width with large gutters (~48–64px) between them. Each tile is minimalist—perhaps just service name, technician, date/time, and a subtle price—arranged in a small vertical stack with 12–16px internal padding and fine typography. Rows are separated by 24–32px of vertical space, and the entire block has a 48–64px top margin from the notes/recurrence area. The bottom margin before the sticky submit bar’s visual edge is at least 80–96px, so the list feels fully above the action bar rather than pinned to it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; scrolls within the main column. As appointments are added, new rows appear below with consistent 3-column layout, maintaining low density.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The appointment-editor-modal appears as a centered overlay above the entire page. The background content (central column, sticky bar, etc.) is dimmed with a translucent white or dark veil, while the modal itself is a narrow, editorial card centered both horizontally and vertically. The modal width is around 640–720px, never exceeding the width of the underlying content column by much, and it uses generous internal padding (32–40px) and large white margins. Fields inside follow a single or two-column layout with ample 24px vertical separation, echoing the page’s low density. The modal’s top edge aligns visually with the central column, reinforcing the fixed-width, book-page feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay positioned in the center of the viewport with a dimmed backdrop; scrollable internally if content overflows, while the page beneath remains static.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-controls live near the bottom of the content column, just above the visual area occupied by the sticky booking-submit-bar. They are aligned with the central column and sit in a narrow horizontal band with a 32–40px margin above the submit bar. On the left, a light, text-only control (e.g., 'Back to services') aligns to the column’s left edge; on the right, secondary actions such as 'Clear selections' or 'Cancel' align to the right edge. The band is visually minimal—no heavy background or borders—allowing it to feel like quiet inline text navigation rather than a second action bar, preserving the primacy of the main submit bar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content column; as the user scrolls, this band can scroll off-screen while the sticky booking-submit-bar remains fixed at the bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-submit-bar is a persistent action bar anchored to the bottom of the viewport, visually aligned to the central content column’s width. On larger screens, the bar appears as a floating strip with a subtle shadow, inset from the left and right edges of the window so its width matches the central column (about 60–70% of the viewport) and centered horizontally. Inside, the left side summarizes the number of appointments and total estimated time/cost in fine, understated typography, while the right side hosts a prominent, blush-accent primary button for proceeding to Cart & Booking Summary. There is ample internal padding (16–24px vertically, 24–32px horizontally) and generous separation between summary text and the primary button, ensuring it feels premium and unhurried. When scrolled to the page bottom, the form content ends slightly above this bar, leaving airy space so content does not collide with it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed to the bottom of the viewport, always visible as the user scrolls, but horizontally constrained to and centered with the main content column to maintain the editorial, centered layout.

name: Shop Products
title: Shop Products
description: Browse and refine the list of retail products (aftercare, skincare, accessories), filter by price, rating, and fulfillment options, sort results, open product details, and add products to the cart.

core_features: Load available product filter and sort options using getProductFilterOptions and render them as interactive controls.
List products using listProducts with filters for category, maximum price, minimum rating, fulfillment mode, and sort and pagination parameters derived from the URL.
Allow users to change category, price range, rating, and fulfillment filters and update the URL and product list accordingly.
Allow users to change the sort order of products (e.g., by price or rating) and sync this with the URL and the product list.
Provide pagination controls that update the page URL parameter and reload products via listProducts.
Allow users to open individual product detail pages by clicking a product image, name, or a dedicated details link, passing productId in the URL.
Allow users to add products directly to the cart from the product list using addProductToCart, with quantity control where appropriate.
Provide navigation to the cart/booking summary after items are added (via a cart link or button).
user_workflows: Initial load workflow: 1) User navigates to shop.html (optionally with URL parameters such as categoryId, maxPrice, minRating, fulfillmentMode, sort, page). 2) The page controller parses URL parameters into a filter/sort/pagination state object. 3) The page calls getProductFilterOptions (no parameters) to retrieve categories, price ranges, rating options, fulfillment options, and sort options. 4) Using the parsed URL parameters and the available options, the page constructs the filters, sort, and pagination objects and calls listProducts. 5) The product list, current filter selections, sort selection, and pagination state are rendered. 6) The user sees products that match the incoming URL state.

Change category workflow: 1) User selects a category in the filter sidebar (e.g., a radio or button group bound to categoryId). 2) The filter component updates the categoryId value in the in-memory filter state and updates the categoryId query parameter in the URL (using history APIs so the page can stay mounted). 3) The page controller resets or validates the page parameter (e.g., moves to the first available page as defined by the implementation) and triggers a new call to listProducts with the updated filters (category_key from categoryId plus other current filters). 4) The product list re-renders with products matching the new category.

Filter by price, rating, and fulfillment workflow: 1) User selects a price range, rating threshold, or fulfillment mode using controls backed by getProductFilterOptions (e.g., selecting an option with a max_price, min_rating, or fulfillment mode value). 2) On each change (or on an explicit 'Apply Filters' action), the filter component updates the corresponding URL parameters (maxPrice, minRating, fulfillmentMode) while preserving other parameters. 3) The page controller listens for URL changes, constructs the filters object (mapping URL params to the fields expected by listProducts), and calls listProducts. 4) The product grid is updated to show only products that satisfy all active filters. 5) If no products are returned, the grid displays an empty state message driven by dynamic data (no hardcoded product examples).

Change sort order workflow: 1) User changes the sort option using a sort dropdown populated from getProductFilterOptions.sort_options. 2) The sort-bar component updates the sort value in the URL (sort parameter) and resets/validates the page parameter as needed. 3) The page controller calls listProducts with the new sort value and current filters/pagination. 4) The product grid rerenders in the new order while preserving the current filters.

Pagination workflow: 1) User clicks a pagination control (next page, previous page, or a specific page index). 2) The pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL while keeping categoryId, maxPrice, minRating, fulfillmentMode, and sort values unchanged. 3) The page controller reads the new page from the URL and calls listProducts with the updated pagination object. 4) The user sees the corresponding subset of products and the active page is visually indicated using only dynamic data (no static labels).

Open product detail workflow: 1) User clicks on a product’s image, name, or a dedicated 'View Details' link within a product card. 2) The product-grid component triggers navigation to product_detail.html with a query parameter productId set to the clicked product’s Product.id. 3) No product detail data is fetched on this page; the detail page handles that.

Add product to cart from list workflow: 1) Within a product card, the user sets a quantity (if exposed) or accepts the default quantity. 2) User clicks an 'Add to Cart' action (button or icon). 3) The add-to-cart form component calls addProductToCart with productId (from the Product.id bound to that card) and the selected quantity. 4) On success (success = true), the component can display a confirmation state using the message from the API and optionally update a cart indicator (if present) without hardcoding any numeric totals. 5) The user may then click a cart link or 'View Cart / Booking Summary' action that navigates to cart.html (no additional parameters required as per the outgoing connection). 6) On failure (success = false), an error message from the API is displayed without hardcoded content, and the product is not added.

Update filters via URL/share link workflow: 1) User receives or manually enters a shop.html URL that includes some combination of categoryId, maxPrice, minRating, fulfillmentMode, sort, and page parameters. 2) When the URL is loaded or modified, the page controller re-parses these parameters, updates the internal state (used by filter controls, sort dropdown, and pagination), and calls listProducts with a matching filters/sort/pagination object. 3) The UI controls reflect the state encoded in the URL, allowing the same product set to be reproduced across sessions or shared links without using localStorage.
interactions: On page load or URL change, the page controller invokes getProductFilterOptions once (if not already loaded) and then invokes listProducts with filter, sort, and pagination arguments derived from URL parameters.
Changing the category selection in the filter sidebar updates the categoryId URL parameter and triggers a new listProducts call with filters.category_key set accordingly.

Selecting a price range updates the maxPrice URL parameter (and optionally a min price if the backend supports it), and the page reloads the product list with an updated filters object containing the price constraint.
Selecting a minimum star rating updates the minRating URL parameter and triggers a new product list request with filters.min_rating bound to that parameter.
Selecting a fulfillment option updates the fulfillmentMode URL parameter and triggers a new product list request with filters.fulfillment_mode bound to that parameter.
Changing the sort dropdown updates the sort URL parameter, resets pagination as needed, and triggers listProducts with the new sort value.
Clicking a pagination control updates the page URL parameter and invokes listProducts with pagination.page set to that value, while preserving the existing filters and sort in the URL.
Clicking on a product’s name, image, or details link initiates navigation to product_detail.html with productId set from the Product.id for that card.

Submitting the add-to-cart form within a product card calls addProductToCart with the card’s productId and the chosen quantity; on success, an in-page confirmation state is shown and a link to cart.html is available.
Clicking on the cart icon or 'View Cart / Booking Summary' link navigates to cart.html without modifying the current shop filters in the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for the currently active filters, sort, and pagination. On every load or navigation event within shop.html, the controller parses the following parameters: categoryId, maxPrice, minRating, fulfillmentMode, sort, and page. These values are mapped into the request body for listProducts: the filters object includes a category key derived from categoryId (mapped to Product.category_key), an optional maximum price derived from maxPrice, an optional minimum rating derived from minRating, and an optional fulfillment mode derived from fulfillmentMode. The sort parameter is passed as-is (after validation against getProductFilterOptions.sort_options) into the sort argument of listProducts. The pagination.page field is taken from the page parameter (or from the implementation’s default for the first page when absent), and the pageSize is determined by configuration or backend defaults, not hardcoded in this specification. When the user interacts with filter, sort, or pagination controls, components update the URL using pushState/replaceState (or full reload if required) so that every change is encoded in the query string; the controller then re-runs listProducts with the new parameters. Filter controls (category selector, price, rating, fulfillment) and the sort dropdown are always derived from getProductFilterOptions, and their selected values are set based on the current URL parameters, ensuring the UI is synchronized with the URL at all times. No state is stored in localStorage; the URL and the live API responses are the only persisted state mechanisms.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and state synchronization for the Shop Products page. On initialization and URL changes, it loads filter/sort options and the product list from the appropriate interfaces, maintains an in-memory representation of current filters/sort/pagination derived from the URL, and passes the relevant data and callbacks down to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Called when shop.html is loaded. Parses URL parameters into a state object, calls getProductFilterOptions, and then calls listProducts with the derived filters, sort, and pagination.
onUrlChange: Called whenever the URL query string changes (e.g., through pushState or direct navigation). Re-parses URL parameters, updates internal state, and re-invokes listProducts.
onFiltersChange: Receives new filter values from filter-sidebar (categoryId, maxPrice, minRating, fulfillmentMode). Updates the URL query parameters accordingly and triggers onUrlChange logic.
onSortChange: Receives a new sort value from sort-bar, updates the sort parameter in the URL, resets/validates the page parameter, and triggers onUrlChange logic.
onPageChange: Receives a new page index from pagination-controls, updates the page URL parameter, and triggers onUrlChange logic.
onAddToCartSuccess: Receives the addProductToCart response from product-grid or add-to-cart-form and forwards any success or error messages to a notification mechanism or cart indicator.

components.functionality: Displays and manages filter controls for category, price, rating, and fulfillment. Options are populated dynamically via getProductFilterOptions. When a user updates filters and applies them, it updates the URL via the page controller.
event_handlers: onCategoryChange: Triggered when the user selects a different category option. Calls a callback from the page controller with the updated categoryId value.

onPriceRangeChange: Triggered when the user selects or adjusts a price range. Extracts max_price (and min_price if available) from the selected option and passes maxPrice (and optional minimum) to the page controller.
onRatingChange: Triggered when the user selects a minimum rating option. Passes the selected min_rating as minRating to the page controller.
onFulfillmentChange: Triggered when the user selects a fulfillment option. Passes the selected fulfillment mode as fulfillmentMode to the page controller.
onApplyFilters: Optional aggregation handler invoked if filters are applied in batch; sends the current combined filter state to the page controller to update URL parameters together.

onClearFilters: Clears current filter selections (except fields the implementation chooses to preserve), resets associated URL parameters (e.g., removes maxPrice, minRating, fulfillmentMode, and possibly categoryId), and triggers a refresh via the page controller.

components.functionality: Provides a control (e.g., dropdown) for selecting the sort order of products. Sort options are sourced from getProductFilterOptions.sort_options. Updates the URL sort parameter through the page controller.
event_handlers: onSortOptionChange: Triggered when the user selects a different sort option. Passes the selected sort value to the page controller’s onSortChange, which updates the URL and triggers a listProducts call.

components.functionality: Renders the current page of products returned by listProducts as a grid or list of product cards. Each card shows key information (name, price, rating, fulfillment indicators, and image) and exposes navigation to product detail and add-to-cart actions.
event_handlers: onProductClick: Triggered when a user clicks a product’s image, name, or details link. Navigates to product_detail.html with productId set to the corresponding Product.id in the URL query string.

onAddToCartClick: Triggered from an embedded add-to-cart-form or button on a product card. Forwards productId and quantity to the add-to-cart-form component or directly calls addProductToCart via the page controller, then handles success or error states.
onProductsUpdated: Called by the page controller when a new listProducts response is available, causing the grid to re-render with the updated products array.

components.functionality: Embedded within each product card, this functional component handles quantity selection (if applicable) and submitting a product to the cart using addProductToCart.
event_handlers: onQuantityChange: Updates the internal quantity state for the current product card when the user changes the quantity input.

onSubmitAddToCart: Prevents default form submission, calls addProductToCart with the bound productId and current quantity, and handles the response. On success, it surfaces the success message to the user and notifies the page controller via onAddToCartSuccess; on failure, it displays the error message from the API.

onAddToCartResponse: Receives the result of addProductToCart (success flag, cart_item_id, message) and updates the card UI state accordingly (e.g., temporary confirmation or error state) without hardcoding any cart totals or product details.

components.functionality: Displays pagination elements based on metadata returned by listProducts (total, page, pageSize) and lets users navigate between pages by updating the page URL parameter.
event_handlers: onNextPageClick: Increments the current page index if another page exists, and calls the page controller’s onPageChange with the new value.
onPreviousPageClick: Decrements the current page index if applicable, and calls onPageChange.
onPageNumberClick: Called when the user selects a specific page index; passes that index to onPageChange.
onPaginationDataUpdate: Triggered when new pagination metadata arrives from listProducts; updates internal view of available pages and disabled states.

components.functionality: Provides access to the cart/booking summary page. Typically displayed in the header or near add-to-cart confirmations to allow users to review items after adding products.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigates the user to cart.html when the link or icon is activated. Does not alter current shop.html URL filter parameters, allowing users to return to the same filtered view if they navigate back.

components.functionality: Optionally displays a concise summary of active filters (category, price, rating, fulfillment) and allows quick removal of individual constraints. Acts as a bridge between URL parameters and visible filter state.
event_handlers: onRemoveFilterTag: Triggered when the user removes a specific active filter (e.g., clears a rating or fulfillment selection). Updates the corresponding URL parameter (e.g., removes minRating or fulfillmentMode) via the page controller, which then updates listProducts.

onClearAllFilters: Removes all filter-related URL parameters (categoryId, maxPrice, minRating, fulfillmentMode) while keeping sort and page logic consistent, then triggers a refresh via the page controller.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a vertical, centered flow inspired by the mockup: global navigation (with the cart link) at the very top, then a tall block of whitespace, then the shop-page-controller as a large, centered page header. Directly under this header, a thin divider and the filter-summary-bar form a subtle transition into the browsing area. Below that, the main content splits into a two-column band within the same fixed-width central column: a narrow filter-sidebar on the left and a wider product area on the right. The product area stacks the sort-bar, then the product-grid, followed by the pagination-controls at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a vertical, centered flow inspired by the mockup: global navigation (with the cart link) at the very top, then a tall block of whitespace, then the shop-page-controller as a large, centered page header. Directly under this header, a thin divider and the filter-summary-bar form a subtle transition into the browsing area. Below that, the main content splits into a two-column band within the same fixed-width central column: a narrow filter-sidebar on the left and a wider product area on the right. The product area stacks the sort-bar, then the product-grid, followed by the pagination-controls at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A fixed-width central column (roughly 70–80% of viewport width, max ~1100–1200px) is centered with wide outer margins on both sides to preserve the editorial feel. The upper half of the first viewport is dominated by vertical whitespace around the shop-page-controller and its divider. Below, the content band allocates about 25–30% of the central column to the filter-sidebar on the left and 70–75% to the product area on the right, with a generous horizontal gap between them. Within the product area, the product-grid uses three equal columns with wide gutters, echoing the service grid from the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low and airy: the header occupies a large vertical span with minimal text; the filter-sidebar groups have generous vertical separation; the sort-bar is a slim, unobtrusive control strip; product cards have ample padding and breathing space, with only three products per row and generous gaps between rows. Pagination controls are isolated with significant vertical margin above, avoiding any crowded footer effect.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the top navigation (cart link highlighted at the right edge) down to the large, centered Shop Products title. A thin divider under the title gently pulls attention downward into the filter-summary-bar. From there, the gaze naturally shifts slightly right into the main product area, first scanning the sort-bar and then the first row of product cards from left to right, row by row, echoing the zig-zag reading pattern in the mockup’s service menu. The filter-sidebar sits calmly to the left as a persistent, secondary focal column. At the bottom of the viewport, the centered pagination provides a clear stopping point and call to continue browsing.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a calm, centered, editorial shopping experience. A slim, minimal global header runs across the top, with the cart link aligned to the far right within a fixed-width central content column. Below it, a generous stretch of whitespace leads into a prominent, centered Shop Products header that uses refined serif typography and a blush accent to signal a premium brand. Beneath the title, a fine horizontal rule and a low-profile filter-summary-bar form a soft threshold into the shopping area. The layout then opens into a two-column band: a narrow, understated filter column on the left and a more dominant product column on the right. Within the product column, a light sort-bar sits at the top, followed by a three-column product-grid where each card is spacious, aligned, and separated by wide gutters. Add-to-cart controls live quietly at the bottoms of cards, keeping the visual emphasis on the product imagery and titles. Generous margins and vertical spacing throughout ensure the page feels airy and boutique, while the centered pagination at the bottom of the product column neatly caps the experience and invites further browsing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shop-page-controller sits near the top center of the page inside the fixed-width central column, directly below the global navigation and cart link. It begins after a tall vertical margin (roughly 80–96px) that separates it from the header, reinforcing the calm, editorial feel. The component is center-aligned and spans about 60–70% of the central column width, leaving generous whitespace on either side. At its core is the large 'Shop Products' title, set in refined serif type and optionally accented with the blush brand color, centered horizontally. A short, single-line page description sits directly beneath the title, also center-aligned, with modest line length for easy reading. Below the description, a very thin horizontal divider extends across a moderate portion of the central column (about 40–50% width), centered, acting as a subtle visual break to the content below. The bottom of this component leaves another generous gap (around 48–64px) before the filter-summary-bar appears.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main document flow; it anchors the top of the content area and does not stick on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar starts just below the shop-page-controller (and roughly aligned vertically with the sort-bar to its right), forming the left column of the main content band. It is positioned flush to the left edge of the central content column and occupies about 25–30% of that width. A generous horizontal gap (about 48–64px) separates it from the product area on the right, preserving the airy, editorial layout. Vertically, the sidebar extends downward along the page, but each filter group—such as price range, rating, fulfillment options—stacks with generous vertical spacing (24–32px between groups). Each group has its title left-aligned with fine, understated text, with controls (checkboxes, sliders, etc.) arranged in a clean vertical stack with ample breathing room. The overall look is light, with plenty of white space around each control. The top of the sidebar aligns approximately with the bottom of the filter-summary-bar, ensuring a cohesive top edge with the product area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky column on larger screens: once scrolled into view, it remains fixed relative to the viewport’s top (with an offset that clears the header and page title spacing, around 80–96px), so filters stay accessible while the product grid scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-bar is located at the top of the right-hand product area, directly to the right of the filter-sidebar and just below the filter-summary-bar. It spans the full width of the product column (roughly 70–75% of the central content column) and is left-aligned with that column’s inner edge. The bar itself is slim, with modest vertical height (about 32–40px tall plus 8–12px padding), and uses fine typography and subtle borders to keep a low visual profile. Inside, the primary sort control (e.g., a dropdown labeled 'Sort by') appears left-aligned, while secondary information such as result count and view toggles (if any) are aligned to the right, creating a balanced horizontal line. A faint bottom border or hairline rule may separate the sort-bar from the product-grid that follows. A comfortable vertical gap (about 24–32px) is left between the sort-bar and the first row of products, preventing visual crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the product column; it scrolls away with the content and does not remain fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid sits directly below the sort-bar within the right-hand product column and is the main interactive field for browsing products. It occupies the entire width of the product column (approximately 70–75% of the central content column), with a consistent inner margin that aligns card content vertically and horizontally. Products are arranged in a three-column grid on larger screens, mirroring the mockup’s three-column service menu: each row displays up to three product cards, evenly distributed across the width with substantial gutters (about 32–48px) between cards. The cards align to a shared top baseline in each row. Vertical spacing between rows is generous (around 32–48px), so each row feels distinct and uncluttered. The grid stretches down the page, maintaining consistent column widths and gutters as far as the viewport height allows. The left edge of the grid aligns visually with the inner edge of the product column, and the right edge respects the same generous margin to the central column border, ensuring the grid never feels cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing vertically as more products are listed; it scrolls in sync with the page content while the filter-sidebar may remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each product card in the product-grid, the product-card-add-to-cart-form occupies the lower portion of the card, reinforcing a consistent visual rhythm. The card itself is a vertically oriented rectangle with ample padding around its edges. Product image and title/price information appear in the upper portion, while the add-to-cart-form sits at the bottom, separated from the main content by a modest vertical gap (about 16–24px) or a subtle divider line. The form spans nearly the full width of the card’s inner content area, with small horizontal padding on both sides. Controls such as quantity selector and add-to-cart button are arranged in a simple, linear layout—either in a single horizontal row (quantity on the left, button on the right) or stacked with the button centered—ensuring they are easy to reach but visually quiet. Vertical padding inside the form (8–12px top and bottom) matches the overall component scale. The bottom edges of all add-to-cart sections in a row align horizontally, giving each row of cards a clean, unified baseline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each card; it is anchored to the bottom area of the card’s layout and moves only as part of the card when scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls are placed directly below the product-grid, still within the right-hand product column. They are centered horizontally within this column, mirroring the centered editorial feel of the page header. A large vertical gap (approximately 48–64px) separates the last row of product cards from the pagination, clearly framing it as a distinct control area rather than part of the grid. The pagination elements (previous/next, page numbers) are laid out in a single horizontal line with generous spacing between clickable numbers (12–16px minimum), using fine, understated typography. The overall width of the pagination strip is relatively compact compared to the product column, leaving ample white space on both sides. A small amount of space (24–32px) is reserved below the pagination before the page ends or any footer begins, maintaining the lightness of the composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and positioned at the end of the main product content; it scrolls into view after the grid and does not remain fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-link lives in the global navigation at the very top of the page, aligned to the far right within the fixed-width central column that spans the header. While the nav itself is slim with minimal vertical height, the cart-link is given additional visual emphasis through uppercase or slightly bolder text and optional blush accent details (such as an underline or icon). It is horizontally aligned with any left-side branding or nav items, maintaining a clean top baseline. There is comfortable horizontal padding around the link (about 16–24px), but the overall header remains visually light with plenty of whitespace above and below relative to the rest of the page. The header content, including the cart-link, is centered within the viewport with wide left and right margins, echoing the editorial layout below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Typically part of a header that may be fixed at the top of the viewport on scroll; the cart-link remains visible as the user scrolls through the content, maintaining quick access to the cart.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-summary-bar is positioned directly below the shop-page-controller’s divider, acting as a delicate bridge between the page header and the main two-column browsing area. It spans most of the central content column, with slight insets on the left and right (e.g., occupying about 80–90% of the central column width), and is horizontally centered. The bar has a low visual height, with compact vertical padding (8–12px), and uses fine typography and light chip-like elements to summarize active filters (e.g., small pill-shaped tags). These filter tags flow horizontally from left to right and wrap to a second line only when necessary, maintaining plenty of surrounding white space. There is generous vertical spacing (around 24–32px) above the bar from the divider and a similar gap below it before the main content band begins. Visually, it remains understated so as not to compete with the page title but clear enough to signal the current filter state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, the filter-summary-bar can become sticky just below the header and page title area: as the user scrolls past the initial hero spacing, the bar pins near the top of the viewport (beneath any fixed navigation), maintaining visibility of filter state while the product area and filter-sidebar scroll beneath it.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected retail product and allows the user to add it to the cart, with emphasis on in-store pickup when available.

core_features: Read the productId from the URL and fetch full product information using getProductDetails(productId).
Display key product data including name, category, description, price, rating, rating count, and main image.
Display extended product information returned by getProductDetails (such as ingredients and usage instructions) when available.
Display fulfillment options based on Product.fulfillment_options and is_free_in_store_pickup, emphasizing in_store_pickup when present.
Allow the user to select a quantity and add the product to the current cart using addProductToCart(productId, quantity).
Show feedback after adding to cart (success or error) using the response of addProductToCart, and provide a 'View Cart' navigation action.
Provide a 'Back to Products' link that returns the user to the product listing (shop.html), optionally preserving any upstream list context via URL parameters.
Handle loading and error states when the productId is missing or getProductDetails fails.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – View a product detail from a product listing:
1) User clicks a product in shop.html, index.html, or offers.html; that link navigates to product_detail.html with ?productId={id} and optional context parameters.
2) Product Detail page reads productId from the URL and calls getProductDetails(productId).
3) While loading, the page shows a loading state and disables add-to-cart actions.
4) On success, the page displays the product’s name, description, price, rating, image, and fulfillment options.
5) User can review ingredients and usage instructions (if provided by getProductDetails) to confirm suitability.

Workflow 2 – Add a product to the cart:
1) On the Product Detail page, user reviews the product details.
2) User sets the desired quantity in the quantity input of the Add to Cart form (defaulting to the minimum allowed, typically 1).
3) User clicks the 'Add to Cart' button, which triggers addProductToCart(productId, quantity).
4) While the request is processing, the Add to Cart button is disabled and a submitting state is reflected.
5) On success (success = true), the page stores the returned cart_item_id in local page state, shows a success message from the response message property, and displays a 'View Cart' link.
6) On error (success = false), the page displays the response message as an error and allows the user to retry.

Workflow 3 – Go to the cart after adding a product:
1) After a successful addProductToCart call, the success feedback area displays a 'View Cart' action.
2) User clicks 'View Cart'; the page navigates to cart.html without additional required parameters.
3) The cart page then shows the updated cart, including the item identified by cart_item_id, using its own logic (outside of this page).

Workflow 4 – Return to the product listing:
1) User reviews the product and decides to continue browsing.
2) User clicks 'Back to Products' on the Product Detail page.
3) If the Product Detail URL includes optional context parameters (e.g., fromPage and returnQuery), the click navigates to that context (e.g., shop.html with the provided query string) to restore the filtered list.
4) If no context parameters are present, the click navigates to a default listing page such as shop.html.

Workflow 5 – Handle missing or invalid productId:
1) User lands on product_detail.html without a productId or with a malformed productId.
2) The page controller detects that productId is missing or invalid before calling getProductDetails.
3) The page sets an error state indicating that the product cannot be displayed and disables the Add to Cart form.
4) The error panel shows a generic message and a 'Back to Products' action that sends the user to shop.html so they can pick a product again.
interactions:
Page load with productId:
- Trigger: Browser loads product_detail.html with a valid ?productId={id}.
- Behavior: Page controller parses the URL; if productId is present, it calls getProductDetails(productId). It sets a loading state to true until the API returns. On success, it stores the Product object in state and sets loading to false. On error, it records the error and sets an error state.

Page load without productId or with invalid productId:
- Trigger: Browser loads product_detail.html without productId or with an invalid value.
- Behavior: Page controller skips the getProductDetails call, sets an error state indicating the product cannot be displayed, and shows the error panel and 'Back to Products' link. Add-to-cart interactions remain disabled.

Quantity change in Add to Cart form:
- Trigger: User changes the quantity field value.
- Behavior: Component validates that the quantity is a positive integer within allowed constraints. If invalid, it shows inline validation feedback and disables the Add to Cart submit action until corrected. The validated quantity value is stored in local component state.

Click 'Add to Cart':
- Trigger: User clicks the Add to Cart button on the form.
- Preconditions: Product details have successfully loaded; quantity is valid; no add-to-cart request is currently in progress.
- Behavior: The handler calls addProductToCart(productId, quantity). While awaiting the response, it sets a submitting state (disabling the button). On response:
  - If success = true, store cart_item_id in page state, clear any previous error, show a success notification with message, and reveal the 'View Cart' link.
  - If success = false, display the returned message as an error notification and keep the product details visible for user adjustment.

Click 'View Cart':
- Trigger: User clicks 'View Cart' after a successful add-to-cart operation.
- Behavior: Browser navigates directly to cart.html. No additional API calls are made from the Product Detail page.

Click 'Back to Products':
- Trigger: User clicks 'Back to Products' link at any time.
- Behavior: If optional context parameters (e.g., fromPage and returnQuery) were provided in the Product Detail URL, the link reconstructs the original product listing URL (such as shop.html plus returnQuery) and navigates there. If no context is available, the link navigates to a default listing page (shop.html).

Fulfillment options display behavior:
- Trigger: Product data is successfully loaded.
- Behavior: Fulfillment options component reads Product.fulfillment_options and is_free_in_store_pickup. If 'in_store_pickup' is included, it prominently marks in-store pickup as available and indicates if it is free according to is_free_in_store_pickup. It also displays any other fulfillment modes from the list without making assumptions beyond the data provided.

Rating summary display behavior:
- Trigger: Product data is successfully loaded.
- Behavior: Rating component reads Product.rating and Product.rating_count. If rating is present, it renders a rating summary. If rating_count is present, it shows the number of ratings. If rating is absent, it omits the rating display without substituting static text.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver for this page is the URL parameter productId. On every page load or navigation event that changes productId (including browser back/forward), the page controller reads the current productId from the query string. If productId is present and syntactically valid, the page invokes getProductDetails(productId) and updates the internal page state with the returned Product object and derived flags such as loading, error, and hasFulfillmentOptions. If productId is missing or invalid, the page enters an error state and does not call getProductDetails.

The page may optionally read additional context parameters (for example fromPage and returnQuery) if they are present in the URL. These are used only to construct the target URL for the 'Back to Products' link and do not affect calls to getProductDetails or addProductToCart.

Internal UI state (such as loading, submitting, current quantity, lastAddToCartStatus, and lastAddedCartItemId) is maintained only in memory for the current page instance and is not persisted via URL parameters or localStorage. The URL is not modified by the page except through normal navigation actions triggered by user clicks (e.g., navigating to cart.html or shop.html). All data about the product itself comes exclusively from getProductDetails(productId), and all changes to the cart are initiated via addProductToCart(productId, quantity).

components.functionality: Coordinates reading URL parameters, fetching product data via getProductDetails, managing loading and error states, and providing the Product object and contextual navigation data to child components.
Displays the primary product image based on Product.image_url and supports basic image-state handling (e.g., showing a text-based placeholder state when no image_url is provided).
Shows high-level product information including Product.name, Product.category_key, Product.price, and an integrated rating summary when available.
Reads Product.rating and Product.rating_count from the loaded Product object and renders a rating summary only when rating data exists, without inferring or hardcoding rating values.

Displays detailed product information including Product.description and any extended fields returned by getProductDetails (such as ingredients and usage instructions), without assuming the presence of specific optional fields.

Reads Product.fulfillment_options and Product.is_free_in_store_pickup to list all available fulfillment modes and highlight in_store_pickup when present, indicating whether in-store pickup is free based on the provided boolean.

Allows the user to specify a quantity for the current Product and submit an add-to-cart request via addProductToCart(productId, quantity), managing validation and button disabled states during submission.

Displays success or error feedback after an addProductToCart call using the returned success flag and message, stores the returned cart_item_id in local state on success, and renders an accessible 'View Cart' action when appropriate.

Provides a 'Back to Products' link that uses any optional context URL parameters (such as fromPage and returnQuery) when present to reconstruct the originating product list URL, falling back to shop.html when context is not available.
Shows a loading state whenever the page controller is waiting for getProductDetails(productId) to resolve, and hides itself once loading completes or fails.
Displays an error message when productId is missing or when getProductDetails(productId) fails, and provides a 'Back to Products' action via the back-navigation-links component.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strictly top-to-bottom, centered composition anchored by a fixed-width central column. At the top of this column sit the back-navigation links, followed by generous whitespace and then a large, centered product title and rating cluster that echoes the mockup’s hero heading. Below this title block, the layout transitions into a calm, two-column product body band inside the same central column: the product image gallery occupies the left side, while the fulfillment options and add-to-cart form stack vertically on the right. Beneath this band, the product description returns to a single-column, full-width block within the central column. Status layers (notification banner, loading indicator, error panel) appear over or just above this primary flow without breaking the underlying linear structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strictly top-to-bottom, centered composition anchored by a fixed-width central column. At the top of this column sit the back-navigation links, followed by generous whitespace and then a large, centered product title and rating cluster that echoes the mockup’s hero heading. Below this title block, the layout transitions into a calm, two-column product body band inside the same central column: the product image gallery occupies the left side, while the fulfillment options and add-to-cart form stack vertically on the right. Beneath this band, the product description returns to a single-column, full-width block within the central column. Status layers (notification banner, loading indicator, error panel) appear over or just above this primary flow without breaking the underlying linear structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page reserves the upper ~40–50% of the initial viewport for whitespace, back-navigation, the centered title, rating, and a subtle divider, mimicking the mockup’s light upper half. The product body band (image + purchase controls) fills the middle portion, but with ample lateral and vertical breathing room between elements. Side margins are substantial; the central content column spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport, centered, with the image and right-hand panel sharing that width in a loose 55/45 split. Vertical separations between major sections (back-links to title, title to image band, image band to description) sit in the 64–96px range, while inner spacing between stacked components in the right-hand column stays around 24–32px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is deliberately low. Each major component is treated as a distinct block with generous whitespace around it. The title, rating, and divider sit in a largely empty upper area, giving them a premium editorial feel. The image gallery and the right-hand purchase controls are visually substantial but not crowded; controls use comfortable padding and avoid tight stacking. The description appears as a spacious text block with ample line height and paragraph spacing rather than dense copy. Notifications and error states are slim and unobtrusive, appearing as refined overlays or narrow bands rather than heavy panels.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first catches the back-navigation at the very top and then is pulled downward into the large, centered product title in a serif style with a soft blush accent. Immediately below, the rating summary and a thin divider line signal the shift from introduction to detailed content. The gaze then drops into the product body band and begins a left–right scan: first the prominent image gallery on the left, then across to the fulfillment options and add-to-cart controls on the right, moving downward within that right-hand stack. After scan-completing the purchase controls, the eye continues down to the product description, which reads in a relaxed, editorial flow. Any add-to-cart feedback appears as a slim banner near the top of the content column, momentarily intercepting the eye without disrupting the overall vertical reading path. Loading and error states temporarily dominate the central column but remain centered and minimal.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a refined, boutique product detail experience built around a narrow, centered content column floating in generous white space. At the very top of this column, understated back-navigation links sit left-aligned, with a notable cushion of whitespace below. The central area of the viewport is dominated by a large, elegant product title in a fine serif, centered on the page, supported closely by a compact rating summary and a delicate horizontal divider in a muted blush tone. Beneath this quiet hero block, the layout opens into a horizontally arranged band: on the left, a tall, spacious product image gallery anchors the page; on the right, a vertical stack of fulfillment options and add-to-cart controls creates a calm shopping panel, aligned to the right edge of the central column. The spacing between these elements is generous, ensuring no sense of crowding. Below this band, the layout collapses back into a single central column where the product description flows like editorial copy, with comfortable line length and airy line spacing. Lightweight overlays for loading or errors appear centered within the same axis, slightly dimming the background but preserving the sense of calm. The result is a high-end, minimal product page that feels more like a curated catalog spread than a typical e-commerce template.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the overall canvas and central content column. It sets a full-viewport-width background in white, with the main content constrained to a centered column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width (for example, a max-width of about 1040px) and is aligned to the vertical center axis of the page. All other components stack within this column. The top of the content column begins just below the global navigation (if present), with a generous top margin of approximately 96px before the first in-page element (the back-navigation links). Vertical spacing between major sections (navigation links, hero title block, product body band, description) follows a consistent rhythm in the 64–96px range, reinforcing the airy, editorial feel. The controller also manages layering so that notification banners, loading indicators, and error panels appear within or over this central column without breaking its centered composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the fixed structural frame for the page content. The central column is responsive but always centered, with consistent outer gutters. It scrolls normally with the page; overlay elements (loading indicator, error panel when modal-like) are positioned relative to this controller, often centered within the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product image gallery sits in the lower half of the initial viewport, forming the left side of the main product body band. It begins approximately 64–80px below the thin divider line under the product title and rating. Within the central column, the gallery occupies roughly 55% of the column’s width and is left-aligned, leaving a wide gutter between it and the right-hand purchase panel. The primary product image is displayed as a tall, vertically oriented tile, with ample whitespace surrounding it at the top and sides. Thumbnails or alternate views, if present, are arranged either in a slim column to the left or in a single row below the main image, using modest spacing (8–16px) to maintain the minimal aesthetic. The gallery’s vertical height is sufficient to visually anchor the page but stops short of dominating the entire viewport, allowing the right-hand column to be fully visible on most desktop screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column. On wider screens, it remains in a two-column relationship with the right-hand panel. On narrower screens (if responsive behavior is considered), it would naturally stack above the right-hand panel while maintaining generous top and bottom margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product detail header, containing the product name (and optionally key details like size or variant), is the visual hero of the page. It is positioned centrally in the upper half of the viewport, approximately 64–80px below the back-navigation links. The header spans the full width of the central column but the text is centered, creating a broad, calm block of whitespace on either side. The product name is set in a large, fine serif style, occupying roughly one to three lines at most and visually consuming about 20–30% of the central column’s height in this region. There is generous whitespace above and below the product name, with a margin of around 24–32px separating it from the rating summary below. A thin horizontal rule in the blush accent runs beneath the rating (or directly beneath the title when rating is absent), stretching across about 40–50% of the column’s width and centered, reinforcing the sense of a distinct title section before the more detailed content begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored to the central column and scrolls out of view as the user moves down. It establishes the primary visual axis, with other elements aligning relative to its center.
