component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rating summary is positioned immediately below the product detail header within the same centered hero block. It occupies a narrow horizontal band, centered within the central column and spanning roughly 25–40% of the column’s width. Visually, it sits about 24px below the product name, with a compact arrangement of star icons, rating value, and review count. The text size is restrained, clearly subordinate to the title but still legible and visible at a glance. Approximately 16–24px below the rating summary, a thin blush-toned divider line stretches horizontally across the middle portion of the column, visually separating the hero information from the product body content below. The overall effect is a small, refined cluster that reinforces trust without overwhelming the serene heading area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and center-aligned, moving with the scroll. It remains visually tied to the product title and does not behave independently (no stickiness).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product description section sits below the main product body band (image gallery on the left, purchase controls on the right). It begins approximately 64–96px beneath the bottom edge of the right-hand add-to-cart form, ensuring a clear sectional break. The description spans the full width of the central column and is left-aligned, returning the layout to a single-column editorial feel. The heading for this section (if present) is modest and aligned with the left edge of the column, with about 16–24px separating it from the body text. The copy itself uses comfortable line lengths (roughly 60–70 characters per line) and generous line height, with paragraph spacing of 16–24px to keep the text light and readable. Bullet points or additional details, if included, are spaced with similar care to avoid any sense of clutter, making this section feel like a calm, magazine-style product write-up.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked below the product body band within the central column. It extends vertically as needed but maintains consistent left and right margins aligned with all other content in the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fulfillment options panel is located in the upper portion of the right-hand column of the product body band, directly opposite the product image gallery. The top edge of this panel aligns vertically with the top of the image gallery, creating a balanced horizontal band across the page. Within the central column, this right-hand panel occupies roughly 45% of the width and is right-aligned, leaving a generous central gutter between it and the image gallery. The panel itself feels like a refined, lightly outlined or subtly shaded card, with internal padding in the 24–32px range. Information about in-store pickup, shipping, and availability is stacked with clear typographic hierarchy and ample vertical spacing (12–16px between lines/groups). In-store pickup details are emphasized near the top of the panel, perhaps with a blush accent subheading or label, ensuring that this option is noticed quickly as part of the purchase path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and grouped visually with the add-to-cart form as part of a unified purchase column. As the page scrolls, it moves normally; it is not sticky. On narrower layouts, it would stack above the add-to-cart form and potentially above or below the image gallery depending on responsive rules.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart form sits directly beneath the fulfillment options panel in the right-hand column, separated by a comfortable vertical gap of about 24–32px. It shares the same width as the fulfillment panel (about 45% of the central column, right-aligned), creating a clean vertical stack of purchase-related components. The form elements (quantity selector, variant selector if present, and the main add-to-cart button) are arranged in a linear vertical flow with generous spacing—approximately 16–24px between controls. The primary add-to-cart button spans nearly the full width of this panel, with substantial padding (around 12px vertical and 24px horizontal) and minimal styling, possibly using the blush accent as a subtle border or fill. Below the button, any supporting messages (like stock status or small print) are displayed in reduced text size with at least 12–16px of separation to avoid visual congestion. The bottom of the form leaves a margin of 32–48px before the next major section (description) begins, reinforcing its importance as a distinct interaction area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and vertically stacked under the fulfillment panel, moving naturally with scroll. It is not fixed or sticky, in keeping with the calm, editorial feel. On smaller screens, it would stack beneath fulfillment options and potentially beneath the image gallery while maintaining its generous padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart feedback component appears as a slim, refined notification banner within the central content column, near the top of the page. When visible, it sits just below the global navigation and above the back-navigation links, spanning the full width of the central column. The banner’s height is modest—approximately 40–56px—and it uses a light blush background or subtle border to gently draw attention without disrupting the serene layout. Text is center-aligned within the banner, with comfortable horizontal padding (16–24px) and plenty of whitespace above and below relative to neighboring content. When shown, it pushes the rest of the content downward, preserving the vertical rhythm and avoiding overlap with the back-navigation links or title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically in the normal document flow within the central column. It is not fixed to the viewport; instead, it scrolls away with the content. In some implementations it could briefly slide down from below the nav and then collapse, but always within the bounds of the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-navigation links occupy a thin horizontal band at the very top of the main content column, directly below any global navigation. They are left-aligned within the central column and span only as wide as their text content (for example, a simple “Back to products” link or breadcrumb), leaving generous empty space to the right. There is a substantial top margin (approximately 64–96px) between the bottom of the global nav and these links, and a smaller but still airy gap (24–32px) between the links and the start of the product title header below. The styling is understated—small caps or light-weight text—so they serve as an orienting element without competing with the hero title that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, at the top of the central column’s content flow. They scroll normally and are not sticky. On smaller screens, they remain left-aligned but retain the same generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-state indicator appears as an unobtrusive overlay centered within the viewport, aligned to the vertical axis of the central column. When active, it dims or softly blurs the underlying page content with a translucent white overlay, maintaining the light, minimal aesthetic. At the center of the screen, a simple, elegant spinner or progress glyph is displayed, possibly accompanied by a single line of small, muted text. The indicator occupies a compact central area (roughly a 120–160px square visual footprint) while its overlay covers the content region to signal that interactions are temporarily paused. The surrounding whitespace and the softness of the overlay prevent it from feeling heavy or disruptive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed, full-page (or full-content-area) overlay tied to the page controller. It remains centered in the viewport as the user scrolls (scroll may optionally be locked), and disappears once loading is complete, revealing the underlying central column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product error panel appears within the central content column, replacing or overlaying the main product content when an error occurs (for example, the product cannot be loaded). It is positioned slightly below the area where the product title would normally appear—roughly 96–128px from the top of the central column—so that it aligns with the established visual center. The panel spans about 60–70% of the column’s width and is centered, with ample whitespace around it to maintain the premium feel. Inside, a concise error heading and message are stacked with 16–24px spacing, optionally accompanied by a subtle blush accent icon. Additional actions (like a retry button or link back to the product list) are placed beneath the text with generous padding. The panel’s borders and background are kept minimal—perhaps a fine outline or very light tint—so the treatment feels calm and composed rather than alarming.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable central column if used as an inline replacement for content, or centered within a light overlay if used as a modal-like interruption. In both cases it remains aligned to the central axis and does not move independently of the page controller. When present, it either pushes or obscures the usual product detail components to clearly indicate an error state.

name: Cart & Booking Summary
title: Cart & Booking Summary
description: View and manage all appointments, packages, custom sessions, products, and gift cards in a single cart before proceeding to checkout.

core_features: Load the current cart and booking summary using getCartSummary, including all cart items, appointments, products, gift cards, totals, and applied promotions.
List all appointment-type items (single services, packages, custom sessions) with service or package name, date, time, duration, and technician information when available.
List all product and gift card items with names, configurable quantities, unit prices, and line totals.
Allow users to remove any cart item (appointment, product, or gift card) via removeCartItem and update the summary accordingly.
Allow users to adjust the quantity of product and gift card cart items via updateCartItemQuantity and reflect updated line totals and cart totals.
Allow users to request schedule adjustments for appointments via updateAppointmentSchedule (e.g., changing appointment start date/time while keeping or updating the technician when possible).
Provide a promo code input that can be pre-filled from the promoCode URL parameter, and apply the code via applyPromoCode with clear success or error feedback.
Display all currently applied promotions (from getCartSummary and applyPromoCode) with their discount amounts and descriptions, and show their impact on the cart totals.
Display summarized monetary information for the cart (subtotal, tax if provided, total) based on values from getCartSummary.

Provide navigation controls to continue shopping (using the origin URL parameter and/or last cart item type) and to proceed to checkout.html, as well as a link to offers.html near the promo code field.
user_workflows: Viewing the cart and booking summary: 1) User navigates to cart.html optionally with promoCode and origin URL parameters. 2) On page load, the page parses URL parameters and calls getCartSummary. 3) The page renders all cart items, grouped or labeled by type (appointments, products, gift cards), and shows subtotal, tax, total, and any applied promotions. 4) The promo code field is pre-filled with the promoCode URL parameter if present.

Removing an item from the cart: 1) User clicks a remove action on a specific cart item row. 2) The page calls removeCartItem with the selected CartItem.id. 3) On success, the page calls getCartSummary again (or updates local cart state based on the response pattern) to refresh items and totals. 4) If the last item is removed, the page displays the empty-cart state and a Continue Shopping action determined by origin or item types.

Updating product or gift card quantity: 1) User changes the quantity in a quantity control for a product or gift card cart item. 2) The page validates the input (e.g., minimum of 1) on the client; if valid, it calls updateCartItemQuantity with the CartItem.id and new quantity. 3) On success, the page refreshes cart data using getCartSummary or updates the affected line and totals. 4) If the update fails, the previous quantity is restored and an error message is shown.

Adjusting an appointment schedule inline: 1) User clicks a control such as 'Change time' in an appointment item. 2) A date/time selector is displayed for that appointment; the user picks a new start date/time and optionally a technician identifier if such selection is supported in the UI. 3) The page calls updateAppointmentSchedule with appointmentId, newStartDateTime, and technicianId (either unchanged from the current appointment or updated based on the selector). 4) On success, the appointment item’s displayed date/time (and technician if changed) are updated or the page re-calls getCartSummary to refresh appointment data.

Rescheduling an appointment via the booking page: 1) In a single-service appointment item, the user clicks a 'Reschedule' action. 2) The page navigates to booking.html with URL parameters appointmentType=single_service, serviceId equal to the appointment’s service_id, and origin=cart. 3) The user completes rescheduling in the booking flow. 4) When returning to cart.html (e.g., via a header link or post-booking redirect with origin=cart), getCartSummary is called again and shows the updated appointment time.

Applying a promo code manually or from offers: 1) User enters a promo code in the promo field (or arrives on cart.html with a promoCode URL parameter pre-filling the field). 2) User submits the promo form, which calls applyPromoCode with the entered promoCode. 3) If applyPromoCode returns success, the page either updates the applied promotions list using the returned applied_promotion and then refreshes the cart via getCartSummary, or directly relies on the backend-updated cart in the response if that is part of the API contract. 4) The discount is reflected in the applied promotions section and totals. 5) If the promo code is invalid or not applicable, the page shows the returned message and leaves the cart totals unchanged.

Exploring offers and returning to the cart: 1) Near the promo input, user clicks a 'View available offers' link. 2) The page navigates to offers.html with URL parameters that can include origin=cart and possibly the current promo code, so that offers.html can pre-filter or highlight relevant promotions. 3) When the user selects an offer on offers.html, that page navigates back to cart.html with a promoCode URL parameter. 4) On load, cart.html pre-fills the promo code field with this value and the user can apply it via the promo form.

Continuing shopping from the cart: 1) User clicks 'Continue Shopping'. 2) The page determines the navigation target: if an origin URL parameter is present, it maps this to an appropriate page (for example, shop.html when origin indicates a product context, or booking.html when origin indicates a service/booking context). If origin is not present, the page inspects the current cart_items from getCartSummary; when the most recently added items are products, the page navigates to shop.html as specified; when they are appointments, the page navigates to booking.html; other mappings can be defined consistently with the site architecture. 3) The user continues browsing and can later return to cart.html via the global header cart link.

Proceeding to checkout: 1) After reviewing items and applied promotions, the user clicks 'Proceed to Checkout'. 2) The page performs any final client-side validation (e.g., that the cart is not empty). 3) The page navigates to checkout.html, optionally including origin=cart or other non-sensitive URL parameters that may help pre-configure the checkout flow. 4) The checkout page uses its own interfaces to complete payment and confirmation.
interactions: Page initialization: On load of cart.html, parse promoCode and origin from the URL query string, then call getCartSummary. Bind the returned cart, cart_items, and applied_promotions to page state used by all other components.

Cart item rendering: For each entry in getCartSummary.cart_items, render a row or block that displays data based on item_type. For appointments, show appointment_type, associated service/session/package name, start_datetime, duration_minutes, price, technician information when available, and any notes. For products, show Product.name, price, rating if provided, quantity, and line_total. For gift cards, show GiftCardTemplate.name, configured amount and recipient details from GiftCardPurchase, quantity, and line_total.

Remove item interaction: Each cart item row includes a remove trigger (button or link). On click, confirm the action if desired, then call removeCartItem with cartItemId equal to the row’s CartItem.id. On success, call getCartSummary to refresh cart items and totals, and update the empty-state view if no items remain.

Quantity change interaction: Product and gift-card rows include a quantity input or stepper control bound to CartItem.quantity. When the user changes quantity and confirms (e.g., blur or explicit 'Update' action), validate the new quantity, then call updateCartItemQuantity with the corresponding cartItemId and quantity. On success, refresh via getCartSummary or update line_total and totals based on response behavior. On failure, revert to the previous quantity and show the error message from the API.

Inline appointment schedule change interaction: Appointment rows can include a 'Change time' control that opens an inline panel with a date/time selector, pre-populated with appointment.start_datetime. When the user picks a new start time (and optionally a technician identifier if offered), the component calls updateAppointmentSchedule with appointmentId, newStartDateTime, and technicianId. While waiting for the response, the appointment row can be marked as 'updating'. On success, either update that appointment’s fields from the returned Appointment object or call getCartSummary again; on failure, restore previous display values and show the error message.

Reschedule via booking interaction: Single-service appointment rows include a 'Reschedule' link. On click, the page navigates to booking.html with query parameters appointmentType=single_service, serviceId equal to Appointment.service_id, and origin=cart, without calling any cart interface at that moment.

Promo code input interaction: The promo input field is bound to an internal promoCode state. If the URL contains a promoCode parameter, the field is initialized with that value. When the user submits the promo form, the component calls applyPromoCode with the current promoCode value. Based on the response, it either updates the applied promotions and totals (calling getCartSummary if needed) or shows an error message from the API without changing the cart.

Applied promotions display interaction: The page displays the applied_promotions array from getCartSummary, as well as any newly applied_promotion from applyPromoCode after it is persisted to the cart. Each promotion shows its promo_code, discount_amount, and optional conditions_text or description. This section updates whenever cart data is refreshed.

Totals display interaction: The totals component reads cart.subtotal, cart.tax (if present), and cart.total from getCartSummary and recomputes the displayed breakdown whenever the cart state changes due to item removal, quantity updates, or promotions. The component does not compute monetary values itself beyond simple formatting; it relies on API-provided numbers.

Continue shopping navigation interaction: The 'Continue Shopping' control reads the origin URL parameter if present and maps it to a destination URL. When origin is absent, it derives a default destination from getCartSummary.cart_items (for example, shop.html when the most recent items are products, booking.html when they are appointments). Clicking the control triggers navigation to the computed URL with any necessary query parameters (for example, origin=cart for analytics).

Proceed to checkout navigation interaction: The 'Proceed to Checkout' control checks that there is at least one item in getCartSummary.cart_items. If the cart is not empty, clicking this control navigates to checkout.html, optionally appending origin=cart in the URL parameters. No cart modification APIs are called at this point from this page.

View offers navigation interaction: A link near the promo code field navigates to offers.html. It can pass origin=cart and the current promo input value as a promoCode URL parameter so that offers.html can highlight or validate it. The user can then choose an offer that sends them back to cart.html with an updated promoCode parameter.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL parameters and API responses, without using localStorage. On initial load of cart.html, the script parses the query string for promoCode and origin. The promoCode parameter, if present, is used to pre-fill the promo input component but the promo code is applied only when the user submits the promo form via applyPromoCode, avoiding implicit changes to the cart without user action. The origin parameter is stored in in-memory state during the page session and is used in two main scenarios: 1) choosing the destination for the 'Continue Shopping' control by mapping known origin values (such as those corresponding to service_detail.html, booking.html, shop.html, product_detail.html, gift_card_detail.html) to appropriate URLs (e.g., shop.html or booking.html) and appending origin=cart when returning to this page from others; 2) optionally tagging navigations to offers.html and checkout.html so downstream pages know the user came from cart.html. All cart content (items, appointments, products, gift cards, subtotal, tax, total, and applied promotions) is obtained exclusively via getCartSummary and is treated as the single source of truth. After any mutating action (applyPromoCode, removeCartItem, updateCartItemQuantity, updateAppointmentSchedule) completes successfully, the page refreshes its state by calling getCartSummary or by merging the known changes into the existing state according to the API response contract. The URL can be updated (for example, modifying the promoCode parameter when a new code is applied) using query string manipulation so that a page reload preserves the entered promo code and origin, but the cart contents themselves are not persisted in the URL. When the cart becomes empty, the page shows an empty-state view and the Continue Shopping control uses origin or a default mapping (e.g., shop.html) to guide the user back into the browsing or booking flows.

components.functionality: Initializes the Cart & Booking Summary page by parsing URL parameters, calling getCartSummary, and populating shared state for cart data, applied promotions, promo input value, and origin.
event_handlers: onInit: parse URL parameters promoCode and origin and store them in page state
onInit: call getCartSummary and bind response.cart, response.cart_items, and response.applied_promotions to state
onUrlChange: if promoCode or origin in the query string changes due to navigation, update the corresponding state and, if needed, re-render dependent components

components.functionality: Displays all cart items returned by getCartSummary, grouped or labeled by type (appointments, products, gift cards). Provides per-item controls for removal, quantity changes (where applicable), and access to scheduling interactions or rescheduling links.
event_handlers: onRender: iterate over getCartSummary.cart_items and create item rows based on item_type and related entities
onClickRemove(cartItemId): call removeCartItem with cartItemId, then on success trigger cart-page-init to refresh getCartSummary data
onChangeQuantity(cartItemId, newQuantity): validate newQuantity, then call updateCartItemQuantity with cartItemId and newQuantity, then on success trigger cart-page-init to refresh data
onClickRescheduleSingleService(appointmentId, serviceId): navigate to booking.html with URL parameters appointmentType=single_service, serviceId, and origin=cart without calling an API
onClickExpandAppointmentDetails(appointmentId): toggle display of additional session or package details using already-bound session or package data from getCartSummary

components.functionality: Provides inline controls within appointment cart items to request basic schedule adjustments using updateAppointmentSchedule, such as changing the start date and time while optionally preserving or updating technician assignment.
event_handlers: onClickChangeTime(appointmentId): open a date/time selector initialized with the appointment.start_datetime from state

onSubmitScheduleChange(appointmentId, newStartDateTime, technicianId): call updateAppointmentSchedule with appointmentId, newStartDateTime, and technicianId (which may be unchanged from the current appointment); on success, update the local Appointment fields or trigger cart-page-init to refresh getCartSummary
onCancelScheduleChange(appointmentId): close the inline editor and revert any unsaved selections

components.functionality: Manages promo code input, including pre-filling from the promoCode URL parameter and applying the code to the current cart via applyPromoCode. Handles success and error messaging and coordinates refreshing cart data.
event_handlers: onInit: read promoCode from page state (set by cart-page-init from URL parameters) and set as the input’s initial value
onChangePromoInput(value): update internal promoCode state with the user-entered value

onSubmitPromo(): call applyPromoCode with the current promoCode state; on success, optionally merge the returned applied_promotion into state and call getCartSummary to refresh cart and applied_promotions; on failure, display the returned message without changing cart data
onPromoAppliedSuccessfully(applied_promotion): update the applied-promotions-list component with the new promotion details

components.functionality: Displays all promotions currently applied to the cart, including their codes, descriptions when available, and discount amounts, based on data from getCartSummary and successful applyPromoCode calls.
event_handlers: onCartDataChange: when cart-page-init refreshes getCartSummary, render the list of applied_promotions associated with the cart
onNewPromotionApplied(applied_promotion): insert or update the displayed promotion entry according to the response from applyPromoCode

components.functionality: Shows cart-level monetary summary including subtotal, tax if provided, and total, reflecting the latest state of the cart after item changes and promotions.
event_handlers: onCartDataChange: read subtotal, tax, and total from the Cart object returned by getCartSummary and render them in the totals box
onPromotionsChange: when applied_promotions changes, ensure that the totals box reflects the updated Cart.total from the latest getCartSummary response

components.functionality: Determines and executes the appropriate 'Continue Shopping' destination based on the origin URL parameter when available, or by inspecting the types of items currently in the cart when origin is not set.
event_handlers: onInit: read origin from page state (set by cart-page-init from URL parameters)

onClickContinueShopping(): determine target URL by first checking origin; if origin maps to a known page (e.g., a booking or shop context), navigate there; if origin is absent or unrecognized, inspect getCartSummary.cart_items (e.g., use the type of the most recently added item) to choose a default destination such as shop.html for product-heavy carts or booking.html for appointment-heavy carts; then perform navigation using URL parameters as needed, including origin=cart on the destination for analytics

components.functionality: Provides the main action to move from the Cart & Booking Summary to the checkout process, ensuring the cart is not empty before navigation.
event_handlers: onClickProceedToCheckout(): verify that getCartSummary.cart_items is not empty; if it contains at least one item, navigate to checkout.html, optionally appending origin=cart as a URL parameter; if the cart is empty, do not navigate and instead trigger an inline message indicating that there are no items to check out

components.functionality: Allows users to navigate from the cart directly to the offers page, passing along context such as origin and any currently entered promo code.
event_handlers: onClickViewOffers(currentPromoCode): navigate to offers.html with URL parameters origin=cart and promoCode=currentPromoCode so the offers page can use this information when presenting promotions

components.functionality: Displays a simple empty-cart message and a context-aware Continue Shopping action when there are no items in the cart.
event_handlers: onCartDataChange: if getCartSummary.cart_items is empty, hide the cart-item-list, promo-code-form, and applied-promotions-list components and show the empty-cart-state instead
onClickEmptyStateContinueShopping(): invoke the same routing logic as continue-shopping-control to navigate the user back to a browsing or booking page

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the linear top‑to‑bottom flow from the mockup: global nav at the very top, followed by a generous vertical gap, then a large, centered page title 'CART & BOOKING SUMMARY' with a thin divider beneath. Below the divider, the cart content sits inside a single centered content column that is narrower than the viewport. Within this column, the main cart area is organized into a two‑column grid: the left column hosts the cart items and appointment editor, while the right column hosts totals, promos, and checkout controls. On small screens this collapses back into a single vertical stack, preserving the editorial, low‑density feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the linear top‑to‑bottom flow from the mockup: global nav at the very top, followed by a generous vertical gap, then a large, centered page title 'CART & BOOKING SUMMARY' with a thin divider beneath. Below the divider, the cart content sits inside a single centered content column that is narrower than the viewport. Within this column, the main cart area is organized into a two‑column grid: the left column hosts the cart items and appointment editor, while the right column hosts totals, promos, and checkout controls. On small screens this collapses back into a single vertical stack, preserving the editorial, low‑density feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper half of the first viewport is dominated by whitespace and the centered page title, echoing the WAXING layout. The cart content begins roughly halfway down the viewport. Side margins are wide so the main content column spans about 60–70% of the viewport at desktop. Within the cart section, the left column occupies about 60–65% of the content width (items and schedules), and the right column about 35–40% (totals, promos, primary CTA). Vertical spacing between major sections (title to cart content, cart content sections to footer) uses 64–96px increments, and spacing between related components within a column uses 24–32px, keeping the page airy.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low: no dense tables or cramped lists. Each cart item row has comfortable vertical padding, and blocks (item list, editor, totals, promo form) are visually separated by generous whitespace. The right‑column summary stack is short and clean, with only essential lines. Text sizes and line heights are tuned for readability rather than fitting maximum content, mimicking the nuanced spacing of the three‑column service grid without overloading the user.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the nav down to the large, centered page title, then to the subtle divider, which cues the transition into the functional area. From there, the left‑hand column draws attention first (cart items and schedule controls), since it is wider and begins higher. After reviewing items, the eye naturally shifts right to the totals and checkout button. Within each column, the gaze moves top‑to‑bottom. A persistent site‑wide chat bubble remains at the bottom right, acting as a secondary peripheral focal point without competing with the primary cart and checkout actions.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a calm, editorial summary of your bookings and purchases. At the very top, the existing minimalist nav stretches full width but remains visually light. Beneath it, a tall band of white space leads the eye down to a large, centered serif title—‘CART & BOOKING SUMMARY’—set in the same muted blush accent as the WAXING heading. A fine horizontal rule, aligned to the central content column, sits directly under the title, acting as a quiet threshold into the cart. Below this, the main cart area lives inside a centrally aligned column that occupies about two‑thirds of the viewport width, with generous white margins to either side. This area is divided into a wider left column for the cart item list and inline appointment editor, and a narrower right column for the compact totals box, promo code form, applied promotions, and checkout navigation controls. Each block is separated by ample vertical spacing, so nothing feels crowded; the overall impression is of a curated, boutique summary rather than a busy ecommerce cart. On smaller screens, the two columns collapse into a single vertical stack that maintains the same order and breathing room. If the cart is empty, the two‑column structure is replaced by a single, centered empty‑state message below the title, surrounded by generous whitespace that preserves the high‑end, editorial character.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-page-init loader is not a visible permanent element but controls an overlay within the main content column. When data is loading, a translucent white veil covers the central cart area starting just below the title divider and extending to the end of the cart section, leaving the nav and page title visible. At the center of this veiled area, vertically and horizontally aligned within the content column (not full viewport), a small, refined spinner or minimal ‘Loading your cart…’ line appears in muted gray serif text. This preserves the airy, editorial feel while clearly indicating that the cart content below is in a loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an overlay anchored to the bounds of the main cart content column; appears only during data loading and scrolls with the page if the user is mid‑scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-list sits in the upper left portion of the main cart area, directly below the page title divider and a 64px vertical gap. It forms the top of the left column, occupying roughly 60–65% of the central content column’s width. Its left edge aligns precisely with the left edge of the overall content column and its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the cart-totals-box in the right column, creating a clean two‑column grid. Each cart item row has comfortable vertical padding (around 16–24px) and a subtle bottom divider or large vertical gap (24–32px) between items, echoing the generous spacing of the service menu rows. The list does not extend edge‑to‑edge visually; text and any thumbnails are inset, maintaining ample inner padding so the layout remains light. If many items are present, the list can extend vertically down the page, while the right column content remains visually anchored starting at the top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, expanding vertically as items increase. On smaller screens, it becomes full‑width below the title divider, preceding all other cart components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The appointment-schedule-editor is positioned immediately beneath the cart-item-list in the left column, separated by a 32–48px vertical gap so it feels related but distinct. It spans the same width as the cart-item-list (about 60–65% of the content column) and maintains the same left alignment. Internally, its fields are arranged in a calm, linear layout instead of a busy grid: date/time selectors and session adjustments stack vertically with 16–24px spacing, preserving the low density. A subtle section label like ‘Adjust your schedule’ can sit left‑aligned at the top, aligned optically with the first item name above, reinforcing continuity. As content grows (more appointments to edit), the editor simply extends downward, maintaining generous whitespace above and below within the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, directly following the cart-item-list. On mobile, it appears full‑width beneath the cart-item-list and above all right‑column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promo-code-form is housed in the right column, beneath the cart-totals-box but still above the primary checkout button. It begins roughly 24–32px below the bottom edge of the totals box, maintaining clear separation while remaining part of the same summary stack. It aligns to the right column’s left and right edges, spanning about 35–40% of the content column width. The input field and apply button are laid out horizontally where space allows: the input occupies roughly two‑thirds of the right column width, with the apply button aligned to its right, separated by a small 8–16px gap, evoking the compact pairing style of the service/price layout. The overall height of the form is modest, with 8–12px vertical padding inside, keeping it visually subordinate to the totals box while still clearly actionable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, following the cart-totals-box. On smaller screens, it becomes a full‑width row stacked under the totals box and above the applied-promotions-list, maintaining the same left alignment as other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-promotions-list sits immediately beneath the promo-code-form in the right column, separated by a 16–24px gap. It spans the same width as the promo-code-form and cart-totals-box, keeping the right column visually coherent. Each applied promotion appears as a single line with the promotion name left‑aligned and the discount amount right‑aligned on the same line, echoing the service name/price pairing from the original menu, but in smaller, lighter text. Individual promotions are separated by 8–16px vertical gaps rather than heavy dividers to keep the list visually light. The block as a whole remains compact and does not visually overpower the totals or primary checkout button below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, expanding vertically as more promotions are applied. On mobile, it appears as a full‑width block between the promo-code-form and the cart-totals-box or directly beneath totals, depending on content grouping, but remains grouped with promos and savings.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-totals-box anchors the upper part of the right column and is visually paired with the cart-item-list height on the left. It begins at the same vertical level as the top of the cart-item-list, but in the right 35–40% of the content column. It appears as a clean, lightly bordered or subtly shaded block with generous internal padding (24–32px) and three to five lines of summary text (subtotal, discounts, taxes, total). Each line uses a two‑column inline pattern: label on the left, value aligned to the right, mirroring the service/price layout from the mockup. The final total line uses the blush accent color or a slightly heavier weight to stand out, but maintains the minimalist typography. The box height is modest, and ample white space surrounds it on all sides, especially above the promo-code-form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, forming the top of the summary stack. On larger screens, it can optionally adopt a sticky behavior, remaining visible as the user scrolls through long item lists on the left, but only within the vertical bounds of the main cart section to avoid overlapping the footer. On mobile, it becomes a full‑width block placed below items and schedule, but above checkout actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The continue-shopping-control is positioned beneath the primary checkout button in the right column, separated by a 16–24px gap. It is aligned left with the right column’s content and presented as a light‑weight text button or understated outline button, much less visually dominant than the proceed-to-checkout-button. Its width is minimal—just enough for its label plus comfortable horizontal padding—so it appears as a small, elegant control rather than a competing CTA. It sits closer to the checkout button than to the lower edge of the cart section, visually grouping it as a secondary option related to the checkout decision area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, always following the proceed-to-checkout-button. On mobile, it appears as a full‑width or content‑width control directly under the primary checkout button but styled lightly to indicate secondary importance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The proceed-to-checkout-button sits in the right column directly beneath the applied-promotions-list (or promo-code-form if no promotions are applied), separated by a 24–32px vertical gap. It stretches nearly the full width of the right column, with generous horizontal padding (16–24px) and vertical padding (12–16px), making it the most visually prominent interactive element on the page. The button aligns perfectly with the left and right edges of the cart-totals-box above, forming a strong vertical column of summary and action. Its color uses the blush accent to stand out against the white background, with subtle hover or pressed states. There is ample white space beneath and above this button so it is clearly isolated as the primary next step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column as part of the summary stack. When the totals box is sticky on large screens, the button can optionally be included in that sticky region, so totals and primary action remain visible together while scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-offers-link is positioned subtly near the promo area in the right column, either directly beneath the promo-code-form (if no promotions are applied) or beneath the applied-promotions-list, separated by an 8–16px gap. It is left‑aligned with the right column’s content and styled as small, understated text with a simple underline or thin accent line, not as a full button. It occupies only as much width as its text requires, tucked into the whitespace so it feels like a quiet, optional enhancement rather than a competing call to action. The link visually relates to promotions, encouraging exploration without disrupting the main cart-review-to-checkout flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column’s promo/summary cluster. On mobile, it appears directly under the promo-code-form or applied-promotions-list, retaining its subtle, inline-text character.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the cart is empty, the normal two‑column cart layout is suppressed and replaced by a single, centered empty-cart-state block inside the main content column. This block appears beneath the page title divider with a generous 64–96px vertical gap, occupying at most 50–60% of the content column width and centered horizontally. A simple line icon or minimal illustration sits at the top of this block, followed by a short headline like ‘Your cart is empty’ and a brief, calm description line, all center‑aligned. Below, a single primary action—likely a ‘Browse services’ or ‘Book now’ button—sits centered with generous padding. The surrounding whitespace is ample above, below, and to the sides, mirroring the airy feeling of the original service menu when it has few rows. There is no right column or summary box visible in this state; the page feels like a quiet editorial message inviting the user back into the experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the entire cart content area beneath the title. Static and vertically centered within the scrollable content column; as content is minimal, it typically fits within the first viewport with ample whitespace.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Review your waxing service bookings, gift cards, and product purchases, confirm contact details and notification preferences, and proceed to the payment step.

core_features: Fetch and display a read-only checkout summary (cart, items, appointments, products, gift cards, and applied promotions) using getCheckoutSummary.
Show detailed, non-editable line items for each appointment, package, custom session, product, and gift card in the cart.
Display pricing breakdown including subtotal, tax (if provided), applied promotion discounts, and final total from the Cart and AppliedPromotion data.
Pre-fill and edit customer contact information and notification preferences based on contact_info returned from getCheckoutSummary.
Validate and persist customer contact information and notification preferences via saveCheckoutContactInfo before proceeding to payment.
Provide navigation back to the cart/booking summary and to policy pages using URL parameters (origin, policyType) without modifying cart contents.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Load checkout summary: 1) User arrives on checkout.html (typically from cart.html with optional origin URL parameter). 2) Page initialization triggers getCheckoutSummary. 3) The returned cart, cart_items, applied_promotions, and contact_info populate the summary, totals, and contact form. 4) User reviews all details without being able to edit cart contents here.

Workflow 2 – Review appointments and purchases: 1) After data load, user scans the list of cart items (appointments, packages, custom sessions, products, gift cards). 2) User optionally expands any item to see additional details from related entities (e.g., appointment date/time, technician, package name). 3) If changes are needed, user uses the Back to Cart & Booking Summary navigation to go to cart.html, adjust items there, and can then return to checkout.

Workflow 3 – Enter and save contact details: 1) User reviews the pre-filled contact_info (full_name, email, phone, notification preferences). 2) User edits or completes missing fields. 3) On clicking Continue to Payment, the checkout form validates required fields locally. 4) If valid, the form calls saveCheckoutContactInfo with the updated contactInfo object. 5) If the API response indicates success, the user is allowed to proceed to the next payment-related step (outside this page). 6) If the response indicates failure, an error message is displayed and the user can correct and resubmit.

Workflow 4 – Navigate back to cart and policies: 1) User can click Back to Cart & Booking Summary, which reads the origin URL parameter and navigates to cart.html while preserving any necessary context via query parameters. 2) User can click Terms & Conditions or Privacy Policy links, which open policies.html with policyType set appropriately (e.g., policyType=terms) without affecting the current checkout state. 3) User can return from policies.html to checkout.html via browser navigation or a link, and the page will re-run getCheckoutSummary to restore the latest server-side state.
interactions: On page load of checkout.html, the application reads the origin URL parameter (if present) and immediately calls getCheckoutSummary; the results populate in-memory state for cart, cart_items, applied_promotions, and contact_info.

User scrolls through the read-only item list; clicking an individual line item toggles a details view showing additional data from related entities (Appointment, Service, Session, Package, Product, GiftCardPurchase, GiftCardTemplate) without allowing quantity or content changes.

User can click a Back to Cart & Booking Summary control; this reads the origin parameter to decide how to construct the cart.html URL (e.g., optionally appending origin=checkout) and navigates there without calling any API from this page.

User can edit contact fields (full_name, email, phone) and toggle notification preferences (notify_by_email, notify_by_sms); each input is bound to an internal contactInfo state object that starts with the contact_info returned by getCheckoutSummary.

On clicking Continue to Payment, the checkout form validates that required contact fields meet basic requirements (e.g., non-empty, valid email format) and that at least one notification preference is allowed if business rules require it.

If validation passes, the checkout form triggers saveCheckoutContactInfo with the current contactInfo state; based on the response, the page either shows a success state and navigates to the next payment step (e.g., payment entry page handled elsewhere) or displays an error banner from the returned message.

If saveCheckoutContactInfo returns success=false, a non-blocking error message is shown near the contact form and the Continue to Payment action is prevented from navigating until the user corrects the issues and retries.

User can click Terms & Conditions or Privacy Policy links in the checkout footer; this navigates to policies.html with the appropriate policyType query parameter and does not trigger any additional API calls on this page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for checkout.html is derived from URL query parameters and server responses. The page reads the `origin` URL parameter on load (e.g., checkout.html?origin=cart). This parameter is not used to filter data from getCheckoutSummary; instead, it influences navigation behavior for the Back to Cart & Booking Summary control and can optionally be echoed when navigating back (e.g., cart.html?origin=checkout). All cart contents, totals, applied promotions, and contact_info are sourced exclusively from getCheckoutSummary, which is called on initial page load and may be re-called on explicit refresh actions. The page does not cache cart data in URL parameters or localStorage; it relies on the server session or another backend mechanism to associate the current Cart and related entities with the user. For outgoing navigation to policies, the page constructs URLs like policies.html?policyType=terms strictly from link targets, without persisting cart or contact data in the URL. Any change to URL parameters (such as reloading checkout.html with a different origin) causes a fresh call to getCheckoutSummary to ensure the UI reflects the latest server-side state.

components.functionality: Initializes the checkout page by reading URL parameters, calling getCheckoutSummary, and storing the resulting cart, cart_items, applied_promotions, and contact_info in page-level state for other components to consume.

Displays a read-only list of all CartItems in the current Cart, grouped or labeled by type (appointment, product, gift card), and shows key details from related entities such as service names, package names, appointment date/time, technician, product names, and gift card recipient information.

Shows the monetary breakdown of the cart, including subtotal, tax if provided, discounts from applied promotions, and the final total; uses the read-only Cart and AppliedPromotion data without performing any calculations on the client that would conflict with server values.

Displays a detailed list of promotions that have already been applied to the Cart, including promo_code and discount_amount from AppliedPromotion and any descriptive text available from the related Promotion entity if it is provided via getCheckoutSummary.

Collects and edits customer contact information (full_name, email, phone) and notification preferences (notify_by_email, notify_by_sms), pre-filling fields from contact_info returned by getCheckoutSummary and submitting updates via saveCheckoutContactInfo before the user can proceed to payment.
Displays validation errors and server-side error messages related to contact information saving, based on the response from saveCheckoutContactInfo and local form validation logic.

Provides navigation actions for the checkout page, including going back to cart.html while respecting the origin URL parameter and linking to policies.html with the appropriate policyType for Terms & Conditions and Privacy Policy links.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered content column beneath the existing full‑width navigation and a large, blush 'CHECKOUT' heading with a thin divider, directly mirroring the mockup. Within this central column, components are arranged in a linear top‑to‑bottom flow: data initialization (non‑visual) at the very top in DOM order, followed by the booking summary list, applied promotions, totals panel, validation messages, the contact/notification form, and finally the navigation controls at the bottom. Inside the summary and totals areas, information is structured into informal columns reminiscent of the 3‑column service menu: each line item reads left‑to‑right with description/details on the left and prices aligned toward the right edge.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered content column beneath the existing full‑width navigation and a large, blush 'CHECKOUT' heading with a thin divider, directly mirroring the mockup. Within this central column, components are arranged in a linear top‑to‑bottom flow: data initialization (non‑visual) at the very top in DOM order, followed by the booking summary list, applied promotions, totals panel, validation messages, the contact/notification form, and finally the navigation controls at the bottom. Inside the summary and totals areas, information is structured into informal columns reminiscent of the 3‑column service menu: each line item reads left‑to‑right with description/details on the left and prices aligned toward the right edge.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, capped at a comfortable fixed max‑width (around 960–1040px), with generous left and right margins to preserve the editorial, centered composition. A large vertical gap (about 80–96px) separates the nav from the 'CHECKOUT' title, and a similar gap separates the title/divider from the first content block (summary list), emphasizing an airy upper half of the page. Within the main column, major content groups (summary + promotions + totals, then contact form, then navigation controls) are separated by 48–64px gaps, while items within each group (rows in the summary, lines in the totals, individual form fields) use tighter but still generous spacing in the 16–32px range. The totals panel occupies the rightmost 40–50% of the central column, echoing the right‑aligned price column in the service menu.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains intentionally low. The summary list surfaces only essential information for each booking/product in a clean, single line per item, with 24–32px vertical space between rows. There are no heavy table borders or dense grids; instead, subtle alignment and white space define structure. The totals panel uses a minimal stack of 3–5 key lines (subtotal, tax, promotions, grand total) with generous line spacing and breathing room around the block. The contact form fields are stacked vertically with comfortable spacing and no side‑by‑side input clusters, avoiding visual clutter. Overall, more vertical space is allocated than strictly necessary, preserving the calm, premium, boutique feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the mockup’s pattern: the user first encounters the light navigation at the very top, then their eye drops to the large, centrally aligned 'CHECKOUT' heading and the subtle divider beneath it. From there, attention moves down into the checkout content column. The eye scans horizontally across each summary row—left to right from service/product details to the right‑aligned price—row by row. After the summary block, the viewer’s gaze briefly pauses on the applied promotions (if present) and then on the right‑anchored totals panel, where the grand total is an intentional focal point. Below that, attention naturally flows into the contact and notifications form fields, and finally settles on the right‑aligned primary action button in the navigation controls. The persistent chat bubble in the bottom right remains a peripheral callout that does not compete with the main vertical flow.

layout.overall_description: Beneath a full‑width, minimalist navigation bar and a generous band of white space, a large, blush‑colored 'CHECKOUT' title sits centered on the page, with a fine horizontal divider just below it, echoing the original ‘WAXING’ mockup. All checkout content is contained in a single, fixed‑width central column that floats within wide left and right margins. After another tall cushion of white space, the column begins with a clean list of booked waxing services, gift cards, and products, each line stretching across the column in an informal three‑part rhythm: concise item details toward the left, any secondary info (such as date or quantity) toward the middle, and a price aligned to the right edge, with ample vertical gaps between each row. Immediately beneath this, applied promotions are listed in a lighter, smaller block that visually tucks under the prices, and then a right‑aligned totals stack (subtotal, discounts, tax, grand total) forms a compact but prominent block in the lower right quadrant of the column, mirroring the price column from the mockup. Below this financial summary, a clearly separated section contains vertically stacked contact and notification fields, laid out with ample breathing room between labels and inputs. At the very bottom of the column, after another noticeable vertical gap, a right‑aligned group of navigation controls presents the primary ‘Proceed to payment’ button alongside a more subdued back link, with extra white space beneath to keep the composition light. Throughout, the layout remains quiet and editorial: generous negative space, a restrained color palette, and subtle alignments guide the eye gently down the page from review to confirmation to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is positioned at the very top of the central content column in the document order, immediately following the page’s title/divider region but before any visible checkout components. Visually, it occupies no perceivable space—no box, no background, no text—so the user sees an uninterrupted flow from the heading down to the summary list. Its logical position ensures that the summary, promotions, totals, and form only render once the necessary checkout data is available, without altering the visual spacing or the calm, airy layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non‑visual/background component that participates only in the logical flow, not in the visual layout. It has effectively zero height and width, does not affect spacing, and does not scroll or fix to any region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout summary list sits as the first visible block of the checkout content, centered within the fixed‑width main column. It begins approximately 80–96px below the thin divider under the 'CHECKOUT' heading, leaving a large band of white space above. The list spans nearly the full width of the central column (about 90–100% of the column width) while still respecting the wide outer page margins. Each line item in the list is laid out to echo the three‑column menu logic: the left third holds the primary item information (service or product name), the middle region holds secondary metadata (date/time, duration, quantity, recipient), and the right third is reserved for the price, right‑aligned to form a clean vertical price column. There is generous vertical spacing—around 24–32px—between each row, with no visible row borders, letting white space define separation. The entire list block occupies roughly the upper third to half of the content column’s visible area depending on the number of items, forming the primary review area before totals and form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally with the page scroll as part of the main column. Internally, it behaves like a responsive three‑column row system: on wider screens, the three segments align horizontally; on narrow screens, the segments may stack or reflow while maintaining the same vertical spacing rhythm and right‑aligned prices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied promotions list appears directly beneath the checkout summary list, separated by a modest vertical gap of roughly 24–32px so it reads as related but secondary information. It shares the same left and right boundaries as the summary list, spanning the full width of the central column. Each promotion is displayed as a subtle, slim row, typically with the promotion label or code aligned left and the discount amount aligned to the same right edge as the summary prices. The rows use slightly tighter vertical spacing than the main summary items—around 16–24px—to appear more like annotations. When present, the promotions block visually tucks under the list of items and leads the eye toward the pending totals; when there are no applied promotions, this component collapses to zero height, preserving the surrounding white space and preventing any empty boxes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flowing block within the central column. Expands vertically to fit the number of promotions or collapses entirely when there are none, smoothly shifting the totals panel upward without disrupting the overall spacing rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The totals panel is positioned just below the promotions list, with a slightly larger vertical gap of about 32–48px to signify a distinct summary section. Within the central column, the totals panel hugs the right side, occupying roughly 40–50% of the column width and aligning its right edge with the price column of the summary list above. Inside this panel, subtotal, discounts, tax, and grand total are stacked vertically, each line left‑aligned within the panel but collectively forming a tight vertical group that stands out against the surrounding white space. The spacing between lines is generous (16–24px), with the grand total given slightly more visual weight (e.g., marginally larger type or bolder weight) while still adhering to the minimalist style. The left side of the central column remains mostly empty in this band, which enhances the editorial feel and directs attention toward the numbers on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document order under the promotions list. It is not sticky or fixed, but its right‑aligned placement within the central column is preserved across viewport sizes; on smaller screens, it may expand toward full width while keeping its internal right‑aligned amounts for consistency.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation messages panel sits directly under the totals panel and just above the contact form, with a vertical gap of about 24–32px above and below it. It spans the full width of the central column, aligning its left edge flush with the other content blocks. When inactive, it occupies minimal or zero height, so the user moves seamlessly from totals to the form. When errors or important messages are present, it manifests as a slim, unobtrusive horizontal band or stacked lines of text using the blush accent color for text or a fine border, preserving the light, editorial feel rather than a heavy alert box. This placement ensures that once the user has reviewed financial details, any issues with their input are communicated at the exact point where they re‑engage with the form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flowing block that expands vertically only when there are messages to show. It pushes nearby components up or down as needed, without overlaying content or fixing itself to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form occupies a substantial section beneath the validation messages, separated from the totals by a generous 48–64px gap that clearly marks the transition from review to input. It spans nearly the full width of the central column (about 90–100%), with all labels and inputs left‑aligned to create a clean, vertical rhythm. Fields (such as name, email, phone, and notification preferences) are arranged in a single column stack; no side‑by‑side fields are used, maintaining low visual density. Each field group has 16–24px vertical spacing, with a slightly larger margin—24–32px—between logically distinct groups (e.g., contact vs notification preferences). The section may include a subtle heading like 'Contact details & notifications' at the top, aligned with the left edge of the form, with a modest gap (16–24px) below it before the first input. This section typically occupies the middle to lower third of the content area, giving the user a calm, uncluttered space to confirm or enter their details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally with the page; it is not fixed or sticky. On smaller screens, the form simply narrows with the central column while maintaining the same vertical stacking and spacing, preserving readability and the airy feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation controls sit at the very bottom of the central column, directly beneath the contact form, separated by a comfortable 48–64px vertical gap so they feel like a distinct conclusion to the checkout step. The control group does not span the full column width; instead, its buttons are aligned to the right edge of the column, echoing the right‑aligned totals and price column. The primary action button (e.g., 'Proceed to payment') is placed at the far right, with a slightly larger size and stronger visual weight, while the secondary action (e.g., 'Back' or 'Return') sits immediately to its left, separated by 16–24px. There is generous white space (64–96px) beneath the buttons before the page bottom, maintaining the light, editorial composition and preventing the controls from feeling cramped against the viewport edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow component located at the end of the content column. It scrolls with the rest of the page and is not fixed or sticky, allowing the persistent chat bubble in the page’s bottom right to remain the only floating element in that region.

name: Gift Cards
title: Gift Cards
description: Page for browsing and filtering available gift card types, focusing on waxing-related gift cards, and selecting a template to configure on the Gift Card Detail page.

core_features: Retrieve and display a list of available gift card templates using the getGiftCardTemplates interface.
Apply an optional usageType filter (e.g., waxing_only, waxing_services, all_services, products_only, spa_combination) based on the URL parameter and in-page filter controls.
Highlight and quickly surface templates that are relevant to waxing services when the usageType filter is set to waxing_only or waxing_services.
Allow users to click a gift card template (or a dedicated select action) to navigate to gift_card_detail.html with the chosen templateId passed as a URL parameter.
Provide educational information about how each gift card can be used based on its usage_type and descriptive data returned by getGiftCardTemplates.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Arrive from another page with a preset usageType filter
1. User clicks a link to gift_cards.html from index.html or offers.html, which includes a usageType URL parameter.
2. Gift Cards page reads usageType from the URL query string.
3. Page calls getGiftCardTemplates(usageType) to retrieve only matching gift card templates.
4. Filter bar shows the selected usageType as active.
5. Gift card list displays templates returned by the interface.
6. User clicks on a specific gift card template.
7. Browser navigates to gift_card_detail.html?templateId=<selectedTemplateId>.

Workflow: Browse all gift cards without filters
1. User navigates directly to gift_cards.html without a usageType parameter.
2. Page initializes with no active usageType filter.
3. Page calls getGiftCardTemplates() with no parameter or a null/undefined usageType so all templates are returned according to interface behavior.
4. Filter bar indicates that all usage types are shown.
5. User scrolls through the list of templates and optionally reads usage information per template.
6. User selects a template, triggering navigation to gift_card_detail.html?templateId=<selectedTemplateId>.

Workflow: Change usageType filter within the page
1. User is on gift_cards.html viewing a current list of templates.
2. User interacts with the usageType filter component (e.g., chooses waxing_only or waxing_services).
3. The filter component updates the URL query string to set usageType=<chosenUsageType> (replacing or adding the parameter) without navigating away from the page context.
4. After the URL is updated, the page logic detects the changed usageType parameter.
5. Page calls getGiftCardTemplates(usageType) again to fetch templates matching the newly selected usageType.
6. Gift card list re-renders with the updated templates.
7. Any contextual usage-type info panel updates its messaging based on the new usageType value.

Workflow: Understand how a specific gift card can be used
1. User sees a list of templates after getGiftCardTemplates is called.
2. User hovers over or clicks to expand information on a given template card.
3. The card or a linked info panel displays details derived from template fields (name, description, usage_type, min_amount, max_amount, preset_amounts if present).
4. User confirms that the gift card can be used for waxing services only, waxing services plus other services, or broader usage depending on usage_type.
5. User proceeds to select the template and navigate to gift_card_detail.html with the corresponding templateId.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial data load
- Trigger: Page load event on gift_cards.html.
- Behavior: Read usageType from URL query string if present. Call getGiftCardTemplates(usageType) and populate internal state with the returned array of GiftCardTemplate records. Render loading state until data is returned, then render the list or an empty-state message if no templates are returned.

Interaction: Change usageType via filter controls
- Trigger: User action on usageType filter (e.g., clicking a filter option or changing a dropdown value).
- Behavior: Update the URL query parameter usageType to the selected value or remove it if the user chooses an 'all types' option. Re-run getGiftCardTemplates with the new usageType value. Re-render the list and update any educational copy tied to usageType.

Interaction: Inspect a template’s usage details
- Trigger: User clicks a 'More info' area, expands a card, or hovers (depending on implementation) on a specific gift card template.
- Behavior: Component displays data from that GiftCardTemplate (description, usage_type, min_amount, max_amount, preset_amounts) and may show generic explanatory text mapped to the usage_type enum value. No new interface call is required; all information is derived from the existing getGiftCardTemplates response.

Interaction: Select template and navigate to detail page
- Trigger: User clicks on a gift card template card or a dedicated 'Select' button within the card.
- Behavior: Browser navigates to gift_card_detail.html with templateId set to the selected GiftCardTemplate.id as a URL query parameter. No other API call is triggered on this page by this action.

Interaction: Handle empty or error states
- Trigger: getGiftCardTemplates returns an empty array or an error.
- Behavior: Display a functional message indicating that no gift card templates are available for the current usageType, and provide controls to clear or change the usageType filter. For errors, show a retry action that re-triggers getGiftCardTemplates with the last-known usageType.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from the usageType URL query parameter and the results from getGiftCardTemplates. On every page load or when the URL changes, the page reads usageType from location.search. If usageType is present and matches one of the defined usage_type enum values (waxing_only, waxing_services, all_services, products_only, spa_combination), the page calls getGiftCardTemplates(usageType); otherwise, it calls getGiftCardTemplates() without a filter (or with a null/undefined parameter) to retrieve all active templates as defined by the backend. The currently selected usageType is stored in in-memory page state only for immediate access by components (e.g., filter bar, usage info panel). When the user changes filters, the URL is updated first (e.g., gift_cards.html?usageType=waxing_only), which becomes the single source of truth for filtering; a URL change listener or controlled navigation logic then re-reads usageType and re-invokes getGiftCardTemplates. No localStorage or other persistent client state is used. Navigation to gift_card_detail.html is handled via standard links including templateId=<GiftCardTemplate.id> in the query string, and this page does not manage state beyond that navigation.

components.functionality: Provides in-page controls to filter gift card templates by usageType (e.g., waxing_only, waxing_services, all_services, products_only, spa_combination). It reads the current usageType from the URL on initialization and renders the appropriate filter option as selected. When the user changes the selection, it updates the URL query parameter and triggers a refresh of the templates list.

Central logic to call getGiftCardTemplates based on the current usageType URL parameter. Manages loading, success, empty, and error states. Exposes the fetched array of GiftCardTemplate records to child components such as the list and usage info panel.

Displays the collection of GiftCardTemplate items returned by gift-card-list-loader. For each template, shows key fields such as name and description, and may include visible indicators derived from usage_type and amount-related fields to help users differentiate between card types.

Represents an individual GiftCardTemplate within the list, encapsulating interactions specific to that template: showing its basic and expanded details and allowing the user to select it. It uses fields like id, name, description, usage_type, min_amount, max_amount, and preset_amounts to display relevant information.

Displays contextual educational information about how gift cards can be used, tailored to the currently effective usageType. It may combine generic explanatory text mapped to usage_type enum values with aggregated data from the currently loaded GiftCardTemplate items (e.g., counts or presence of waxing-only cards). Updates whenever usageType or the fetched template list changes.

Handles display when getGiftCardTemplates returns no templates or when the interface call fails. For empty results, it suggests adjusting the usageType filter (via interaction with usage-type-filter-bar). For errors, it displays a retry control that re-invokes getGiftCardTemplates with the last known usageType.

Centralizes navigation logic when a user selects a gift card template. It listens for selection events from gift-card-card or gift-card-list and constructs a navigation URL in the format gift_card_detail.html?templateId=<GiftCardTemplate.id>, then initiates navigation.

Synchronizes page state with URL query parameters. On load and whenever the URL changes, it parses usageType from the query string and exposes it to other components (e.g., usage-type-filter-bar and gift-card-list-loader). When components request changes to usageType, it updates the URL query string accordingly, ensuring that usageType in the URL remains the single source of truth for filtering.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps a linear, top‑to‑bottom flow: global nav (out of scope) at the very top, followed by a tall block of whitespace, then a large, centered 'GIFT CARDS' page heading and thin divider (mirroring the 'WAXING' title treatment). Directly beneath this, the functional content appears in a single, centered content column: first the usage‑type filter bar, then a small, right‑leaning usage‑type info panel, and finally the main gift card list laid out in a loose three‑column grid. Status elements (loader, empty/error panel) are vertically centered within the gift card list area when active. A subtle template selection navigator is anchored to the bottom of the viewport, aligned with the same central column. Non‑visual state components sit behind all of this.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps a linear, top‑to‑bottom flow: global nav (out of scope) at the very top, followed by a tall block of whitespace, then a large, centered 'GIFT CARDS' page heading and thin divider (mirroring the 'WAXING' title treatment). Directly beneath this, the functional content appears in a single, centered content column: first the usage‑type filter bar, then a small, right‑leaning usage‑type info panel, and finally the main gift card list laid out in a loose three‑column grid. Status elements (loader, empty/error panel) are vertically centered within the gift card list area when active. A subtle template selection navigator is anchored to the bottom of the viewport, aligned with the same central column. Non‑visual state components sit behind all of this.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with generous left/right margins creating an editorial, centered layout. The vertical space above the heading is large (around 96px), and between heading and filters around 64–80px. Between the filter bar, info panel, and gift card list, spacing steps down to 24–48px, maintaining airiness. Each row of gift cards has about 24–32px vertical gap beneath it, and the three informal columns of cards have wide horizontal gutters (approximately 48–64px) to keep the grid light. The bottom sticky navigator is slim, around 56–72px tall, and inset to align with the content column rather than stretching edge‑to‑edge.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low.
