There are a limited number of interactive elements in any vertical band: a single filter row, a small supporting info panel, then a grid of cards with generous breathing room between rows and columns. Cards themselves are minimalist—mostly typography with ample internal padding and whitespace—so even a full row of three cards feels light. Status states replace or overlay the list in a centered, compact way, rather than adding clutter. The layout never shows dense tables or tightly stacked controls; negative space is used as a primary design element.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the nav down into the central 'GIFT CARDS' title, then to the thin divider beneath it. From there, attention drops into the usage‑type filter bar (the first interactive element), then to the smaller, right‑aligned info panel. Next, the gaze progresses downward into the gift card list and begins scanning each row from left to right, moving through up to three cards per row with wide gutters between them, then dropping to the next row. The sticky template selection navigator at the bottom edge of the content column becomes a subtle, persistent secondary focal point once a card is selected, echoing the peripheral focus behavior of the original chat bubble in the mockup.

layout.overall_description: The Gift Cards page presents as a calm, editorial layout centered within a wide, airy viewport. After a light global navigation and a deep band of whitespace, a large, refined 'GIFT CARDS' heading appears in the center, set in an elegant serif with a soft blush accent. A thin, understated divider directly beneath the title marks the transition into the functional area. Below this, the content sits within a fixed‑width central column that feels narrower than the full viewport, emphasizing a boutique, printed‑catalog vibe. The usage‑type filter bar stretches modestly across the center of this column, with generous padding around it. Just under it, a smaller, right‑aligned info panel offers contextual explanation without competing for attention. Further down, the page opens into a three‑across grid of gift card options: rows of minimalist cards, evenly spaced with wide horizontal gutters and ample vertical separation. When data is loading or unavailable, a single centered panel occupies the list area, maintaining the visual rhythm. At the bottom of the viewport, aligned to the same central column, a slim, sticky navigator appears when needed, inviting the user to proceed with a selected template while remaining visually quiet. The overall impression is serene, premium, and uncluttered, guiding the eye gently downward through a sequence of spacious, well‑ordered elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The usage‑type filter bar is positioned near the top of the main content column, directly below the large 'GIFT CARDS' heading and its thin divider. There is a generous vertical gap—roughly 64–80px—between the divider and the top edge of the filter bar, reinforcing the airy separation between the title area and interactive content. Horizontally, the filter bar is centered on the page and constrained to about 60–70% of the viewport width, matching the implicit central column. It appears as a single horizontal band of controls (e.g., segmented buttons, pills, or dropdowns) with approximately 16px vertical padding and 16–24px side padding inside the bar itself. The bar does not touch the full browser edges; instead, it floats within wide side margins, keeping the editorial, centered feel. Beneath the filter bar, a moderate gap of around 24–32px separates it from the usage‑type info panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column; it scrolls with the page and maintains its centered alignment as viewport width changes, capped at a max content width consistent with the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift‑card‑list loader occupies the same visual footprint as the gift card list and is only visible while data is being fetched or refreshed. It is centered within the main content column, starting approximately 24–32px beneath the usage‑type info panel (or filter bar if the info panel is absent). Horizontally, it spans about 60–70% of the viewport width, matching the gift card list area and central column. Within this area, a loading indicator (such as a spinner or simple animated glyph) is vertically and horizontally centered in a block roughly 200–300px tall, leaving generous whitespace above and below. The loader may optionally sit on a very subtle, translucent overlay above a blank list background, but it does not darken the entire page; it feels light and non‑intrusive, aligned with the minimal aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in place of the gift card list. It is not fixed; it flows within the document and collapses entirely when not active, allowing the list or status panel to occupy that space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift card list is placed in the lower half of the main content column, beneath the filter bar and the usage‑type info panel. A vertical gap of about 32–48px separates the bottom of the info panel from the top of the list area, preserving the calm, tiered structure. The list spans the full width of the central column (approximately 60–70% of the viewport) and is centered on the page with wide, symmetric side margins. Internally, the list adopts a three‑column row pattern: each row contains up to three gift‑card cards, evenly distributed from left to right. The card blocks are aligned across an implicit 12‑column grid so that each card occupies roughly 3–4 columns, leaving substantial horizontal gutters of about 48–64px between cards. There is a generous vertical margin of 24–32px between consecutive rows, so each row feels distinct and airy rather than tightly packed. The list can extend downward as needed, with consistent spacing maintained regardless of how many rows appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column, expanding vertically as more items are shown. It reflows responsively: on narrower screens it can gracefully reduce to two columns and then one column while retaining strong lateral margins and vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each gift‑card card is an individual item within the gift card list, occupying one of the informal three columns per row. Visually, a card is a vertically oriented rectangle with a clean, minimalist layout and plenty of internal padding—approximately 16–24px on all sides. It is top‑aligned with sibling cards in the same row, with equal horizontal spacing on its left and right edges relative to neighboring cards and the column boundaries. In a full‑width central column, each card takes up roughly one‑third of the available width minus the wide gutters, so that three cards align neatly from left to right and appear balanced. Typography inside the card is slightly larger than body type but smaller than the main page heading, with clear hierarchy between gift card title, brief description, and key metadata (such as price or range), all separated by 8–16px vertical gaps. The card’s background remains predominantly white, with any blush accents used sparingly (e.g., tiny labels or subtle borders) to maintain the high‑end, editorial feel. Vertically, each card contributes to a row height that leaves breathing room beneath the content before the next row begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a responsive grid item. Multiple cards line up in rows within the gift card list; as available width decreases, cards reflow to fewer columns per row while maintaining consistent inner padding and vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The usage‑type info panel sits just below the usage‑type filter bar and slightly above the main gift card list, acting as a brief, contextual explanation or helper. There is a modest vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the filter bar and the top of this panel. Horizontally, the panel is aligned to the right side of the central content column, occupying roughly 40–50% of that column’s width. This right‑leaning placement keeps the left side visually open and mirrors the asymmetrical, editorial patterns common in high‑end layouts, while still keeping the panel clearly associated with the filters above and the list below. The panel height is relatively small (often a one‑ or two‑line block of text) with 12–16px internal padding and minimal bordering—perhaps just a subtle, thin line or a faint blush accent at the top edge—to avoid overpowering the composition. Ample whitespace remains above and below, so the panel feels like a gentle note rather than a heavy box.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow, expanding vertically if more explanatory text is needed but always anchored to the right side of the central column. On very narrow screens, it can stack full‑width beneath the filter bar while preserving comfortable vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty‑or‑error‑state panel appears in place of the gift card list whenever there are no results or an error occurs. It is centered within the same central column region where the list would normally live, starting roughly 32–48px below the usage‑type info panel (or directly below the filter bar if the info panel is absent). The panel itself occupies about 40–60% of the central column width, leaving broad margins on both sides, and its content is vertically centered within a block about 240–320px tall. Inside, a concise heading, short explanation, and perhaps a single call‑to‑action button are stacked with 8–16px vertical spacing, maintaining the minimal, calm aesthetic. There are no heavy borders or loud icons; any visual embellishment (like a small line icon or subtle blush accent) is restrained and placed near the top of the panel to anchor the eye. The surrounding space remains mostly empty, reinforcing the sense of deliberate simplicity even in error or empty states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the gift card list area when active, occupying its space in the layout. It scrolls naturally with the page and collapses entirely once valid list content is available again.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The template selection navigator is a slim, persistent bar that appears only after a gift card template is selected, helping the user move to the Gift Card Detail page. Visually, it is anchored near the bottom of the viewport, aligned with the central content column rather than stretching full‑bleed. Horizontally, it matches the width of the main content column (approximately 60–70% of the viewport) and is centered with generous margins on either side. The bar height is modest—around 56–72px—with a light background and subtle top border or shadow, ensuring it feels integrated but not heavy. Within the bar, text describing the current selection is left‑aligned, while a primary action button (e.g., 'Continue' or 'Customize') is right‑aligned, both inset from the edges by 16–24px. A small amount of space (16–24px) separates the bar from the very bottom of the viewport on larger screens, allowing it to float slightly above the edge and echo the original mockup’s peripheral CTA positioning. It never obscures core content; instead, the page content scrolls behind it, with enough bottom padding on the main column to prevent final rows of cards from being hidden.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the bottom of the viewport and horizontally centered, constrained to the same max width as the central column. It appears conditionally when a selection is made and remains visible as the user scrolls, acting as a persistent, but understated, call to proceed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is non‑visual and does not occupy any spatial area in the layout. Conceptually, it sits behind all visible components, synchronizing filters, selected usage types, and chosen templates with the browser’s URL. Its presence is perceived only through behavior—such as the page re‑loading a specific filter or preselected card when the URL is revisited—not through any graphical element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual footprint; it is effectively fixed to the application layer, monitoring and updating state as the user interacts with the filter bar, list, and template selection navigator.

name: Gift Card Detail
title: Gift Card Detail
description: Configure a specific gift card template (amount, delivery method, schedule, and recipient details) and add it to the cart.

core_features: Load and display details for the selected gift card template using the templateId URL parameter and getGiftCardTemplateDetails.
Allow the user to select or enter a gift card amount, constrained by the template’s allowed amount rules.
Allow the user to choose a delivery method (such as email_delivery or physical_card) for the gift card.
Allow the user to choose when the gift card should be sent (send_now or schedule_for_later) and, if scheduled, select a future date.
Collect recipient information: recipient name, recipient email, optional personal message, and optional sender name.
Validate all configuration inputs (amount, delivery selection, send timing, date, and recipient information) before submission.
Create a configured GiftCardPurchase and add it to the cart by calling createGiftCardPurchaseAndAddToCart.
After adding to cart, show confirmation and provide navigation to cart.html with origin=gift_card_detail for viewing the cart or proceeding to checkout.
Provide a way to navigate back to the gift_cards.html overview page.
user_workflows:
Configure and add an email-delivery gift card:
1) User lands on gift_card_detail.html with ?templateId={id} in the URL.
2) The page reads templateId and the template loader component calls getGiftCardTemplateDetails(templateId).
3) Once loaded, the amount selector shows the template name and available amount options (e.g., presets, min/max range) based on the returned GiftCardTemplate.
4) The user selects a preset amount or enters a custom amount; the component validates that the value is numeric and within the allowed range.
5) In the delivery options section, the user selects deliveryMethod=email_delivery and send_timing=send_now.
6) In the recipient form, the user enters recipient_name, recipient_email, an optional message, and an optional from_name.
7) The summary and submit component validates all fields; if any are invalid, errors are shown inline and in a general error area.
8) When validation passes, the user clicks Add to Cart. The page calls createGiftCardPurchaseAndAddToCart with templateId and the configured fields (amount, deliveryMethod, sendTiming, scheduledSendDate if applicable, recipientName, recipientEmail, message, fromName).
9) On success, the page stores the returned GiftCardPurchase and cart_item_id in page state and may update the URL with a status=added parameter.
10) A success area appears with options to View Cart or Proceed to Checkout, both linking to cart.html?origin=gift_card_detail.

Schedule a gift card to be sent at a future date:
1) User opens gift_card_detail.html with ?templateId={id}. Optional URL parameters such as amount, deliveryMethod, and sendTiming may prefill the form (if valid).
2) The template loader fetches details via getGiftCardTemplateDetails(templateId).
3) The user configures the amount as allowed by the template.
4) The user selects a delivery method (for example email_delivery) and sets send_timing=schedule_for_later.
5) A date input becomes active; the user selects a send date in the future. The component validates that the date is a valid calendar date and is not in the past.
6) The user enters recipient_name, recipient_email, and any optional message/from_name.
7) The user clicks Add to Cart. The form validates amount, deliveryMethod, sendTiming, scheduledSendDate (required for schedule_for_later), and required recipient fields.
8) If validation passes, createGiftCardPurchaseAndAddToCart is called with scheduledSendDate populated and all other configuration values.
9) On success, the user sees a confirmation and can either stay on the page to configure another gift card or go to cart.html?origin=gift_card_detail via the post-add action buttons.

Handle missing or invalid templateId and recover:
1) User accesses gift_card_detail.html without a templateId URL parameter, or with a templateId that returns an error from getGiftCardTemplateDetails.
2) On page initialization, the template loader checks for templateId; if missing, it sets an error state without calling the API.
3) If templateId is present but getGiftCardTemplateDetails returns an error, the loader sets an error state describing that the gift card template could not be loaded.
4) While in an error state, the configuration form and Add to Cart button remain disabled and do not call createGiftCardPurchaseAndAddToCart.
5) The page shows an error notification with an option to navigate Back to Gift Cards (link to gift_cards.html) so the user can choose another template.
interactions: On page load, read the templateId URL parameter; if present, trigger getGiftCardTemplateDetails to fetch the GiftCardTemplate; if not present, display an error and disable the configuration form.

When getGiftCardTemplateDetails resolves successfully, populate template-dependent fields such as displayed name, description, preset_amounts, min_amount, and max_amount in the amount selector and page header.
When the user selects a preset amount button, update the currently selected amount state and clear any amount-related validation errors.
When the user types a custom amount, validate that it is numeric and, if min_amount or max_amount are provided by the template, ensure the value falls within that range before allowing submission.

When the user selects a delivery method (email_delivery or physical_card), update the configuration state and adjust any helper text or hints; ensure that recipient_email remains required per data model constraints.
When the user selects send_timing=send_now, ensure scheduledSendDate is cleared in the configuration state and that date-related validation errors are removed.
When the user selects send_timing=schedule_for_later, require the scheduledSendDate field and validate that the selected date is a valid future date before allowing Add to Cart.

On changes to recipient_name, recipient_email, message, or from_name fields, update the pending GiftCardPurchase-like configuration state; validate required fields and email format on blur or on submit.

When the user clicks Add to Cart, trigger full form validation across all sections; if any validation fails, prevent the API call, scroll or focus to the first error, and show messages in the error notification component.

If validation passes, call createGiftCardPurchaseAndAddToCart with the current configuration values; show a loading state on the Add to Cart button while the call is in progress to prevent duplicate submissions.

On successful response from createGiftCardPurchaseAndAddToCart (success=true), store the returned GiftCardPurchase and cart_item_id in page state, optionally update the URL to include status=added and cartItemId, and show a success confirmation with navigation buttons.

On error response from createGiftCardPurchaseAndAddToCart (success=false or network error), show an error notification with the returned message (if provided) and keep the configuration form available for correction and retry.
When the user clicks View Cart or Proceed to Checkout in the post-add confirmation area, navigate to cart.html?origin=gift_card_detail.
When the user clicks Back to Gift Cards, navigate to gift_cards.html, clearing any gift-card-detail-specific URL parameters.
When the user dismisses an error or success notification, clear the related message from page state and, if desired, remove status-related URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary context is determined by the templateId URL parameter. On each load of gift_card_detail.html, the page reads templateId from the query string. If templateId is present, getGiftCardTemplateDetails(templateId) is called to retrieve the GiftCardTemplate; if it is absent or invalid, the page enters an error state and disables the configuration and Add to Cart actions. Optional URL parameters can prefill or influence the configuration state without using localStorage. For example: an amount parameter (if numeric and within the template’s allowed range) can pre-populate the amount selector; a deliveryMethod parameter matching a valid delivery_method enum can pre-select the delivery option; a sendTiming parameter matching send_now or schedule_for_later can pre-select the send timing; and, if sendTiming is schedule_for_later, a scheduledSendDate parameter in an agreed date format can prefill the scheduled date input (subject to validation that it is a valid and applicable date). After a successful createGiftCardPurchaseAndAddToCart call, the page may update the URL to include status=added and cartItemId (set to the returned cart_item_id). On reload, if status=added is present, the page can display a non-blocking confirmation that the last configured gift card was added to the cart while still allowing the user to configure another gift card. All transient form fields (amount, delivery, recipient details) are managed in page memory/state and are not persisted via localStorage; URL parameters are only used for initial state, navigation context (origin), and optional post-action status indicators.

components.functionality: Initialize the page by reading the templateId from the URL, calling getGiftCardTemplateDetails to fetch the GiftCardTemplate, and exposing loading, success, and error states to other components.

Present and manage the selection or entry of a gift card amount based on the loaded GiftCardTemplate, including preset_amounts and min_amount/max_amount constraints, and store the chosen amount in the pending GiftCardPurchase configuration state.

Allow the user to select delivery_method (email_delivery or physical_card) and send_timing (send_now or schedule_for_later). When schedule_for_later is selected, require and validate a scheduledSendDate, ensuring it is a valid future date before the configuration is considered complete.

Collect recipient_name, recipient_email, optional message, and optional from_name for the gift card configuration, validate the required fields and email format, and update the pending GiftCardPurchase configuration state.

Display a summary of the configured gift card (derived from GiftCardTemplate and the pending GiftCardPurchase-like state) and manage the Add to Cart action. On submit, perform final validation and, if successful, call createGiftCardPurchaseAndAddToCart. Handle the API response to update state with the created GiftCardPurchase and associated CartItem, then trigger success or error notifications.

After a successful createGiftCardPurchaseAndAddToCart call, show confirmation messaging and provide actions for the user to View Cart or Proceed to Checkout, both navigating to cart.html with origin=gift_card_detail in the query string.

Display aggregated error messages for template loading issues, form validation failures, or createGiftCardPurchaseAndAddToCart errors. This component listens to error states from other components and provides a dismiss action.
Provide a Back to Gift Cards link that navigates to gift_cards.html and can optionally clear gift-card-detail-specific URL parameters such as templateId, amount, or status.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strictly vertical, top‑to‑bottom flow: a light navigation header spans full width at the very top; immediately beneath it (when present) sits a slim error notification aligned to the central content column; after a generous vertical gap, the main gift card configuration content begins as a single, centered column. Within this column, the three form sections (amount selector, delivery options, recipient details) are stacked in order, each treated as a distinct row. A final summary/add‑to‑cart section sits at the bottom of the column, with any post‑add actions appearing directly below it. A global template loader can temporarily overlay everything when necessary.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly vertical, top‑to‑bottom flow: a light navigation header spans full width at the very top; immediately beneath it (when present) sits a slim error notification aligned to the central content column; after a generous vertical gap, the main gift card configuration content begins as a single, centered column. Within this column, the three form sections (amount selector, delivery options, recipient details) are stacked in order, each treated as a distinct row. A final summary/add‑to‑cart section sits at the bottom of the column, with any post‑add actions appearing directly below it. A global template loader can temporarily overlay everything when necessary.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A relatively narrow central column (about 60–70% of the viewport width, max ~960px) is centered horizontally, with substantial left and right margins to create an editorial feel. The navigation header is shallow in height but separated from the main content by a large vertical gap (~64–96px). Each major form section is given tall vertical breathing room: ~48–64px between sections and generous internal padding. The summary/add‑to‑cart area is visually grounded at the bottom of the content stack with extra top spacing and a subtle divider, echoing the transition line under the title in the mockup. The loader, when active, temporarily takes full viewport real estate visually (while underlying layout remains fixed).
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density remains low: only a handful of major blocks are visible in any scroll segment. Each component is surrounded by ample whitespace; fields within form sections are spaced out vertically. There are no tightly packed tables or multi‑step wizards; instead, the page reads like a quiet, editorial form. Where multiple options are shown (e.g., delivery methods), they are laid out with wide gutters, echoing the three‑column service menu spacing without appearing cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first meets the navigation header at the very top, then moves downward through a large patch of whitespace before encountering the first prominent form section (the amount selector, which doubles as the conceptual 'title' of the configuration). From there, the user’s gaze runs straight down the column: amount selection → delivery method → recipient details → summary and submit. Within sections that contain multiple options, the scan pattern is left‑to‑right across the row, mirroring the original three‑column service menu behavior. Error notifications interrupt the flow just below the nav, ensuring they are seen before the user continues down into the form. The loader, when shown, temporarily takes visual precedence by dimming or covering the underlying layout.

layout.overall_description: The Gift Card Detail page appears as a calm, centered column of content floating in generous white space. A slim, understated navigation header stretches across the very top of the viewport, but its light typography and shallow height keep it from feeling heavy. Below it, a band of empty space creates a pause before the main content begins, giving the impression of a refined editorial layout. At the heart of the page is a single, vertically stacked sequence of form sections, all constrained to a narrow central column that occupies roughly two‑thirds of the screen width and is sharply aligned to the center. The first section introduces and captures the gift card amount, followed by delivery options, then a recipient details block; each section feels like its own ‘article’ on the page, clearly separated by generous vertical gaps. At the bottom of this column, a slightly more framed summary and add‑to‑cart area anchors the flow, with a subtle divider and extra white space above acting like the thin line under the WAXING title in the mockup. Post‑add actions sit quietly below as small, text‑only links. If an error occurs, a refined banner appears directly under the nav, nudging the content downward but still respecting the airy margins. When data is loading, a soft, full‑viewport overlay with a centered indicator temporarily sits above this structure. The overall effect is minimal, luxurious, and unhurried: a single, elegant column of decisions leading down to a considered primary action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation header is positioned flush at the very top of the page, spanning the full viewport width. Its content (logo, navigation links) is horizontally centered within the same implicit central column used by the rest of the page (about 60–70% of viewport width, max ~960px), leaving large, even margins on the left and right. The header itself is relatively shallow—roughly 48–64px tall—with minimal bottom border, if any, to keep it visually light. Below the header, there is a generous vertical gap of about 64–96px before the first main form section (or the error notification, when present), preserving the airy separation between navigation and content that mirrors the mockup’s nav‑to‑title spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow (not sticky); scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page. Defines the top edge of the central column alignment used by all subsequent components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error notification banner sits directly below the navigation header and is horizontally centered on the page, aligned to the same central content column. It spans the full width of that central column (roughly 60–70% of viewport width, max ~960px), leaving the same wide side margins as the form sections. Vertically, it is relatively slim but comfortable, with around 16–24px of vertical padding, and appears visually as a floating strip of muted blush or soft neutral against the white background. When present, it reduces the vertical gap between the nav and main content so that the space from the bottom of this banner to the first form section is around 48–64px, maintaining the feeling of generous whitespace while emphasizing the notification. When not present, its space collapses completely, and the first form section moves closer to the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline in the flow, pushing subsequent content downward; does not overlay. It may slide in/out from the top but occupies static space while visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The template loader manifests as a full‑viewport overlay that sits above all other content, including the navigation. It stretches from the very top to the bottom edges and from left to right edges of the viewport. The background is a soft white or very light translucent layer, subtly dimming the underlying page without fully obscuring its structure, preserving the sense of a single, calm canvas. At the exact center of the viewport (both horizontally and vertically), a small, minimal loading indicator and/or short label appears, aligned to the same visual style as the site (fine typography, understated accent color). The loader does not change the underlying layout; it simply masks it until loading is complete.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay anchored to the viewport; it covers and disables interaction with all underlying components while active, without affecting their document positioning or scroll state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The amount selector is the first major form section in the main content area. It is centered on the page within the implicit central column that occupies about 60–70% of the viewport width (capped at roughly 960px), leaving wide gutters on both sides. The top of this section begins approximately 64–96px below the bottom of the navigation header (or the error notification banner when it is present), creating a large cushion of whitespace that visually echoes the mockup’s title area. The section itself is treated almost like a hero form block: a clear heading (e.g., ‘Gift Card Amount’) appears at the top, aligned left within the column but surrounded by generous top and bottom padding (~24–32px). The controls for choosing amount (preset options or input) sit directly beneath the heading, aligned left and not spanning edge‑to‑edge, with ample spacing (16–24px) between options. The total height of this block is moderate but feels spacious due to internal padding and a clear separation from the next section with ~48–64px of white space below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the document flow; it defines the visual start of the main content column and scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery options section is stacked directly beneath the amount selector, maintaining the same central alignment and width (approximately 60–70% of the viewport, max ~960px). There is a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px of empty space between the bottom edge of the amount selector and the top of this section, so each reads as a distinct chapter. At the top, a concise section title (e.g., ‘Delivery Method’) sits aligned left, with about 16–24px of spacing between the title and the options. The delivery options themselves (e.g., Email, Print, Physical mail) are arranged horizontally across the width of the section in up to three evenly spaced columns, echoing the three‑column service menu from the mockup: each option block occupies roughly one‑third of the central column width, with large gutters (~32–48px) between them. On narrower viewports, these option blocks wrap to multiple rows but preserve generous spacing around each. The overall height of the section remains moderate with a comfortable amount of white space above and below the options, and about 48–64px of whitespace separates this block from the recipient form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical flow. The internal option layout adapts responsively (3 columns at wide widths, fewer on narrow screens) while keeping wide gutters and airy spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recipient form section sits next in the stack, directly beneath the delivery options, centered in the same content column. There is a gap of around 48–64px between the delivery options section and the top of this form, keeping the layout light. This is the tallest content block on the page, as it holds multiple input fields (recipient name, email, message, schedule, etc.). A left‑aligned section heading appears at the top, with about 16–24px of breathing room before the first row of fields. The fields themselves are arranged in a loose, editorial pattern: single‑column or two‑column rows with generous vertical spacing (~16–24px) between rows and ample horizontal padding inside each input (8–12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal). The edges of the fields never touch the column boundaries; instead, they sit comfortably inset from the left and right, preserving a sense of quiet white space. The bottom of this section ends with extra spacing (~48–64px) before the summary and submit section, mirroring the consistent rhythm of section separation across the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the vertical flow. Internally, the fields may reflow between single and two columns based on viewport width but remain visually calm and spacious.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary and submit section anchors the bottom of the main content column. It begins roughly 48–64px below the recipient form, preceded at the top by a subtle horizontal divider line that spans a portion of the central column (e.g., 40–60% width, centered or aligned left), visually echoing the fine line beneath the WAXING title in the mockup and signaling a transition to a final review state. Below this line, the section content is left‑aligned within the same central column width. The gift card summary details (selected amount, delivery method, recipient name, etc.) are grouped in a compact, vertical stack occupying roughly the left two‑thirds of the column, with comfortable line spacing. The primary add‑to‑cart button is positioned near the bottom right of the section content area, aligned either with the right edge of the summary text block or the overall column, and sized with generous padding (around 12px vertical, 24px horizontal). There is ample white space surrounding the summary content and CTA, giving the impression of a calm, decisive endpoint rather than a cluttered checkout table. The bottom margin of this section leaves about 32–48px of space before any post‑add actions that follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow, serving as the visual terminus of the main task. No sticky behavior by default, though it remains visually distinct due to the divider and increased white space above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post‑add actions appear directly beneath the summary and submit section, aligned within the same central content column. They sit in a relatively shallow horizontal strip with about 24–32px of vertical spacing from the bottom of the summary area. The content consists of small, understated text links (e.g., ‘Continue Shopping’, ‘View Cart’, ‘Send Another Gift Card’), laid out either in a single row aligned to the right edge of the column or spread evenly in two to three small clusters across the width, reminiscent of a very light three‑column row. The typography is lighter and smaller than the main body text, with no heavy background or borders, allowing these actions to feel optional and secondary. There is a modest bottom margin of around 48–64px below this component before the end of the page content, maintaining the airy finish.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow. It typically appears conditionally—after a successful add‑to‑cart event—but when visible, it does not alter the alignment or width of the main content column.

name: Offers & Promotions
title: Offers & Promotions
description: Page that lists all currently active promotions, shows their promo codes and conditions, and lets users navigate to relevant booking or cart flows using those offers.

core_features: Fetch and display all active promotions using getActivePromotions, optionally filtered by targetType from the URL.
Show key details for each promotion, including promo_code, promo_type, discount_value, target_type, and conditions_text, so users understand how to use the offer.
Display the exact promo_code in a way that users can copy or trigger a cart navigation with the code prefilled.
Clarify eligibility conditions using Promotion fields such as min_package_price, min_order_total, starts_at, ends_at, and conditions_text.
Provide deep links from each promotion to relevant booking or cart pages (packages.html, waxing_services.html, cart.html) with appropriate URL parameters.
Allow users to filter the list of promotions by targetType (e.g., waxing_package, service, product, entire_order) via URL parameters and in-page controls.
user_workflows:
View all current promotions:
1. User opens offers.html with no URL parameters.
2. Page reads targetType from the URL; since it is absent, no filter is applied.
3. promotions-loader component calls getActivePromotions with targetType = undefined/null.
4. The returned list of Promotion entities is passed to promotion-filters and promotion-list components.
5. promotion-list displays all active promotions with their promo_code, discount details, and conditions.

Filter promotions by type from another page:
1. User clicks a link to offers.html from index.html or cart.html that includes targetType in the query string (e.g., offers.html?targetType=waxing_package).
2. On page load, promotions-loader reads targetType from the URL and calls getActivePromotions with that value.
3. Only promotions whose target_type matches the parameter are returned and shown in promotion-list.
4. The filter UI in promotion-filters highlights the corresponding filter state to reflect the URL.

Change promotion filter on the page:
1. User is on offers.html and sees filter controls for different promotion target types.
2. User clicks a filter option (for example, a control representing a specific target_type).
3. promotion-filters updates the URL query string with targetType set to the selected value (or removed when selecting an "all" state) using a navigation action.
4. The page is reloaded or the route is updated; promotions-loader reads the updated targetType from the URL.
5. promotions-loader calls getActivePromotions with the new targetType and updates the promotion-list with the filtered results.

Use a waxing package promotion to browse eligible packages:
1. User scans promotion-list and selects a promotion whose target_type is waxing_package.
2. In promotion-item-actions, the user clicks the action to view eligible waxing packages.
3. The page navigates to packages.html with the URL parameters packageType=body_waxing_package and sort=price_asc, as specified in outgoing connections.
4. packages.html handles the parameters and shows relevant packages for the user to book, with the user having seen the applicable promo code on the offers page.

Use a service-related promotion to browse services:
1. User finds a promotion where target_type is service.
2. User clicks the "Browse services" action in promotion-item-actions.
3. The page navigates to waxing_services.html with no additional parameters.
4. On waxing_services.html, the user can choose services while referring back to the promo_code they saw on the offers page.

Send a promo code to the cart:
1. User finds a promotion that uses a promo_code and wants to apply it.
2. User clicks the "Apply this code" action in promotion-item-actions.
3. The page navigates to cart.html with the query parameter promoCode set to the selected promotion's promo_code value.
4. cart.html reads promoCode from the URL and can trigger application of the corresponding Promotion to the user's Cart.
5. The user sees the cart with any applicable discount applied according to business rules handled on the cart page.

Copy a promo code for later use:
1. User views a promotion in promotion-list and wants to manually enter the code elsewhere.
2. User clicks a copy control next to the promo_code in promotion-item.
3. promotion-item-actions triggers copy-to-clipboard behavior for the promo_code (handled on the client, with no additional interfaces).
4. User pastes the promo_code when manually entering it on cart.html or another relevant page.
interactions:
On page load (onInit):
- Read targetType from the query string of offers.html.
- Call getActivePromotions(targetType) from promotions-loader.
- Handle loading, success, and error states; pass results to child components.

Filter selection (onClickTargetFilter):
- Triggered when the user clicks a filter control for a given promotion target type.
- Update the URL query string with the selected targetType (or remove it for an unfiltered state) via navigation.
- Cause the page to re-run the onInit flow and refetch promotions using the new targetType.

Promotion item expansion/details (onToggleDetails):
- When the user clicks on a promotion summary, toggle visibility of its detailed conditions (conditions_text, applicable minimums, date range).
- This is handled entirely on the client state within promotion-item; no new interfaces are called.

View eligible waxing packages (onClickViewPackages):
- Available for promotions where target_type is waxing_package.
- On click, navigate to packages.html with URL parameters packageType=body_waxing_package and sort=price_asc.
- No additional API calls are made from this page for this action.

Browse services for a service-related promotion (onClickBrowseServices):
- Available for promotions where target_type is service.
- On click, navigate to waxing_services.html.
- No parameters are added by this page; further filtering occurs on the destination page if needed.

Apply promo code to cart (onClickApplyCode):
- Available for all promotions that include a promo_code.
- On click, navigate to cart.html with the query parameter promoCode set to the promo_code of the selected Promotion entity.
- The destination page is responsible for validating and applying the promo code to the current Cart.

Copy promo code (onClickCopyCode):
- When the user clicks a copy control in promotion-item-actions, copy the promotion's promo_code into the system clipboard.
- Optionally update local component state to briefly show a confirmation message (e.g., a copied status).

Sort or grouping logic (optional client-side interaction):
- promotion-list may offer client-side grouping or ordering (e.g., by target_type or discount_value) without calling any new interfaces.
- Any such interaction only reorders already fetched Promotion items on the client.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the targetType URL query parameter on offers.html. On each page load, the page reads targetType from the URL: if present and non-empty, it is passed as the targetType argument to getActivePromotions; if absent, getActivePromotions is called without a filter (or with a null/undefined parameter, depending on implementation). The result set of Promotion entities represents the authoritative state of which offers are displayed. The filter UI reflects the current URL state by highlighting the active targetType if one is present. Changing filters updates the URL query string and triggers a new fetch from getActivePromotions. No promotions, codes, or conditions are stored in localStorage; all such data is derived from the Promotion entities returned by getActivePromotions. Navigation actions to packages.html, waxing_services.html, and cart.html encode their required state through URL parameters only: packages.html receives packageType=body_waxing_package and sort=price_asc when the user chooses to view eligible waxing packages; cart.html receives promoCode set to the selected promotion's promo_code when the user chooses to apply a code. The offers.html page itself does not mutate Promotion, Cart, or AppliedPromotion data; it only reads promotions and routes the user to flows where those mutations can happen.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the targetType URL parameter, calling getActivePromotions with that value, and exposing the resulting list of Promotion entities along with loading and error states to child components.

Provides controls for filtering promotions by target_type. Reads the current targetType from the URL (via the parent state) to show the active filter and updates the URL query string when the user selects a different filter, triggering re-fetch of promotions.
Receives the array of Promotion entities from promotions-loader and renders an ordered list of promotion-item components. Handles empty-state logic when no promotions are returned.

Displays the details for a single Promotion entity: promo_code, promo_type, discount_value, target_type, conditions_text, and any date or minimum spend constraints. Provides controls to expand/collapse conditions and exposes actions (view packages, browse services, apply code, copy code) via the promotion-item-actions component.

Renders context-appropriate actions for a given Promotion entity. For target_type=waxing_package, it includes a control that navigates to packages.html?packageType=body_waxing_package&sort=price_asc. For target_type=service, it includes a control that navigates to waxing_services.html. For any Promotion with a promo_code, it provides an action that navigates to cart.html with promoCode set to that promo_code, and a separate control to copy the promo_code to the clipboard.
Shows loading, error, or empty states based on the status of the getActivePromotions request and the size of the returned Promotion list.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear top‑to‑bottom flow as the mockup: global navigation at the very top (outside this spec), followed by a large vertical whitespace block, then a centered page title section ('Offers & Promotions'), a subtle divider line, then the promotion filters as a slim horizontal band, and finally the promotion list as the main content block. Within the promotion list, promotions are laid out in multi‑row, three‑column grids, echoing the service menu structure from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear top‑to‑bottom flow as the mockup: global navigation at the very top (outside this spec), followed by a large vertical whitespace block, then a centered page title section ('Offers & Promotions'), a subtle divider line, then the promotion filters as a slim horizontal band, and finally the promotion list as the main content block. Within the promotion list, promotions are laid out in multi‑row, three‑column grids, echoing the service menu structure from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a narrow, centered content column spanning roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, with generous left/right margins. Allocate the upper half of the first viewport mostly to whitespace and the title region (nav → large gap → title → divider), with the filters tucked close to the title. The lower half is reserved for the promotion list, with ample vertical spacing between rows and comfortable gutters between columns. The loader and state indicator occupy the same central content column and visually replace the list area when active.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep content density low and airy. Each promotion row contains up to three promotion items with generous horizontal gutters and at least 24–32px vertical separation between rows. Inside each promotion item, text lines are well spaced, and actions are limited to 1–2 subtle buttons. Avoid dense tables or tight stacks; the page should feel editorial and premium, even if there are many promotions (they simply extend the page vertically).
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the nav to the large, blush‑accent title in the center, then down to the thin divider and immediately to the concise filter bar. From there, attention flows into the promotion list, scanning left to right across each row of three items, then down to the next row, mimicking the zig‑zag scan pattern of the original service menu. When active, the loader or state indicator briefly become the focal point in the central column, but without breaking the overall column‑based visual rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a calm, editorial offers catalog within a centered column on a white background. At the very top sits a slim, understated navigation bar, followed by a tall band of empty space that lets the layout breathe. Roughly in the upper‑middle of the viewport, the page title 'OFFERS & PROMOTIONS' appears in refined typography and a muted blush accent, perfectly centered within the content column. Directly beneath it, a thin, delicate horizontal rule spans a portion of the column, acting as a subtle separator. Just below this divider, a minimal filter bar stretches across the same content width, its controls arranged lightly with plenty of air around them. Beneath the filters, the main promotion list fills the lower half of the viewport and continues downward as needed: promotions are arranged in rows of up to three, each item occupying one column of a three‑column grid, with wide gutters between them. Every item is clean and mostly text‑based, with a clear promotion title, code, conditions, and a slim action area, all sitting in an invisible grid rather than enclosed cards. When there are no promotions or data is loading, a centered message or loader appears in the same area where the promotions would be, keeping the entire experience anchored in the quiet, luxurious central column aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotions loader occupies the same central content column as the promotion list, acting as a temporary replacement while data is being fetched. It is vertically located just below the filter bar, starting roughly at the midpoint between the title/divider region and the lower edge of the first viewport. Visually, it is centered both horizontally and within the available vertical space in the lower half of the screen. The loader element (spinner, shimmer, or skeleton rows) spans approximately 40–50% of the content column width, leaving generous white space on both sides. If skeleton rows are used, they mimic the three‑column promotion grid: three soft, low‑contrast blocks in a row with 48–64px gutters between them and 24–32px vertical spacing between skeleton rows. The loader does not create its own strong container; instead, it floats within the column, keeping the luxurious, unobtrusive feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned relative within the main content column, replacing the promotion list region when active. It does not overlap the title or filters. On smaller screens, the loader adapts to the single‑column layout, centering a single skeleton or spinner at about 60–80% of the content width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotion filters form a slender horizontal band directly under the page title and its thin divider, aligned to the same centered content column. There is a generous gap of about 32–48px (4–6 base units) between the divider and the filters, preserving the airy upper section. The filter bar spans roughly 70–80% of the content column width, allowing a narrow margin on each side within the column. Filter controls (such as dropdowns or pill buttons for 'All', 'New', 'Expiring soon', etc.) are aligned left within this bar but do not extend to the full width; they occupy roughly the left two‑thirds of the bar, leaving open space to the right to avoid visual clutter. Vertical height is modest (around 48–64px total), with ample padding above and below the controls, so the bar feels like a lightly defined editorial tool row rather than a heavy toolbar. The entire filter bar sits in the upper third of the content area, clearly connected to the list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document layout, fixed to the top of the main content column under the title. It scrolls away with the page (not sticky) to maintain the minimal, calm aesthetic. On narrower viewports, controls stack or wrap gracefully within the same column width, maintaining approximately 16–24px vertical spacing between rows of controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotion list occupies the main lower portion of the page within the centered content column, beginning approximately 48–64px below the filter bar. It spans about 70–80% of the viewport width, centered, with wide outer margins so the list appears as an elegant, narrow editorial column rather than a full‑bleed grid. Inside the list, promotions are arranged in horizontal rows of up to three items, each item taking roughly one third of the list width. Wide gutters of approximately 48–64px separate the three informal columns, mirroring the original service menu. Vertically, each row of promotions is spaced apart by a clear 24–32px gap, keeping the reading rhythm relaxed. The list extends downward as needed, maintaining consistent row spacing and alignment. There are no heavy borders around the entire list; instead, the structure is implied by alignment, spacing, and occasional fine rules beneath rows if needed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a scrollable content region within the page’s normal flow. It does not have its own scroll container; instead, the entire page scrolls. On medium screens, the grid can collapse to two columns (each promotion item at 50% width within the column, maintaining generous gutters), and on small screens to a single column (one promotion per row) with increased vertical spacing (32–40px) to preserve the airy feel in a stacked layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each promotion item is a minimalist, text‑forward block positioned within a three‑column row of the promotion list. Horizontally, an item occupies roughly 30% of the list width, leaving substantial space for the 48–64px gutters between items. Vertically, the item reads like a small editorial snippet: the promotion title sits at the top, left‑aligned, followed by the promo code and conditions in a smaller, well‑spaced type underneath. The content within each item is aligned to a left text edge that aligns with other items in the same column across rows, reinforcing the grid. Internal vertical spacing between text lines is generous (8–16px between key elements) to avoid clutter. The bottom of each item houses its action panel (e.g., 'Apply Offer' or 'Book with this offer'), separated from the descriptive text by about 16–24px of whitespace or a very fine hairline rule. The overall height of each item is flexible, accommodating content variations, but rows remain visually tidy due to consistent top alignment and shared baselines for titles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the list’s grid, maintaining equal column widths and consistent horizontal gutters. On smaller breakpoints, each promotion item expands to occupy the full width of the content column, with increased top and bottom padding (16–24px) to maintain the luxurious spacing when stacked vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotion item actions form a compact, understated action panel at the bottom of each promotion item. It spans the full internal width of the item but uses only a portion of that space for the actual controls, leaving negative space to preserve the minimalist aesthetic. Primary actions (such as 'Apply Offer' or 'Book Now') are right‑aligned within the item’s width, with secondary actions (like 'Details' or 'Copy code') positioned just to the left or beneath, separated by 8–16px. Buttons are slim and refined, with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, and minimal or no borders, relying instead on subtle blush accents or fine outlines. There is about 16–24px of whitespace between the last line of the promotion description and the action panel, creating a clear visual break. The action panel does not introduce a new background or card; it simply organizes the interactive elements within the existing item boundaries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within each promotion item, anchored to its bottom edge in normal flow. It grows horizontally as needed with the item, but maintains its right alignment and spacing regardless of breakpoint. On narrow screens, actions may stack vertically within this area, keeping at least 8–12px vertical spacing between buttons while remaining visually tied to the related offer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promotions state indicator appears in the same spatial zone as the promotion list, acting as a graceful placeholder when there are no active promotions or when an error or empty state occurs. It is centered within the content column both horizontally and vertically in the available space below the filters. The indicator content—such as a brief message ('No promotions available right now') and an optional small icon—occupies only about 30–40% of the column width, leaving a halo of white space around it to maintain the serene, high‑end feel. The message text is set in the same refined type system as the rest of the page, with perhaps a slightly larger or blush‑accented key phrase to ensure visibility without feeling loud. If a secondary action is offered (e.g., 'View all services'), it is placed directly beneath the message with 16–24px spacing and styled similarly to other subtle call‑to‑actions on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the promotion list in terms of visibility: it occupies the list area when the list is empty or unavailable. It is positioned relative within the main content flow, immediately after the filter bar. On smaller screens, it continues to center within the available width, with text lines wrapping gracefully and maintaining comfortable line heights.

name: About
title: About
description: Page that loads and presents the salon’s background, waxing philosophy, hygiene standards, specializations, and technician profiles, with contextual navigation to booking and contact pages.

core_features: Load salon about information (headline, story, hygiene standards, specializations) via getSalonAboutInfo and render it in structured sections.
Load active technician profiles via getTechnicianProfiles and display them in a searchable/filterable list.
Filter technicians by waxing specialization based on the specializations provided by getSalonAboutInfo and the specialties of each Technician.
Highlight a specific technician or section (story, team) based on URL parameters for deep-linking from other pages.
Provide contextual navigation to waxing_services.html to start booking, optionally passing specialization context via URL parameters.
Provide contextual navigation to contact.html to get in touch, optionally passing an intent (e.g., question about a specialization or technician) via URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Read salon story and philosophy:
1. User navigates to about.html.
2. Page controller calls getSalonAboutInfo and waits for the response.
3. Salon story component renders the headline and story from the response.
4. If the URL includes a section parameter with the value corresponding to the story section, the page scrolls to the story area.
5. User reads about the salon’s background and waxing approach.

Understand hygiene standards and safety practices:
1. User scrolls to the hygiene section or selects a hygiene-related section link if available.
2. Page uses the hygiene_standards field from getSalonAboutInfo to populate this section.
3. User can click an internal anchor link to focus this section; the page updates the section parameter in the URL accordingly.
4. User reads hygiene details, then may click the booking CTA within or below this section to navigate to waxing_services.html with a from=about URL parameter.

Explore waxing specializations and find a suitable technician:
1. On page load, controller calls getSalonAboutInfo and getTechnicianProfiles.
2. Specialization filter component lists all specializations from getSalonAboutInfo.specializations.
3. If the URL contains a specialization parameter matching one of the listed specializations, that specialization is preselected and applied as a filter.
4. Technician list component receives all technicians from getTechnicianProfiles and applies the active specialization filter by comparing each technician’s specialties array.
5. User can change the selected specialization via the filter component; the list updates to show only matching technicians and the URL specialization parameter is updated to match the selection.
6. User clicks a technician to expand their profile and see details relevant to that specialization.

Deep-link to and highlight a specific technician profile:
1. Another page links to about.html with a technicianId URL parameter referencing Technician.id.
2. On load, the controller reads technicianId from the URL, then calls getTechnicianProfiles.
3. After technicians are loaded, the technician list component compares technicianId to the ids in the technician data.
4. If a match is found, that technician’s profile is automatically expanded, focused, or scrolled into view.
5. User can then read the highlighted technician’s bio and specialties and optionally click a booking CTA to proceed to waxing_services.html with an additional technicianId parameter for context, if desired by the implementation.

Navigate from About page to booking or contact flows with context:
1. User interacts with a booking-related CTA (e.g., near the story, hygiene, or team sections).
2. The CTA component constructs a URL to waxing_services.html, including optional URL parameters such as from=about and specialization (if a specialization is currently selected).
3. Browser navigates to waxing_services.html; that page can read the parameters to preconfigure its view (implementation on that page).
4. Alternatively, user chooses a contact-related CTA.
5. The CTA component constructs a URL to contact.html with from=about and optional parameters like technicianId or specialization for context.
6. Browser navigates to contact.html with those parameters.
interactions: On page load, the page controller reads URL parameters (section, specialization, technicianId) and initializes internal state before rendering content.
The controller calls getSalonAboutInfo with no parameters and stores the result for downstream components.
The controller calls getTechnicianProfiles with no parameters and stores the result for downstream components.
User can click internal navigation links (for example to the story, hygiene, or team sections); each click updates the section parameter in the URL and scrolls to the corresponding section.

User can click on a specialization option in the specialization filter; this sets the active specialization state, updates the specialization URL parameter, and triggers a re-render of the technician list to show only matching technicians.

User can clear the specialization filter (for example by choosing an ‘all’/no-selection state implemented without hardcoded labels), which removes the specialization parameter from the URL and shows all technicians.
User can click on a technician item in the technician list; this toggles expansion/collapse of the technician details, updates technicianId in the URL, and may scroll the technician into view.

User can click a booking CTA; this triggers construction of a URL pointing to waxing_services.html with optional query parameters from=about, specialization, and technicianId (if currently selected), followed by a standard browser navigation.

User can click a contact CTA; this triggers construction of a URL pointing to contact.html with optional query parameters from=about and context values such as specialization or technicianId, followed by a standard browser navigation.

If any data-loading error occurs for getSalonAboutInfo or getTechnicianProfiles, the controller updates error state and the page displays a generic error message sourced from state (without hardcoded content specifics) and may provide a link to contact.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page derives its behavior from URL parameters and asynchronous data from getSalonAboutInfo and getTechnicianProfiles. Supported URL parameters include: 1) section: Identifies which high-level section to focus or scroll to. Valid values are mapped internally to existing sections (e.g., story, hygiene, team) without hardcoding content; if section matches a known key, the corresponding section is scrolled into view on load or when the URL changes. 2) specialization: Represents the currently selected waxing specialization filter. On load, if specialization is present and its value matches one of the entries in getSalonAboutInfo.specializations, the specialization filter component marks it as active and passes this value to the technician list as a filter criterion. If specialization is missing or does not match any available specialization, no specialization filter is applied. When the user changes the specialization filter, the page updates this parameter (or removes it when clearing the filter) using the browser’s history API, without reloading the page. 3) technicianId: References a specific Technician.id to highlight or expand on the page. After getTechnicianProfiles resolves, if a technician with this id exists, that technician entry is programmatically expanded and focused. When a user manually selects a technician, the technicianId parameter is set to that technician’s id; when the user collapses the selection or selects a different technician, the technicianId parameter is updated or removed accordingly. 4) from: Can be used when other pages link into about.html to indicate the referrer context (for example, from=waxing_services). The About page reads this parameter but does not strictly depend on it; it may be stored in state for analytics or subtle behavioral tweaks (such as preferring to highlight certain sections) without hardcoding behavior tied to fixed sources. 5) Additional navigation parameters: When navigating away from about.html, the booking and contact CTAs construct URLs including from=about plus the current specialization and technicianId values, if set. This allows downstream pages (waxing_services.html and contact.html) to interpret the context and pre-fill or pre-focus their own views. All state transitions are driven solely by changes in these URL parameters and the results of the two assigned interfaces; no localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading from getSalonAboutInfo and getTechnicianProfiles, interprets URL parameters (section, specialization, technicianId, from), manages global page state (loading, error, activeSection, activeSpecialization, highlightedTechnicianId), and provides derived state and callbacks to child components.

Displays the salon headline and story text provided by getSalonAboutInfo, and can be focused when the active section state indicates the story section. It also exposes an anchor target for deep-linking via the section URL parameter.

Displays the salon’s hygiene standards and safety practices using hygiene_standards from getSalonAboutInfo. Can be scrolled into view when the active section state is set to this section and exposes an internal anchor for navigation.

Lists all waxing specializations from getSalonAboutInfo.specializations and allows the user to select or clear a specialization. The currently selected specialization is synchronized with the specialization URL parameter and used to filter the technician list.

Displays technician profiles loaded via getTechnicianProfiles, optionally ordered by display_order and filtered by the active specialization. Supports expanding a technician entry to show the full bio and specialties, and highlights a technician when highlightedTechnicianId is set.

Represents a single technician entry within the technician list. Shows basic information by default and reveals full bio and specialties on expansion. When selected, it notifies the controller to update technicianId in the URL and highlight the item.

Groups the specialization filter and technician list under a single section representing the team. It exposes an anchor target for the team section and integrates with the active section state for scrolling and focusing when section URL indicates the team.

Provides secondary calls-to-action to navigate to waxing_services.html (for booking) and contact.html (for inquiries). It reads current state (activeSpecialization, highlightedTechnicianId) and appends them as URL parameters along with from=about when constructing navigation links.

Optional inline navigation component that lets the user jump between major sections (story, hygiene, team) within the About page. It updates the section URL parameter and notifies the controller to scroll to the appropriate section.

Shows a non-specific error state when either getSalonAboutInfo or getTechnicianProfiles fails. Provides a generic message based on error state and may include a link to contact.html for assistance, constructed without hardcoded assumptions about content.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top‑to‑bottom composition with a global nav (handled outside these components), then a large vertical buffer, then a centered About page title and subtle divider line (within the page controller). Immediately beneath that, a slim inline section navigation sits in the same central column. From there, content sections (salon story, hygiene standards, team) stack vertically in the central column. Within the team section, the technician list uses a 3‑column grid pattern similar to the service list in the mockup, while the specialization filter appears just above it, aligned with the same grid. The secondary CTA bar sits near the bottom of the central column as a distinct horizontal band. The error state replaces the main content area when needed, still centered in the same column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top‑to‑bottom composition with a global nav (handled outside these components), then a large vertical buffer, then a centered About page title and subtle divider line (within the page controller). Immediately beneath that, a slim inline section navigation sits in the same central column. From there, content sections (salon story, hygiene standards, team) stack vertically in the central column. Within the team section, the technician list uses a 3‑column grid pattern similar to the service list in the mockup, while the specialization filter appears just above it, aligned with the same grid. The secondary CTA bar sits near the bottom of the central column as a distinct horizontal band. The error state replaces the main content area when needed, still centered in the same column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: All main content is constrained to a fixed‑width central column (approximately 70–75% of typical desktop width, e.g., 960–1120px on a 1440px viewport), with generous left and right margins. The top half of the initial viewport is dominated by whitespace and the About heading, mirroring the original WAXING page. Between major sections (story, hygiene, team, CTA) there are large vertical separations in the 64–96px range. layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low throughout: text sections are limited to moderate line lengths and broken into short paragraphs with generous line spacing and paragraph spacing. Each section occupies its own clear vertical zone. The technician profiles are presented as light, card‑like list items with ample white space around each card and between grid rows. Only a handful of visual elements appear in any one viewport, reinforcing a calm, luxury editorial feel.
