layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the global nav at the top to the large, centered 'ABOUT' title, which acts as the hero element. The subtle divider below the title signals the start of detailed content. Just beneath, the inline section nav provides quick anchors but remains visually quiet. From there, the gaze flows down into the salon story (first narrative block), continues to the hygiene section (reinforcing trust), then transitions into the more structured team area. In the team section, the eye moves from the section heading to the specialization filter, then scans left‑to‑right across each row of technician profiles, row by row, following a similar zig‑zag pattern to the original service menu. Finally, the gaze lands on the secondary CTA bar near the bottom, which gently directs users to booking or contact. If an error occurs, the central error display becomes the focal point while preserving the same central alignment and breathing room.

layout.overall_description: The About page adopts a refined, editorial layout: a slim, understated nav at the very top, then a large field of white space that leads into a big, centered 'ABOUT' heading in muted blush serif type. A fine horizontal line sits just beneath the title, visually anchoring the start of the content column. Immediately below, a delicate inline section navigation offers links like ‘Story’, ‘Hygiene’, and ‘Team’, centered and widely spaced. The core content lives inside a fixed‑width central column, leaving generous white gutters on both sides. First comes the salon story, a single column of text with comfortable line lengths and ample spacing that reads like a magazine feature. Below that, the hygiene standards section continues the narrative, perhaps with short subheadings, again spacious and calm. Further down, the team section opens with its own heading, followed by a minimal specialization filter arranged in a single line; beneath it, technician profiles appear in airy rows of three, each with a portrait, name, role, and a short blurb, floating within abundant white space reminiscent of the original three‑column service menu. A slim, softly highlighted CTA bar spans the width of the content column near the bottom, inviting visitors to book or get in touch without breaking the minimalist aesthetic. Throughout, the page feels light and premium, with every element centered, well‑spaced, and never crowded; should an error occur, a centered, elegantly styled error message occupies the same central column, preserving the overall sense of calm and order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-controller acts as the structural frame for the entire page. It positions all About content within a fixed-width central column that sits in the middle of the viewport, leaving wide, symmetric left and right margins (approximately 12–15% of viewport width on each side on desktop). Vertically, the controller reserves a large block of whitespace between the global site navigation and the About page title—roughly 96px–128px of clear space—so the top half of the first viewport is dominated by air and the hero title. The controller centers the main 'ABOUT' heading horizontally at the top of this content column, followed by a thin, full-width hairline divider (about 40–50% of the column width, centered beneath the title) with 16–24px of space above and below it. All subsequent sections (section-nav-inline, salon-story-section, hygiene-standards-section, team-section-wrapper, secondary-cta-bar, or about-error-state) are stacked vertically inside this same column, each separated by generous vertical margins (typically 64–96px between major blocks). On narrower screens, the controller ensures the central column scales down proportionally (e.g., 90% width with 5% margins) while preserving the same vertical rhythm and centered alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flows with the document; defines a centered, fixed-width column layout that all nested components respect, with responsive scaling but non-sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The salon-story-section is the first major content block beneath the inline section navigation. It begins approximately 64–80px below the section-nav-inline, occupying the full width of the central content column defined by the page controller. At the top of this section, a small, uppercase label such as 'OUR STORY' or a similarly understated heading is left-aligned relative to the column’s text baseline but still visually centered within the page due to the symmetric margins. Below the heading, the main story text is arranged in a single column with a comfortable readable width (roughly 60–70 characters per line), using generous line spacing and 16–24px between paragraphs. There are at most one or two subtle subheadings or pull quotes, each separated by 24–32px of vertical space to maintain light density. The section’s bottom edge leaves a clear 80–96px gap before the hygiene-standards-section begins, so the story feels like an independent editorial block. On smaller screens, the section simply narrows while maintaining the same left alignment and vertical spacings, ensuring the narrative remains airy and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flow block within the central column; no sticky or floating behavior, simply maintains large top and bottom margins to reinforce section separation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hygiene-standards-section sits directly beneath the salon story, separated from it by a generous vertical gap of about 80–96px to give the impression of a new editorial chapter. It spans the same central column width, maintaining the same left text alignment and margins as the story section. At its top, a clear section heading such as 'HYGIENE & SAFETY' is presented in a slightly more prominent serif or small caps style, left-aligned within the column. Below this heading, content may be structured as a short introductory paragraph followed by either a simple vertical list of key standards or 2–3 short sub-blocks stacked vertically. Each item or sub-block is separated by 24–32px spacing, preventing any sense of crowding. Bullets or icons, if present, are minimal and finely styled, aligned to the left edge of the text block. The overall density remains low: the section occupies roughly one to one and a half viewport heights on desktop, with plenty of white space around the text. A 80–96px gap is reserved below this section before the team-section-wrapper begins, maintaining a clear visual break between narrative content and team profiles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, non-sticky content block aligned in the main column, purely governed by vertical stacking and generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The specialization-filter is positioned at the top of the team-section-wrapper, sitting between the team heading and the technician-list. Horizontally, it aligns with the central column and spans about 70–80% of its width, centered or slightly left-biased depending on the typography style (e.g., left-aligned text with equal left/right padding). The filter itself is visually minimal: a thin horizontal row of pill-style filter chips or small text buttons representing specializations (e.g., 'All', 'Brows', 'Bikini', 'Sensitive Skin'), laid out left-to-right with 16–24px horizontal gaps between them. Vertical padding within each pill is subtle (6–10px), with 12–16px horizontal padding, matching the understated, editorial aesthetic. The entire filter row sits about 24–32px below the team section heading and about 32–40px above the first row of technician profile items, creating a clear visual hierarchy: heading → filters → profiles. On smaller screens, the filter chips wrap onto multiple lines while retaining ample vertical spacing between lines (~16px), preserving the light, calm feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline horizontal list within the team section, non-sticky; reflows and wraps as needed on smaller screens but always stays above the technician-list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The technician-list occupies the lower portion of the team-section-wrapper and visually mirrors the three-column service menu pattern from the mockup. It spans the full width of the central content column but internally organizes technician-profile-item components into informal rows of up to three columns per row on desktop. Each row’s three items are evenly distributed left to right, with sizeable horizontal gutters between them (approximately 48–64px), so the cards feel like islands of content floating in white space. Vertically, each row is separated by 40–56px from the next, continuing the airy rhythm of the mockup’s service rows. The list starts around 32–40px beneath the specialization-filter and may extend over multiple viewport heights depending on team size. On tablet, the grid gracefully collapses to two columns with similar gutter proportions; on mobile, it becomes a single column centered in the content area, with increased vertical spacing (48–64px) between items so the layout remains luxurious rather than crowded. The list does not introduce heavy borders or backgrounds, relying instead on spacing, subtle typographic hierarchy, and possibly very light hairline separators to define individual profiles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Grid-like list that flows within the central column; it reflows responsively from three to two to one column, maintaining generous gutters and vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each technician-profile-item is a vertically stacked, card-like unit that occupies roughly one-third of the central column’s width on desktop, with wide horizontal gutters separating it from neighboring items in its row. The profile card has no heavy border; instead, it uses subtle cues like a slightly increased line height, minimal top and bottom padding (16–24px), and careful alignment to feel distinct yet integrated with the white background. At the top of the item, a small, centered or left-aligned portrait (circular or softly rounded rectangle) sits with 16–24px of whitespace above it. Below the portrait, the technician’s name appears in a slightly larger serif or refined sans-serif, followed by their title/specialty in smaller, lighter text, with 8–12px spacing between these lines. A brief 2–3 line description or philosophy snippet sits beneath, with 12–16px of spacing above it, ensuring the text block doesn’t feel dense. Overall card height is moderate, with at least 24–32px of whitespace below the last text line before the next row begins. On tablet and mobile, the item stretches to fill more width (half or full width of the column), but its internal vertical spacing increases slightly to maintain the luxurious, airy appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Repeating list item within a responsive grid; its width adjusts with the number of columns (one-third, one-half, full), but it always keeps internal padding and vertical spacing generous.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team-section-wrapper encapsulates the entire team showcase and sits beneath the hygiene-standards-section, separated by 80–96px of vertical whitespace to mark a distinct shift from narrative to people-focused content. It spans the full width of the central content column. At its top, a section heading such as 'OUR TEAM' or 'TECHNICIANS' is centered or slightly left-aligned within the column, styled similarly to other section headings but with slightly higher visual weight to signal its importance. Below the heading, a short introductory line (optional) might be placed with 16–24px spacing before the specialization-filter. The specialization-filter then appears as a horizontal row of pills or text links, and beneath it, the technician-list grid fills the rest of the section. The wrapper ensures there is a clear visual envelope: 32–40px between heading and filter, 32–40px between filter and the first row of profiles, and at least 96px of whitespace below the last technician row before transitioning to the secondary-cta-bar. This creates a self-contained, airy block where the user’s gaze can explore multiple profiles without distraction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard stacked section in the main column; it governs the vertical spacing for its heading, filter, and technician list but does not have fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The secondary-cta-bar is positioned near the bottom of the About page, directly beneath the team-section-wrapper and separated from it by about 96px of whitespace, so it feels like a gentle, concluding prompt rather than an intrusive banner. It spans the full width of the central content column but is visually set apart from the pure white background by either a very subtle blush-tinted background panel or a fine top border line. The bar’s height is relatively slim, roughly 72–112px, and within it, content is horizontally centered: a short line of copy (e.g., 'Ready to book your waxing experience?') centered or left-aligned within the bar, with one or two minimalist buttons or text links (e.g., 'Book Now', 'Contact Us') aligned either just below or to the right with 16–24px spacing. The buttons follow the same minimalist styling as elsewhere—light outlines or low-contrast fills, with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. Generous padding (24–32px) sits above and below the inner content, so the bar reads as a comfortable, breathable strip, not a cramped banner. On small screens, the text and buttons stack vertically, remaining centered with consistent spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, sits at the bottom of the main content flow; it is not sticky, ensuring it feels like a natural conclusion rather than a persistent overlay.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-nav-inline is positioned directly beneath the main 'ABOUT' heading and its thin divider, inside the central content column. It appears approximately 24–32px below the divider, forming a slender horizontal strip of navigation that remains visually light. The nav items (e.g., 'Story', 'Hygiene', 'Team') are laid out in a single row and centered horizontally within the column, with generous horizontal spacing (32–48px) between labels to echo the airy feel of the main nav from the mockup. Text is set in small caps or a refined sans-serif, with understated styling and no heavy background; selection states may be indicated with a subtle underline or color shift in the blush accent. The entire inline nav is only about 24–40px tall, leaving large white spaces above (to the title) and below (to the salon story), so it feels like a whisper of structure rather than a dominant UI element. On narrow screens, the items remain on a single line if possible, otherwise they wrap to two centered lines with 8–12px vertical spacing between rows, keeping density low.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-sticky local navigation; clicking items scrolls smoothly to their sections, but the nav itself scrolls away with content and does not fix to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-error-state is designed to occupy the same central column where the About content would normally appear, preserving the overall layout’s elegance even when something goes wrong. When triggered, it replaces the standard sections (story, hygiene, team, CTA) within the about-page-controller, while keeping the hero 'ABOUT' heading and divider intact above it. Vertically, the error state block is centered within the remaining viewport space beneath the heading: there is at least 96px of whitespace above and below the error message, so it doesn’t feel cramped. The error content itself is centered horizontally in the column and limited to a moderate width (about 60% of the column) to maintain a graceful composition. It may include a small, simple icon at the top, followed by a short heading (e.g., 'Something went wrong') and a brief explanation, all stacked with 8–16px spacing between lines, plus a single, minimalist action button (e.g., 'Try Again' or 'Go Home') beneath with 16–24px spacing. No heavy borders or bright colors are introduced; the error state uses the same muted blush accent and fine typography to remain consistent with the luxury aesthetic. On small screens, the error block simply narrows and remains vertically centered within the scrollable area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional central block; when active, it replaces the main content sections inside the page controller while remaining in the standard document flow and centered within the main column.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Contact page where users can see salon contact details and opening hours, view the location on a map, and send general questions or special requests via a contact form.

core_features: Load and display salon contact information (address, phone, email, opening hours, and map reference) using the getContactInfo interface.
Render a contact form for general questions or special requests that submits data via the submitContactRequest interface.
Pre-fill the contact form subject field based on an optional subject URL parameter passed from other pages (e.g., policies.html, about.html).
Provide quick navigation links from the contact page to the waxing services and packages pages for users who decide to browse or book instead of sending a message.
Handle loading, success, and error states for both contact information retrieval and contact form submission without using localStorage.
user_workflows:
View contact details and opening hours:
  1) User navigates to contact.html (via header, footer, or direct URL).
  2) On page load, the page-init logic calls getContactInfo.
  3) While waiting, the contact-info panel shows a loading state.
  4) When getContactInfo returns, the contact-info panel displays the address, phone, email, and a structured list of opening hours.
  5) The map component receives the map_embed_reference and renders the location.
  6) The user can click the phone or email to initiate a call or open their email client.

Send a general question or special request:
  1) User opens contact.html, possibly with a subject URL parameter (e.g., contact.html?subject=...).
  2) The page-init logic parses the subject parameter and passes it as the initial value for the form’s subject field.
  3) The user fills in required form fields (e.g., name, email, message) and optional fields (e.g., phone, subject if they want to change it).
  4) The user submits the form; the contact-form component validates required fields.
  5) If validation passes, the contact-form component calls submitContactRequest with the current form values.
  6) While waiting for a response, the submit button is disabled and a submitting state is shown.
  7) On success, the form-status component displays the confirmation message from submitContactRequest, and the form may reset its fields (except subject, which can stay as last entered).
  8) On failure, the form-status component displays the error message from submitContactRequest, and the user can correct inputs and resubmit.

Use pre-filled subject from policies or about pages:
  1) User clicks a "Contact us" link on policies.html or about.html that navigates to contact.html with a subject URL parameter.
  2) On load, the page-init component reads subject from the URL query string.
  3) The contact-form component uses this value as the initial content of the subject input.
  4) The user can keep or modify this subject before submitting the form.
  5) On submission, the subject field value (possibly edited) is sent via submitContactRequest.

Navigate to waxing services or packages instead of sending a message:
  1) User visits contact.html (from any source).
  2) After viewing contact info or considering a question, the user notices quick links to view waxing services or packages.
  3) The user clicks "View Waxing Services"; the quick-links component routes to waxing_services.html without additional parameters.
  4) Alternatively, the user clicks "View Packages"; the quick-links component routes to packages.html.
  5) Navigation is handled via standard link clicks or programmatic navigation, without using localStorage.
interactions:
Page load and data retrieval:
  - Event: onLoad (page).
  - Action: Parse the subject URL parameter from contact.html?subject=... and store it in page state.
  - Action: Call getContactInfo with no parameters.
  - Result: When the promise resolves, update state with address, phone, email, map_embed_reference, and opening_hours; when rejected, mark contact info as failed to load.

Click-to-call and click-to-email from contact info:
  - Event: onClick (phone element).
  - Action: Trigger navigation to a tel: link constructed from the dynamic phone value returned by getContactInfo.
  - Event: onClick (email element).
  - Action: Trigger navigation to a mailto: link constructed from the dynamic email value returned by getContactInfo.

View map location:
  - Event: onLoad (map-display component once getContactInfo data is ready).
  - Action: Pass the dynamic map_embed_reference to whatever internal map rendering logic is used.
  - Result: The map is rendered or updated based on the reference.

Fill and update the contact form:
  - Event: onChange (name, email, phone, subject, message inputs).
  - Action: Update local form state for each field as the user types.
  - Special case: The subject field is initially set from the URL’s subject parameter but can be changed by the user; changes affect only in-memory form state and not the URL.

Submit the contact form:
  - Event: onSubmit (contact form).
  - Action: Prevent default browser submission; validate required fields (e.g., that required inputs are non-empty and that email has a basic valid format).
  - If validation fails: Do not call submitContactRequest; mark invalid fields and display inline validation messages.
  - If validation passes: Call submitContactRequest with { name, email, phone, subject, message } from current form state and set a submitting flag.
  - On success response: Clear submitting flag; display success message from the returned message property; optionally reset some or all form fields except where the design specifies otherwise.
  - On error response: Clear submitting flag; display error message from the returned message property; allow user to correct inputs and resubmit.

Handle submitContactRequest response:
  - Event: onChange (internal state when submitContactRequest resolves or rejects).
  - Action: The form-status component reads the success boolean and message from the latest submitContactRequest response in state and shows either a success or error status.
  - Event: onClick (dismiss/close button on status banner, if provided).
  - Action: Hide the status banner without altering the stored response data, so that a subsequent submit can display a new message.

Quick navigation to Waxing Services and Packages:
  - Event: onClick ("View Waxing Services" quick link).
  - Action: Navigate to waxing_services.html with no additional URL parameters.
  - Event: onClick ("View Packages" quick link).
  - Action: Navigate to packages.html with no additional URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven primarily by the subject URL parameter and the results of the getContactInfo and submitContactRequest interfaces. On initial load of contact.html, the page parses the query string for a subject parameter (e.g., contact.html?subject=Some+Question). If present, this value is stored in page state as initialSubject and passed to the contact-form component as the initial value of its subject field. If the parameter is absent, the subject field starts empty. Subsequent user edits to the subject field do not update the URL; they exist only in component state. For contact information, on page load the page-init logic calls getContactInfo with no arguments; while pending, a loading state is stored (e.g., contactInfoStatus = 'loading'). When getContactInfo resolves successfully, the response object is stored in state (e.g., contactInfoData) and contactInfoStatus switches to 'loaded', allowing bound components (contact-info panel and map-display) to render the dynamic address, phone, email, map_embed_reference, and opening_hours. If the call fails, contactInfoStatus becomes 'error' and the UI can display a retry option that triggers getContactInfo again without changing the URL. For the contact form, local form state holds the current values of name, email, phone, subject, and message. On submit, if validation passes, submitContactRequest is called with the current form state; a submissionStatus field (e.g., 'submitting', 'success', 'error') and submissionResponse object (containing success and message from the API) are updated based on the outcome. These submission states do not affect the URL and are cleared or overridden on subsequent submissions. Navigation from this page via quick links uses simple link navigation to waxing_services.html or packages.html without setting or reading any additional URL parameters.

components.functionality: Handles initial page setup: parses the subject URL parameter, triggers loading of salon contact information via getContactInfo, and initializes shared state (loading, success, error) for other components to consume.

Displays the salon’s contact details and opening hours using data returned by getContactInfo. Shows address, phone, email, and a generated list of opening hours. Supports click-to-call and click-to-email interactions and reflects loading or error states from the page-init logic.

Renders the salon location using the map_embed_reference returned from getContactInfo. Waits for contact info to be successfully loaded, then passes the map_embed_reference to the internal map rendering mechanism.

Provides a form for users to send general questions or special requests. Fields include name, email, phone, subject, and message. The subject field is pre-filled from the URL subject parameter if available. On submit, validates required inputs and calls submitContactRequest with the current field values.

Shows feedback to the user after attempting to submit the contact form. Displays the message and success state returned by submitContactRequest, differentiates between success and error, and can be dismissed by the user.
Provides quick navigation actions for users who prefer to browse services instead of sending a message, with links to waxing_services.html and packages.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow with a centered content column. At the very top sits the global navigation/header inside the page-init, spanning full width but visually light. A generous vertical gap leads into a large, centered 'CONTACT' page title with a thin divider line beneath, echoing the mockup. Directly under this hero area sits a compact, centered quick-links row for primary contact actions. Below that, the main content is arranged in a balanced two-column band: contact info on the left and a map on the right, both aligned to the same central grid. The lower portion of the page is dedicated to the contact form, with a status banner just above it, all still within the same fixed-width central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow with a centered content column. At the very top sits the global navigation/header inside the page-init, spanning full width but visually light. A generous vertical gap leads into a large, centered 'CONTACT' page title with a thin divider line beneath, echoing the mockup. Directly under this hero area sits a compact, centered quick-links row for primary contact actions. Below that, the main content is arranged in a balanced two-column band: contact info on the left and a map on the right, both aligned to the same central grid. The lower portion of the page is dedicated to the contact form, with a status banner just above it, all still within the same fixed-width central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies minimal vertical space but is separated from the hero title with a large margin (~64–96px). The hero title and divider inhabit the upper-middle of the viewport with generous breathing room above and below, similar to the original WAXING title treatment. The quick-links nav uses modest vertical height but is surrounded by comfortable whitespace (~32–48px above and below) so it feels light. The info+map section occupies the middle third of the page, with substantial side gutters from the viewport edges and a pronounced gutter between the two columns. The form section has a clear sectional break, with a larger top margin (~80–96px) and ample vertical spacing between form fields (~24–32px) and between the form and the page bottom, preserving the airy editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low. Items are not tightly packed; instead, each block (hero title, quick links, info panel, map, form) is allowed to sit in its own pool of whitespace. The contact info panel groups only a few key pieces of information per vertical segment and uses generous line-height and spacing between groups. The map is not crowded with labels around it, and the form uses tall fields and spacious spacing between inputs, labels, and the submit button. No dense tables or multi-line clusters; the layout prioritizes clarity and calm over maximum above-the-fold content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the top navigation straight to the large centered 'CONTACT' title. The thin horizontal divider just below the title acts as a visual cue that the main content begins beneath it. The user’s attention then drops to the quick-links row, which offers immediate actions like calling or getting directions. From there, the gaze naturally descends into the two-column band: first scanning the left column for address and hours, then cross-checking with the map on the right. After digesting this static information, the eye continues downward into the contact form status banner (if present) and then the form itself as the main interactive task. A persistent chat bubble in the bottom-right (from the broader site pattern) remains a peripheral call to action, secondary to the primary contact mechanisms presented in the central column.

layout.overall_description: The Contact page presents as a calm, editorial spread centered within a wide, white canvas. A slim, full-width navigation bar sits at the very top, aligned to a fixed-width central column. Beneath it, a large cushion of empty space leads into a prominently centered 'CONTACT' heading in refined serif type, with a delicate blush-colored divider line directly below. This hero zone occupies the upper half of the initial viewport, creating a luxurious sense of breathing room. Just under the divider, a minimalist quick-links row offers key actions laid out horizontally within the same central column, almost like a subtle menu band. Scrolling slightly—or on taller screens, just looking lower—reveals a balanced, two-column section: on the left, neatly spaced address, phone, email, and hours; on the right, a map framed with soft edges, both columns aligned to the same baseline and framed by generous side gutters. Further down, separated by another clear band of whitespace, the contact form appears as a centered, slightly narrower column, with a slim status banner above it if needed. Fields are spaced generously, and the submit button stands out through placement rather than heavy styling. The entire page feels light, quiet, and intentional, echoing a high-end boutique’s printed contact card translated into a modern, responsive web layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init defines the overall canvas and top structure. At the very top of the viewport, a full-width header/nav bar spans from left to right, but its interactive content (logo, navigation links) is constrained to a centered fixed-width column approximately 960–1120px wide. The nav bar is relatively shallow in height, with about 16–24px vertical padding, and sits against a white or near-white background so it feels light. Below the nav, there is a generous band of empty space—roughly 80–96px—before the page title appears. The 'CONTACT' heading is centered horizontally within the same content column and occupies roughly one line of large serif text. Immediately beneath the heading, a thin horizontal rule in the blush accent color stretches across a modest width (about 25–40% of the content column), centered under the text. Additional whitespace of around 48–64px appears below this divider, setting up a clear visual break before any subsequent components (beginning with the quick-links nav). The entire page-init block thus occupies the top third to top half of the initial viewport, depending on screen height, with all elements vertically stacked and centered within the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the normal document flow; the header/nav is full-width but not sticky, and all elements scroll away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-links nav sits immediately below the hero divider area, centered within the same fixed-width content column. There is a vertical gap of about 32–40px between the bottom of the page-init divider and the top of this nav. The component takes the form of a single horizontal row of 2–4 succinct links (e.g., Call, Directions, Email), each styled as minimalist text buttons or pill-like labels. The entire quick-links band spans roughly 60–70% of the content column width and is horizontally centered, leaving equal white margins on the left and right. Within the band, links are evenly spaced, echoing the even distribution of service-price pairs in the original mockup, with generous gutters (24–32px) between each link so they never feel crowded. Vertically, the nav has modest height—around 40–56px total—with comfortable padding above and below the link text. A bit of extra whitespace (about 32–48px) separates this quick-links row from the start of the main content section (the info/map band), ensuring it reads as a light, transitional strip rather than a dense toolbar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow, scrolling with the page. No sticky or floating behavior; it acts as a gentle wayfinding aid near the top of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-panel forms the left half of a two-column middle section, aligned under the quick-links nav. A larger vertical margin (~64–80px) separates the quick-links from the top of this two-column band, giving the impression of a new section. The info panel is aligned to the left edge of the main content column and spans roughly 46–48% of that column’s width, leaving a wide central gutter between it and the map on the right. The top of the panel aligns horizontally with the top edge of the map component, creating a clean shared baseline. Inside the panel, the content is arranged vertically with generous spacing: a small section heading (e.g., 'Salon Details') at the top, followed by address, phone, email, and opening hours grouped into clear blocks. Each block uses comfortable line-spacing and 16–24px gaps between text groups; the overall panel height is similar to or slightly less than the map’s height so they visually balance. The surrounding whitespace on the left (outer gutter from the viewport edge) and below (before the next section) maintains the airy, editorial feel. On wider screens, the left column naturally guides the user’s eye first, before it moves across to the map.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central content column, forming the left column of a responsive two-column layout. On narrow screens, it stacks above the map, expanding to nearly full width with its same generous internal spacing preserved.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-map-display is positioned to the right of the contact-info-panel within the same horizontal band, sharing a common top baseline. It occupies roughly 46–48% of the central content column’s width, mirroring the info panel and leaving a wide central gutter between them (~48–64px). The map itself is presented as a rectangular frame with a width equal to its column and a height in the range of 320–400px, creating a stable, postcard-like window onto the location. A subtle border or soft corner radius can be applied, but no heavy chrome or shadows, keeping with the minimalist aesthetic. Above the map, there may be a small, understated label like 'Find Us' aligned to the left edge of the map frame and spaced about 8–16px above it. The map’s right side sits well within the overall page margins, maintaining substantial white space between the map and the browser edge. Beneath the map, a moderate margin (~48–64px) creates a buffer before the beginning of the form/status section, so the map never feels crowded by lower content. The overall effect is of a calm, balanced right-hand visual anchor complementing the text-rich left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, participating in the two-column layout. On narrow screens, it stacks directly below the contact-info-panel, occupying nearly the full width of the main content column while maintaining its aspect ratio and generous margin above and below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form-status-banner appears below the two-column info+map band and directly above the contact form, but only when there is a message to show (e.g., success, error). There is a clear vertical separation of about 72–96px between the bottom of the info/map section and the top of the banner, marking the start of a new lower section focused on interaction. The banner itself is a slim horizontal strip centered within the content column, matching the width of the contact form it precedes—slightly narrower than the full content column, approximately 70–80% of it, so it feels intimately connected to the form. Its height is modest, 40–56px, with vertical padding around the message text and ample side padding within the banner. The background is a very soft, tinted color (e.g., pale green for success or pale blush/red for error) with fine typography for the message, aligning left within the banner. A small amount of vertical spacing (16–24px) separates the bottom edge of the banner from the top of the form, maintaining the relaxed rhythm of the page while clearly tying the message to the form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears within the normal flow above the form based on form state. It does not overlap content or stick to the viewport; it simply shifts the form downward when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form occupies the lower third of the page, centered beneath the info/map band and the optional status banner. It is slightly narrower than the main content column to emphasize focus and reduce horizontal eye travel—approximately 70–80% of the column width—and is centered so equal margin remains on both sides. A generous margin (~16–24px) separates it from the status banner above (or from the info/map section if no banner is present), giving it a distinct sense of being the primary interactive area. Within the form, fields are arranged in a single column for clarity, with labels aligned left and placed above their corresponding inputs. Each field group (label + input) is separated by 24–32px of vertical space. Inputs are given comfortable vertical padding (10–12px) and stretch to fill the form’s width, while textareas for messages are taller but still framed with ample white space. The submit button sits either aligned to the right edge of the input column or centered beneath the last field, with a clear top margin (24–32px) so it does not crowd the inputs. The bottom of the form has a generous margin (~80–96px) before the page footer or end, allowing the page to feel airy even when scrolled to the bottom. Overall, the form reads as a calm, carefully spaced block of fields within a clean editorial layout, inviting considered input rather than rushed completion.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and centered within the main column, stacking naturally within the document flow. On smaller screens, it expands to nearly full width while preserving its generous vertical spacing and single-column organization.

name: Policies
title: Policies
description: The Policies page retrieves and displays dynamic policy content (e.g., booking, cancellations, refunds, privacy, terms) using the getPoliciesContent interface, supports deep-linking to specific sections via the policyType URL parameter, and allows users to navigate between sections and contact the salon with policy-related questions.

core_features: Load all relevant policy sections from the getPoliciesContent interface, optionally filtered or influenced by the policyType URL parameter.
Render each policy section with its title and content, using the data returned by getPoliciesContent without hardcoding any policy text.
Support deep-linking via the policyType URL parameter so other pages (e.g., checkout, cart, contact) can open this page scrolled to and highlighting a specific policy section.
Provide an in-page navigation element built dynamically from the loaded policy sections, allowing users to jump directly to a chosen section and updating the policyType URL parameter accordingly.
Offer a contextual "Contact us about this policy" action within each policy section that navigates to contact.html with the subject URL parameter set to "Policy question".
Handle loading and error states for the getPoliciesContent call so users understand when content is being fetched or if something went wrong.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Viewing all policies
1. User opens policies.html without any URL parameters.
2. The page controller reads the URL, detects that no policyType parameter is present, and calls getPoliciesContent with an undefined or empty policyType parameter (according to the interface contract).
3. getPoliciesContent returns an array of policy objects, each with policy_type, title, and content fields.
4. The page renders an in-page navigation list based on the returned policy objects (using their title and/or policy_type fields) and displays each policy section in the main content area.
5. The user scrolls through or clicks navigation items to move between policy sections as needed.

Workflow: Deep-linking to a specific policy section from another page
1. User clicks a link on checkout.html, cart.html, or contact.html that navigates to policies.html with a policyType URL parameter (for example, policies.html?policyType=<someValue>).
2. The Policies page controller reads the policyType parameter from the URL and passes it into getPoliciesContent.
3. When the policy array is returned, the controller searches for a policy whose policy_type matches the policyType URL parameter.
4. If a matching policy section is found, the page scrolls to that section, marks it as the active/selected section in the in-page navigation, and ensures its state (e.g., highlighted) reflects that it is the targeted policy.
5. If no matching policy is found, the page still displays all policies but does not highlight any specific section, leaving the user free to navigate manually.

Workflow: Navigating between policy sections on the page
1. After policies are loaded and rendered, the user sees an in-page navigation (e.g., a list of policy titles) generated from the returned policy objects.
2. The user clicks an item in this navigation (for example, a particular policy title).
3. The click handler determines the associated policy_type for that navigation item and scrolls the main content area to the corresponding policy section.
4. The page updates the URL policyType parameter using history APIs (without reloading the page) to reflect the selected policy_type.
5. The navigation UI updates its active state to indicate which policy section is currently selected.

Workflow: Asking a question about a specific policy
1. The user scrolls to or navigates to a specific policy section they want to ask about.
2. Within that policy section, the user clicks a "Contact us about this policy" link or button.
3. The click handler constructs a URL for contact.html that includes the subject URL parameter set to "Policy question" (e.g., contact.html?subject=Policy%20question). Additional parameters related to the policy may be appended if supported by the overall system, but are not required by this page's specification.
4. The browser navigates to the contact.html page with the given subject parameter.
5. The Contact page can then pre-fill or contextualize the contact form based on the subject parameter.

Workflow: Updating the highlighted policy section when the URL changes
1. The user may navigate using the browser back/forward buttons, which can change the policyType URL parameter for policies.html in the history stack.
2. The page listens for URL changes via the browser's navigation events (e.g., popstate).
3. When such a change is detected, the controller reads the updated policyType parameter.
4. If policies are already loaded, the controller scrolls to the matching policy section and updates the active state in the in-page navigation; if policies are not yet loaded (e.g., on initial load), the controller ensures that the desired policyType is used when processing the API response.
5. The page state remains synchronized with the URL so that the visible and active policy section always matches the current policyType parameter.
interactions: On page load, the page reads the policyType URL parameter (if present) and triggers a call to getPoliciesContent with that parameter.

When getPoliciesContent succeeds, the page populates the policy list and in-page navigation, then optionally scrolls to a specific policy section if the policyType URL parameter matches one of the returned policy_type values.
If getPoliciesContent fails (e.g., network error), the page displays an error state and a retry action that re-calls getPoliciesContent with the same policyType parameter.
Clicking an in-page navigation item scrolls to the associated policy section, visually marks it as active, and updates the policyType URL parameter (without a full page reload).

Clicking the "Contact us about this policy" control inside any policy section sends the user to contact.html with the subject parameter set to "Policy question" via a standard link or programmatic navigation.

Using the browser back or forward buttons can change the policyType URL parameter; the page responds by updating the active policy section and scrolling appropriately, without refetching policies unless necessary.

If the user manually edits the URL and changes the policyType parameter, then reloads the page, the initial load sequence uses this new value to determine which policy section to highlight after calling getPoliciesContent.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the policyType URL parameter and the data returned by getPoliciesContent. On initial load, the page parses the current URL search parameters to read policyType, if present. This value is passed directly to getPoliciesContent as the policyType argument. The interface returns an array of policy objects, each containing policy_type, title, and content. The page stores this array in its in-memory state (without using localStorage or any persistent client-side storage) and renders all policy sections. After rendering, if a policyType parameter was provided in the URL, the controller searches the policy array for an item whose policy_type equals the parameter value. If a match is found, the page scrolls to that section and marks its corresponding navigation item as active. If no match is found or no policyType is provided, all policies are still rendered but none is specially highlighted. When a user selects a policy section via in-page navigation, the page sets an internal activePolicyType state to the selected section's policy_type and uses the History API (e.g., pushState or replaceState) to update the policyType URL parameter so that the URL always reflects the current selection. This update does not trigger a page reload; instead, the existing policy data is reused and only scroll/active state is updated. When the user navigates via browser history (back/forward), the popstate event is used to detect changes to the policyType parameter. The controller reads the updated parameter, updates the internal activePolicyType state, and scrolls to the corresponding section if it exists in the currently loaded policies. The page does not rely on localStorage or other persistent client-side mechanisms; all state is derived from the current URL parameters and the latest response from getPoliciesContent.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading for the Policies page, synchronizes URL parameters with in-page state, and passes the loaded policy data to child components for rendering and navigation.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered when policies.html is loaded. Reads the policyType parameter from the URL, calls getPoliciesContent with that policyType value (which may be undefined), and transitions the page into a loading state until data is returned.

onPoliciesLoaded: Triggered when getPoliciesContent resolves successfully. Stores the returned array of policies, clears any error state, and notifies child components (e.g., policy-inline-nav and policies-section-list) to render the data. If a policyType parameter is present in the URL, finds the matching policy_type in the array and instructs the view to scroll to that section and mark it as active.
onPoliciesLoadError: Triggered when getPoliciesContent fails. Stores error information in memory and exposes a retry action, while ensuring no stale policy data is displayed.

onPolicyTypeChangeFromNav: Called when a child component (e.g., policy-inline-nav) reports that a user selected a different policy section. Updates internal activePolicyType state, updates the policyType URL parameter using the History API, and scrolls to the corresponding policy section if it exists.

onPopState: Listens to browser navigation changes. When the URL changes due to back/forward navigation, re-reads the policyType parameter, updates activePolicyType, and scrolls to the matching policy section if the data has already been loaded.
onRetryLoad: Called when the user chooses to retry loading after an error. Reinvokes getPoliciesContent with the current URL policyType parameter.

components.functionality: Provides a navigation list of available policies based on the data returned by getPoliciesContent, allows users to quickly jump to specific policy sections, and keeps the active item synced with the current policyType state.
data_binding: Array<{ policy_type, title }> derived from getPoliciesContent(policyType)
activePolicyType (derived from URLSearchParams.policyType or controller state)
event_handlers: onNavItemClick(policy_type): Triggered when a user clicks a navigation item. Notifies the policies-page-controller of the selected policy_type so that it can update the URL policyType parameter, scroll to the relevant section, and update activePolicyType.

onPoliciesDataUpdate: Called when the underlying policy list changes (e.g., after a fresh getPoliciesContent call). Rebuilds the navigation items to match the current set of policies and updates which item is marked as active.

onActivePolicyTypeChange: Invoked when the controller updates activePolicyType (for example, due to URL changes or initial deep-linking). Updates the navigation UI to reflect which item is currently active.

components.functionality: Renders the list of policy sections returned by getPoliciesContent, ensuring each section can be scrolled to and identified via its policy_type, and embeds the contact action for each policy.
data_binding: Array<{ policy_type, title, content }> from getPoliciesContent(policyType)
activePolicyType (to optionally apply active styling to the targeted section)
event_handlers: onPoliciesDataUpdate: Triggered when a new policy array is provided by the controller. Renders or updates all policy-section components accordingly.

onScrollToPolicy(policy_type): Called by the controller when a specific policy section needs to be brought into view (for example, after initial load when a policyType parameter matches one of the sections). The component locates the section whose policy_type matches and scrolls to it.
onActivePolicyTypeChange: Invoked when activePolicyType changes, allowing the component to apply or remove active styling or focus from the appropriate section.

components.functionality: Represents a single policy block on the page, using a unique identifier based on policy_type, displaying the dynamic title and content returned by getPoliciesContent, and providing a contextual contact action.
data_binding: policy.policy_type from a single item in the getPoliciesContent response
policy.title from a single item in the getPoliciesContent response
policy.content from a single item in the getPoliciesContent response
activePolicyType (to determine whether this section should be visually highlighted)
event_handlers: onContactClick: Triggered when the user selects the "Contact us about this policy" action within this section. Navigates the browser to contact.html with the subject URL parameter set to "Policy question" (e.g., contact.html?subject=Policy%20question). Any additional query parameters may be appended by the broader system but are not required by this component.
onSectionMounted: Called when the section is attached to the DOM. Registers its element reference with the parent (policies-section-list) so that scrolling to this section by policy_type is possible.

onActiveStateUpdate: Invoked when the parent indicates that activePolicyType has changed. If the section's policy_type matches activePolicyType, the component updates its state to reflect active/highlighted status.

components.functionality: Displays loading or error feedback while the policies are being fetched via getPoliciesContent, and offers a retry mechanism in case of failure.
data_binding: errorDetails (if any) from the failed getPoliciesContent call
event_handlers: onShowLoading: Called by the controller when getPoliciesContent is in progress. Shows a loading indicator and hides policy content until data arrives.
onShowError: Called by the controller when getPoliciesContent fails. Displays an error message and a retry control.
onRetryClick: Triggered when the user clicks the retry control. Notifies policies-page-controller to invoke onRetryLoad, which re-calls getPoliciesContent with the current URL policyType.

layout.grid_system: Implicit fixed-width central column, likely underpinned by a 12‑column grid with three equal content columns for the service list. Wide outer margins on both sides create a centered layout.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, centered column layout. The global nav (outside this spec) sits at the very top, followed by a tall vertical buffer of whitespace. Centered in the viewport below that is the page hero for Policies (title + divider). Immediately beneath the hero, a horizontal inline navigation bar for policy categories runs across the central column, echoing the 3-column service-row pattern by distributing up to three policy links per row with wide gutters. Below this, the main policy content is presented as a vertical stack of full-width (within the central column) policy sections. The data controller is invisible and wraps this entire content area, and the loading state conditionally replaces the inline nav + section list area when needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, centered column layout. The global nav (outside this spec) sits at the very top, followed by a tall vertical buffer of whitespace. Centered in the viewport below that is the page hero for Policies (title + divider). Immediately beneath the hero, a horizontal inline navigation bar for policy categories runs across the central column, echoing the 3-column service-row pattern by distributing up to three policy links per row with wide gutters. Below this, the main policy content is presented as a vertical stack of full-width (within the central column) policy sections. The data controller is invisible and wraps this entire content area, and the loading state conditionally replaces the inline nav + section list area when needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper half of the viewport is dominated by whitespace and the centered Policies hero, mirroring the airy feel of the waxing title area in the mockup. The inline navigation occupies a shallow band directly underneath the hero with generous top and bottom padding, while the policy sections fill the lower half, but with generous vertical gaps between each section. Horizontally, all interactive and content components sit within a fixed-width central column (roughly 60–70% of the viewport width up to a max width), with large left and right page margins preserving the calm, boutique aesthetic.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low: the hero area shows only the title and a thin divider surrounded by ample empty space; the inline nav displays a small number of links per row with wide spacing; and each policy section is allowed to breathe, with distinct titles, comfortable line lengths, and large vertical separations. No dense tables or tight lists appear; even when there are many policies, they are presented as a relaxed, scrollable sequence of well-separated sections.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s gaze moves from the top nav down into the center of the page where the Policies title in the blush accent color anchors attention. The thin divider directly beneath subtly signals a transition into actionable content. The eye then moves down to the policy inline navigation, scanning left-to-right across up to three category links in a row, similar to the mockup’s service rows. From whichever link is selected, the user’s attention is drawn vertically downward into the corresponding policy section in the list. As the user scrolls, the sticky behavior of the inline nav (on larger screens) keeps navigation peripherally available while the main content remains the primary focus.

layout.overall_description: The Policies page is composed as a calm, editorial column floating in a field of white. At the very top, the site’s main navigation forms a thin strip; beneath it, a tall expanse of negative space draws the eye downward to a single centered heading: “Policies” in a refined serif, slightly oversized and set in the muted blush accent. A hairline divider sits just below, spanning only the central text width. Below this hero, a horizontal, center-aligned inline navigation lists the core policy categories, arranged in up to three evenly spaced items per row with wide gutters that echo the three-column service grid from the waxing menu. This nav sits in a shallow band framed by ample breathing room above and below. Further down, the main body of the page is a vertical stack of policy sections, all constrained to a relatively narrow, fixed-width central column. Each section begins with a left-aligned heading and is followed by paragraphs of copy, with generous spacing between sections to maintain a luxurious, low-density feel. The entire content column remains centered with substantial left and right margins, so on larger screens it feels like reading an elegant print booklet. When content is loading, a minimal loading indicator appears in the center of this column, replacing the nav and sections while preserving the overall airy composition.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies-page-controller is a non-visual wrapper that occupies the full width and full height of the page content area beneath the global navigation bar. Conceptually, it defines the main central column as its primary content region: this column is centered horizontally in the viewport, with wide left and right margins so that it spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width up to a comfortable max width (for example, 960px). Within this controller, vertical flow is strictly linear: first the Policies hero (title and divider, implemented elsewhere), then the policy-inline-nav, followed by the policies-section-list (which contains multiple policy-section components). The controller manages which of these child areas is visible at any time: when data is loading, the policies-loading-state is displayed in the middle of the central column and the inline nav + section list are hidden; once data loads, the loading state is removed and the inline nav and section list appear in their defined positions. This component does not render any visible UI but ensures that all other components snap to the same centered grid and spacing rules.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a full-page, flow-layout container for its children, maintaining the centered column and vertical stacking order; no fixed or sticky behavior of its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-inline-nav sits in the central column directly beneath the Policies hero area. There is a generous vertical gap of roughly 48–64px from the hero’s bottom edge (divider) to the top of this nav, preserving the airy separation seen between title and content in the mockup. The nav itself spans the full width of the central column (about 60–70% of the viewport, constrained to the same max-width as the text), and is horizontally centered. Within this band, individual policy links (e.g., Booking, Cancellations, Refunds, Privacy, Terms) are presented as text-based, low-chrome navigation items set in uppercase or small caps, with fine typographic styling that echoes the main nav. On large screens, links are arranged in rows of up to three items: each row uses an implicit three-column grid where the links are left, center, and right aligned within that row, with substantial horizontal gutters (~48–64px) between them, visually recalling the three service-price pairs of the waxing menu. If there are more than three items, they wrap onto a second row using the same alignment. Vertical padding above and below the nav links is in the 24–32px range, giving them a soft presence rather than a heavy bar. The nav is center-aligned as a whole, but individual link labels are left-aligned within their own touch areas for a subtle editorial feel. As the user scrolls down through the policies-section-list, the nav remains visually connected just above the content; on larger screens it becomes sticky: it pins to the top of the viewport (just under the global nav) once scrolled out of its original position, maintaining a constant slim presence while leaving plenty of white space around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls in normal document flow until it reaches the top of the viewport, then becomes sticky under the global nav on larger screens; within its area, it centers itself horizontally and maintains a multi-column row layout with up to three items per row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies-section-list occupies the central column directly below the policy-inline-nav. There is a comfortable vertical gap of approximately 40–56px between the bottom of the inline nav and the top of the first policy section, echoing the space between menu rows in the mockup. The list spans the same fixed max-width as the inline nav and hero, centered in the viewport. Inside this container, policy sections are stacked vertically in a single column to prioritize readability; each child policy-section takes up the full width of the central column but maintains generous internal margins so the text does not feel edge-to-edge. The vertical spacing between consecutive policy sections is substantial—roughly 64–96px—creating distinct, well-separated content blocks that read like individual pages in a booklet. There is additional padding at the bottom of the list (48–64px) before the page ends or any footer begins, mirroring the tall whitespace above the hero for a balanced composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block-level container within the centered column, expanding vertically as more sections are added; participates in normal scrolling beneath the (potentially sticky) inline nav.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each policy-section appears as a refined, editorial content block within the policies-section-list. It is left-aligned within the central column but centered within the page as a whole. The section starts with a section title (e.g., “Booking Policy”) positioned at the top-left of the block, sitting on the baseline of the central column with a modest top padding (~8–16px) from the section’s anchor area. The title uses a slightly larger serif style or a distinct type treatment that gives it hierarchy without overpowering the main Policies title. Directly below the title, there may be a thin horizontal rule or a subtle increase in whitespace (16–24px) before the body text begins, echoing the divider under the main page title but in a less dominant way. The body copy is presented in short paragraphs, with a comfortable line length (no more than 70–75 characters) and line spacing that aligns with the overall airy aesthetic. If a section contains sub-points (e.g., bullet items or numbered conditions), they are spaced with at least 8–12px between each item to avoid visual crowding. The bottom of each policy-section has a generous margin of 64–96px before the next section begins, clearly separating topics. Each section has an invisible anchor offset slightly above the visual title so that when the user deep-links or taps an item in the inline nav, the scrolled-into-view title doesn’t sit flush against the sticky nav; instead, it appears with a comfortable 24–32px buffer below the nav.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the policies-section-list; each section serves as an anchor target for deep-linking, with additional top offset padding to accommodate the sticky inline nav without visual crowding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies-loading-state appears in place of the inline nav and section list whenever policy content is being fetched. It is centered within the same central column that the other components use: horizontally, it is aligned to the center of the page; vertically, it is positioned so that it feels roughly centered between the bottom of the global nav and the mid-lower portion of the viewport (for example, using a top margin of 25–35% of viewport height), creating a calm, minimal empty state. The loading indicator itself might consist of a small, elegant spinner or animated dot pattern paired with a short, softly styled label such as “Loading policies…”. Both the indicator and text sit inside a very light-weight block with ample padding (~24–32px) and lots of surrounding whitespace. No hard borders or heavy backgrounds are used; the main impression remains mostly white space with a tiny, refined focal point in the center. When loading completes, this entire block disappears cleanly and the inline nav appears in its normal position beneath the hero, followed by the policy sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the policy-inline-nav and policies-section-list within the central column while active; participates in normal scrolling if the page height exceeds the viewport, but is typically positioned near the vertical center of the available space.
